blurb: Superhuman acquired GPTZero, the AI-detection startup with more than 19M registered users and $30M in annual recurring revenue. PitchBook values GPTZero at over $88M, and the deal pushes Superhuman beyond email into content authentication as AI-generated text proliferates.

title: Oracle shed about 20k roles globally in the last year
blurb: Oracle cut about 21,000 jobs over the past year, roughly 13% of its workforce, ending May 2026 with 141,000 employees as it restructures around AI. The reductions cost $1.8B in severance and restructuring, up from $374M a year earlier, and come as Oracle plans to spend at least $50B building data centers for customers like OpenAI and Meta.

title: Adobe Just Made its Biggest AI Push Yet, and it Stretches from Photoshop to Disney World
blurb: Adobe rolled out its biggest AI push yet this week, putting the Firefly AI Assistant into public beta inside Photoshop, Premiere, Illustrator, InDesign, and Frame.io, where each app gets a specialist chatbot that sorts footage, swaps backgrounds, and batch-generates versioned files on command. The same week, Adobe paired with Walt Disney Imagineering to build custom Firefly Foundry models trained on Disney's own design catalogue rather than scraped web data.

title: Atlassian's DESIGN.md is Here: What We Learned Testing Portable Design Context in Practice
blurb: Atlassian tested Google's open-source DESIGN.md format and found it portable but less sophisticated than its own design-context tooling.

title: World model maker Odyssey nabs $1.45B valuation backed by Amazon and other big names
blurb: Odyssey raised a $310M Series B at a $1.45B valuation, led by Natural Capital with Amazon, AMD Ventures, and GV participating. The startup, founded by self-driving veterans Oliver Cameron and Jeff Hawke, builds world models that simulate physical environments with accurate physics, and as part of the deal will make AWS its preferred cloud and optimize its models for Amazon's Trainium chips.

title: SpaceX Sells $25 Billion of Bonds, Cuts Interest Costs
blurb: SpaceX sold $25B of investment-grade bonds on Tuesday, the final step in refinancing the costly debt that funded Elon Musk's acquisition of Twitter, now X, and the loans and bonds xAI issued to cover its rapid cash burn. The investment-grade rating lets SpaceX cut interest costs across the consolidated Musk empire, replacing the high-rate financing taken on during the 2022 deal and xAI's buildout.

title: LastPass notifies users of yet another data breach
blurb: LastPass is notifying users of a breach at market-research partner Klue that exposed CRM contact data, though encrypted password vaults were unaffected.

title: Alibaba's AI video model rises to No. 2 in global rankings, as OpenAI's Sora and ByteDance's Seedance fall away
blurb: Alibaba Cloud released HappyHorse 1.1, an API-first AI video generation model now ranked No. 2 on the Artificial Analysis Video Arena, as OpenAI discontinued Sora over costs and ByteDance shelved Seedance 2.0 amid Hollywood copyright complaints. The model is live on Alibaba Cloud Model Studio with full API access and a 40% launch discount, backed by a $52.7B global infrastructure buildout aimed at enterprise adoption.

title: Google DeepMind and A24 announce first-of-its-kind research partnership
blurb: Google DeepMind and A24 announced a research partnership pairing the lab's AI work with the film studio's filmmakers to develop new creative tools and workflows. The multi-project collaboration embeds DeepMind's models in A24's production process for real-world artist feedback, and Google has also taken an investment stake in the studio.

title: iOS 27 beta 2 includes Write with Siri, the new version of Apple Intelligence writing tools
blurb: Apple's iOS 27 beta 2 introduces Write with Siri, replacing the standalone Writing Tools from the iOS 18 Apple Intelligence rollout with a natural-language system that generates, proofreads, and rewrites text from the keyboard. It ties writing tasks into Siri's personal context and on-screen awareness, and also ships on iPadOS 27 and macOS Golden Gate ahead of a public beta in July and a fall release.

title: Nvidia announces liquid cooling system that runs ‘hotter than a hot tub’ — promises to reduce electricity consumption and cut water use by up to 100%, but sustainability challenges remain
blurb: Nvidia announced a liquid cooling system for its Rubin chips that circulates coolant at 113°F, hotter than a typical hot tub, so data centers can dump heat using outdoor dry coolers instead of power-hungry chillers. The closed loop is filled once for the facility's life, cutting water use by up to 100% and trimming electricity, though sites in hot climates may still need chillers a few times a year.

title: Starlink to power security from space with WEI
blurb: Starlink partnered with WEI to deliver SD-WAN and SASE connectivity via satellite, validated against Cisco, Palo Alto Networks, and HPE platforms.

title: Sakana Fugu Launches With 93.2 LiveCodeBench Score After Claude Ban
blurb: Sakana released Fugu, a multi-model orchestration API scoring 93.2 on LiveCodeBench, after a US directive forced Anthropic to suspend Claude access.

title: Building a ‘digital twin’ 10,000 feet underground: PNNL, Nvidia and Fervo team up on geothermal AI
blurb: Pacific Northwest National Laboratory is partnering with Nvidia and Fervo Energy to build a publicly available digital twin that models geothermal reservoirs up to 10,000 feet underground to optimize power generation. PNNL will train the AI models on Nvidia infrastructure using proprietary data from Fervo's Nevada and Utah sites, replacing today's slow simulations that delay real-time decisions on well design, water injection, and reservoir management.

title: SpaceX lands $6.3B compute deal with open-source AI startup Reflection and Nvidia is on both sides of the trade
blurb: Reflection AI, the open-source startup founded by two former Google DeepMind researchers, signed a deal to pay SpaceX $150M a month for access to Nvidia GB300 chips at the Colossus 2 data center in Memphis, totaling roughly $6.3B if it runs through 2029. The contract pushes SpaceX's committed compute revenue from outside clients past $80B, joining Anthropic, Google, and Cursor on a customer list that turns Colossus into a major commercial AI infrastructure asset.

title: Build an AI Scientist for Life Science Discovery with NVIDIA BioNeMo Agent Toolkit
blurb: NVIDIA released BioNeMo Agent Toolkit, a platform that packages its accelerated digital-biology models into documented, callable tools that AI agents can invoke for tasks like structure prediction, molecular docking, sequence design, and genomics. Each capability ships as a BioNeMo Skill or MCP server wrapper specifying inputs, parameters, and failure modes, letting an agent pick the right model, format a valid request, and interpret results.

title: In memory of the man who put red and green squiggles under words
blurb: Tony Krueger, a longtime Microsoft engineer who shipped more versions of Word than perhaps anyone, made spell-check unobtrusive and introduced the red squiggles that mark misspelled words as you type, later joined by green squiggles for grammar. This Old New Thing remembrance traces how a background process that replaced Word's old blocking spell-check pass became a near-universal interface convention, now echoed across nearly every text editor.

title: The Low-Tech AI of Elden Ring
blurb: FromSoftware implements Elden Ring's enemy AI not with neural networks or machine learning but with a pushdown automaton written in Havok Script, a games-oriented Lua variant. Each actor maintains a stack of parameterized "Goals" that push sub-goals and unwind on success or failure, producing the series' varied boss behavior from a deliberately simple state-machine design.

title: Surprise: Valve’s new Steam Machine is here, but the price is the real shocker
blurb: Valve opened reservations for its 2026 Steam Machine, a living-room gaming PC running SteamOS, starting at $1,049 for the 512GB model and $1,349 for the 2TB version. Valve blamed the higher-than-expected pricing on the ongoing RAM and SSD shortage driven by AI demand, the same crunch that has forced console price hikes and raised costs for new gaming PC builds.

title: DHL Set to Transport Goods on New Wind-Powered Cargo Ships
blurb: DHL signed deals to ship cargo on new wind-powered vessels, using rigid sails and rotor technology to cut fuel use and emissions on ocean freight routes. The move reflects growing pressure on logistics firms to decarbonize shipping, which accounts for roughly 3% of global carbon emissions and faces tightening international regulations.

title: New AI Visibility Insights in Bing Webmaster Tools: Intents, Topics, Citation Share, Compare
blurb: Bing Webmaster Tools added four preview features showing publishers why their content gets cited in AI answers: Intents, Topics, Citation Share, and Compare.

title: Anthropic says Claude may want to see your ID
blurb: Anthropic updated its privacy policy to let it ask flagged users to verify their age or identity by uploading a government-issued ID, a selfie, and a face-geometry template, effective July 8. The company says the checks apply only to a small subset of accounts flagged for potential fraud and tie into its appeals process, not the Fable or Mythos rollout.

title: Cerebras falls 10% after chipmaker forecasts shrinking margin in first earnings report since IPO
blurb: Cerebras fell 10% in extended trading after its first earnings report since May's IPO forecast core gross margin shrinking to 36-38% in Q2, down from 46.5% in Q1. Revenue rose 92% to $193.4 million on a narrowed $14 million net loss, but the margin squeeze rattled investors, leaving the stock down 28% from its debut despite a $20B deal to supply OpenAI with compute.

title: Agility, Maker of Humanlike Robots, to Go Public in $2.5 Billion SPAC Deal
blurb: Agility Robotics agreed to go public through a SPAC merger valuing the maker of humanoid robots at $2.5 billion. The company's Digit robot already works in warehouses and manufacturing facilities for customers including Amazon, putting Agility among the first humanoid developers to reach public markets.

title: See How Sam Altman’s Personal Investments Benefit From Ties to OpenAI
blurb: A Wall Street Journal investigation details how Sam Altman's personal investments in companies like fusion startup Helion have surged in value after OpenAI explored or signed deals with Altman-linked ventures. The report sharpens scrutiny of conflicts of interest at the AI company, where the CEO's outside holdings stand to gain from partnerships he is positioned to influence.

title: Hidden Gems: Part 4
blurb: Zed published the fourth installment of its Hidden Gems series, surfacing lesser-known editor features. The post covers toggling a centered layout for wide screens, keyboard shortcuts for navigating multiple projects and git worktrees, and configuring Zed as the $EDITOR for command-line tools like git commit in the integrated terminal.

title: Google tests literature review matrix tool for NotebookLM
blurb: Google is testing a "Lit Review" tool for NotebookLM that turns uploaded sources into a structured comparison matrix, alongside a Play Books bridge.

title: Adobe’s AI Assistant Wants to Give Photographers More Time for Actual Creative Tasks
blurb: Adobe released its Firefly-powered AI Assistant across Premiere, Photoshop, Illustrator, InDesign, and Frame.io, letting users describe edits in natural language and hand off repetitive work. The agent can batch-remove backgrounds and resize files in Photoshop or import, sort, and rename clips in Premiere, aiming to shift production busywork off creators and into the background.

title: window.showDirectoryPicker opens up a whole new world
blurb: Chrome's window.showDirectoryPicker() API lets a website request access to a local directory and read or write every file inside it, enabling browser apps that own their data rather than storing it in the cloud. The author used it with Claude to build prototypes including a Lightroom-style photo browser and a node-based compositing tool, both manipulating source files directly on the filesystem.

title: LLMs are complicated now
blurb: Modern LLM architectures have accumulated the same kind of complexity that once made recommendation systems intractable, with attention variants like grouped, sparse, sliding-window, and linear stacking alongside mixture-of-experts routing and mixed-in vision and audio encoders. The author argues that as performance optimization shifts from optional to load-bearing, swapping one architecture component for another requires a fused, optimized baseline before you can even tell whether the change is worth pursuing.

title: Metric Semantic Layer: How Lyft Governs and Scales Key Data Definitions
blurb: Lyft built an internal Metric Semantic Layer that stores every key metric in one repository, pairing a plain-English description with the definitive SQL so teams stop diverging on what a given metric means. Definitions live in YAML configs with Jinja SQL templates, letting a single update flow to every downstream application while clarifying ownership and keeping technical and non-technical users on the same definition.

title: Space Force goes to (pretend) orbital war following record-fast Rocket Lab launch
blurb: The US Space Force ran its first live-fire orbital warfare exercise after Rocket Lab launched a dedicated payload on a record-fast turnaround. The drill simulated on-orbit threats and countermeasures, reflecting the military's growing treatment of space as a contested domain rather than a passive vantage point.

title: Mistral OCR 4 Ships Bounding Boxes for 170-Language Document AI
blurb: Mistral released OCR 4, adding bounding boxes, block labels, and per-word confidence scores to its document extraction API across 170 languages. The model returns document regions with coordinates so search and compliance systems can preserve page structure, costs $4 per 1,000 pages, and posted a 72% average win rate against competing OCR systems in Mistral's human evaluation.

title: My Vibe Coding Adventure, The App and the Experience, Ten Takeaways
blurb: Ben Thompson vibe-coded an app he plans to use regularly and distilled the experience into ten takeaways on what current AI coding tools can and cannot do. The piece is reportorial rather than hype, noting where the tools accelerated a non-engineer's build and where they still required manual judgment, debugging, and architectural decisions.

title: Superhuman acquires AI detection startup GPTZero
blurb: Superhuman acquired AI detection startup GPTZero, which Princeton grad Edward Tian built from a senior thesis into a service with 19 million registered users and $30 million in annual recurring revenue. GPTZero raised just $13.5 million across seed and Series A rounds, and joins a Superhuman that already shipped its own AI detector, formed after Grammarly bought the email client and rebranded under its name.

title: AI PACs pour $20 million into New York Democratic primary in AI regulation battle
blurb: AI-affiliated super PACs have spent more than $20M in New York's 12th congressional district Democratic primary, the first House race where competing AI PACs are both active. Leading the Future, backed by Andreessen Horowitz, Greg Brockman, and Perplexity, spent $8M opposing AI-safety advocate Alex Bores, while Anthropic-funded Public First Action put $11M behind him, turning the race into a proxy fight over federal AI regulation.

title: Microsoft’s next big thing for the cloud: an agent that keeps its cool when everything falls apart
blurb: Microsoft made its Azure Copilot Observability Agent generally available, an AI tool that investigates cloud incidents by correlating logs, metrics, and traces across a company's systems to point engineers toward the likely root cause. The agent diagnoses but does not act, leaving fixes like restarts and config changes to humans, and enters a crowded field already serving Datadog's Bits AI SRE and AWS's DevOps Agent.

title: Tesla Crash That Killed a Texas Woman Will be Investigated by Federal Regulators
blurb: Federal regulators opened an investigation into a Tesla crash that killed a woman in Texas on Friday while the vehicle's driver-assistance system was active, according to police. The probe by the National Highway Traffic Safety Administration adds to ongoing scrutiny of Tesla's Autopilot and Full Self-Driving software as the company expands its automated-driving ambitions.

title: Klue says hackers stole credential from 2022 that led to customer data breaches
blurb: Klue confirmed that hackers used a credential issued in 2022 for a limited pilot to breach data from its corporate customers, including LastPass and several other cybersecurity firms. The company never revoked the OAuth token after the pilot ended, and a group called Icarus has claimed the attack and is threatening to leak the stolen data unless its ransom is paid.

title: Mark Zuckerberg wants Meta to launch its own prediction market
blurb: Mark Zuckerberg has approved Meta's development of a standalone prediction market app, internally called "Arena," according to The New York Times. The Polymarket-style app would launch independent of Meta's social platforms and initially use points rather than real money, with cash betting potentially added later, as Polymarket and Kalshi push trading volumes into the tens of billions of dollars.

title: As AI Companies Race for Power, Amazon and Google Have the Lead
blurb: Amazon and Google lead the race to power AI data centers, with Amazon's existing energy infrastructure giving it an incumbent edge and Google standing out for novel sourcing approaches. The advantage matters as compute demand collides with grid constraints, making secured power capacity a competitive moat for the cloud providers selling AI infrastructure.

title: Disguised Russian banking apps topped US App Store again
blurb: A second sanctioned Russian banking app disguised itself as a productivity tool and climbed to number two on the US iPhone App Store this month, posing as an event-MC workspace called Toastmas while actually serving as a stealth client for T-Bank. Apple has since removed both apps, but the repeat incident shows how easily sanctioned financial services slip past App Store review by masquerading as ordinary utilities.

title: iOS 27 upgrades Messages app with key new features for RCS
blurb: iOS 27 beta 2 adds two key RCS upgrades to the Messages app: proper reaction support that ends the "Aaron loved an image" placeholders, and in-line replies. Both features come from the RCS 2.7 standard, and paired with Apple's recent end-to-end encryption support, they move cross-platform RCS chats closer to matching the iMessage experience.

title: Cerebras Drops After 2026 Sales Outlook Disappoints Investors
blurb: Cerebras shares fell about 10% in late trading after the newly public chipmaker issued a 2026 sales forecast that came in below investor expectations. The AI chipmaker faces the same pressure as other would-be Nvidia rivals, where strong earnings no longer guarantee gains because investors now expect the rapid growth and margins set by Nvidia during the data center buildout.

title: Alibaba sues the Pentagon to get off its Chinese military companies list
blurb: Alibaba sued the US Department of Defense in San Jose federal court, asking a judge to strike its name from the Pentagon's Section 1260H list of Chinese military companies. The June 8 designation, which Alibaba calls baseless, bars the Defense Department from contracting with the company starting June 30 and, the filing argues, raises its financing and partnership risks across American markets.

title: A US spectrum auction just raised $3.5B to pay for ripping out Huawei
blurb: An FCC auction of AWS-3 mid-band spectrum raised about $3.5B, with up to $3.3B earmarked to repay a Treasury loan covering the "rip and replace" removal of Huawei and ZTE gear from US networks. The sale closes a roughly $3B shortfall in a program Congress funded at $1.9B against $5B in carrier reimbursement requests, sidestepping the need for a fresh appropriation.

title: Backblaze gets $335 million CoreWeave bonanza
blurb: Backblaze signed a 5-year, multi-exabyte deal with CoreWeave worth $335M to supply HDD-based storage tiers inside CoreWeave's AI Object Storage. The contract reflects neoclouds shifting bulk AI data off flash, now roughly 10x more expensive per terabyte than hard drives, and follows VAST Data's $1.17B CoreWeave deal from November.

title: Boeing wins $2 billion Space Force contract for communications satellites
blurb: Boeing won a U.S. Space Force contract worth up to $2 billion to build two next-generation satellites for the Mobile User Objective System, the military's primary narrowband communications network. The win unseats incumbent Lockheed Martin, which built all five satellites currently in orbit, and extends a UHF constellation valued for penetrating foliage, weather, and urban terrain, with delivery slated for 2035.

title: Bristol fusion startup raises £23M to produce cancer-fighting isotopes while the rest of the industry is still 20 years away
blurb: Astral Systems, a Bristol fusion startup, raised £23M in a Series A led by Mercia Ventures to scale production of medical isotopes used in cancer diagnosis and treatment. Unlike fusion firms targeting long-term power generation, Astral already runs reactors and has earned more than £3M from research contracts, addressing a fragile global isotope supply chain that supports over 50 million nuclear medicine procedures a year with no reliable UK domestic source.

title: MSG Made Dossier on Activists Who Opposed Facial Recognition
blurb: Madison Square Garden compiled a dossier on activists who publicly criticized its facial recognition system, collecting their tweets and comments into a document accessible to staff inside the company. 404 Media found the file in a 45GB cache stolen by hackers and posted online this month, showing the Jim Dolan-run venue tracks specific critics of the biometric surveillance it deploys on patrons.

title: Meta launches new AI glasses in 26 styles and Muse Spark multimodal capabilties
blurb: Meta launched a new line of Meta Glasses with EssilorLuxottica, starting at $299 and shipping in 26 styles across three frames, including a Kylie Jenner collaboration. The glasses run Meta AI powered by Muse Spark, the first model from Meta Superintelligence Labs, which adds multimodal understanding, live translation in 14 new languages, and pedestrian navigation, with the same upgrade rolling out to existing Ray-Ban and Oakley Meta Glasses.

title: Anthropic launches Claude Tag on Team and Enterprise plans
blurb: Anthropic launched Claude Tag, a Slack-based agent in beta for Enterprise and Team customers that teams summon by typing @claude in a channel or thread. Running on Opus 4.8, it reads thread context, uses connected tools, schedules follow-ups, and works under its own agent identity, replacing the older Claude in Slack app, which retires on August 3, 2026.

title: Auditing DiffusionGemma Transparency
blurb: A Google DeepMind audit finds DiffusionGemma roughly as transparent as autoregressive Gemma at the variable level, but harder to follow algorithmically.

title: Free VPN And Streaming Apps Are Polluting Business Networks With Residential Proxy Traffic
blurb: Free VPN and streaming apps quietly turn users into residential proxies; Infoblox found 65% of cloud customers queried such domains in 2026.

title: Valve opens Steam Machine pre-orders with queue lottery and hefty prices amid AI squeeze
blurb: Valve opened pre-orders for its new Steam Machine, using a queue lottery to ration limited stock as component costs climb under AI-driven demand for memory and silicon. Pricing came in steep, with the AI squeeze on DRAM and GPU supply pushing the console-style PC well above earlier expectations and capping how many units buyers can reserve.

title: Meta pauses mandatory AI training program that tracked employee keystrokes after internal data leak exposed sensitive staff information company-wide — employees express frustration over poor handling of data
blurb: Meta paused its mandatory Model Capability Initiative, an internal AI training program that recorded employee keystrokes, mouse movements, conversations, and performance data, after a leak exposed that information company-wide. Meta classified the incident as a SEV 2 and says it found no sign of improper access, but the breach drew internal backlash over privacy and the failure to restrict the data from the start.

title: Meta's WhatsApp head to step down, will be replaced by Indian fintech founder Kunal Shah
blurb: Meta named Cred founder Kunal Shah to lead WhatsApp, replacing Will Cathcart, who ran the messaging service for over seven years and is moving to a product-building role. As part of the appointment, Meta is investing $900M in Shah's Indian fintech startup at a $4.5B post-money valuation, installing an outside founder atop an app with more than 3 billion monthly users.

title: California AB 2047 makes 3D printers off-limits to students, educators, business
blurb: California's AB 2047 would require every 3D printer sold in the state to run a DOJ-certified "detection algorithm," a capability the bill's opponents argue cannot reliably exist. The measure has cleared the Assembly and moved to the State Senate, where critics warn it conflicts with First Amendment protections for source code and could pull printers from thousands of schools, libraries, and small businesses.

title: Microsoft’s Satya Nadella: We Can’t Let AI Giants Eat the Economy
blurb: Microsoft CEO Satya Nadella argued that AI companies must "earn society's permission" rather than concentrate economic power, warning that a handful of dominant labs could capture too much of the economy. He framed AI's legitimacy as contingent on broad-based productivity gains and trust, not just model capability, positioning Microsoft against a winner-take-all outcome.

title: Sequoia and Founders Fund just backed a drone startup whose drones failed: report
blurb: Stark, the Berlin maker of autonomous strike drones, raised €500M at a valuation above €3.5B from Sequoia, Founders Fund, and the NATO Innovation Fund, roughly €200M more than it sought. The round closed despite Virtus drones failing to hit a single target across four live-fire trials with British and German forces in November 2025, after which Germany approved a €269M Bundeswehr contract anyway.

title: Tencent Testing New AI Agent for WeChat Workplace App
blurb: Tencent began rolling out Dayuan, an AI assistant built on DeepSeek's V4 model, to select users of WeCom, the enterprise version of WeChat. The natural-language agent intensifies the contest among Chinese tech giants to embed AI into their workplace ecosystems and lock in corporate users in the post-ChatGPT era.

title: German Rail Services Disrupted After Comms System Failure
blurb: Deutsche Bahn brought trains to a nationwide standstill late Tuesday after a failed replacement component took down its GSM-R digital-radio network, the system that links dispatchers and drivers. Technicians restored the radio network within hours and service had broadly resumed by Wednesday morning, though the outage shows how a single swapped part can halt an entire country's rail network.

title: Sorry, Slackbot. Claude is taking your job
blurb: Anthropic introduced Claude Tag (@Claude), a research-preview Slack assistant that anyone in a channel can summon to share one always-on identity, pick up where colleagues left off, and learn a team's work over time. Admins control its access to tools and sensitive data per channel, and an optional "ambient" mode lets it proactively surface relevant updates and chase stale tasks, starting with Claude Enterprise and Team customers.

title: SoftBank’s Son Sets Bold Net Asset Value Target on Promise of ‘Super AI’
blurb: SoftBank CEO Masayoshi Son set a target of growing the company's net asset value to $6.189 trillion within the next decade, betting on the arrival of what he calls "super AI." The goal would require roughly a tenfold increase from SoftBank's current value, underscoring Son's continued concentration of the firm around large AI infrastructure and chip bets.

title: Swift Package Index joins Apple, pledges to remain open source
blurb: Swift Package Index, the community-run open-source search engine and metadata index for Swift packages, is joining Apple with no immediate changes to how packages are indexed or documentation is hosted. The team pledged to keep the project open source and flagged plans for package signing and identity features, while native Xcode integration for searching and adding dependencies looks like a plausible next step.

title: Superhuman has acquired AI authenticity service GPTZero
blurb: Superhuman acquired AI-detection service GPTZero to fold hallucination and plagiarism checks into its Superhuman Go assistant; terms were undisclosed.

title: UK funds £60M AI labs to challenge US tech dominance with open-source models
blurb: The UK government awarded £60M to Oxford and University College London to build two academic AI labs focused on open-source, low-hardware model architectures. Spread over six years, the funding aims to cut Britain's reliance on US tech firms and includes £2M per lab to hire at least ten doctoral students each.

title: Optocam Zero: a Pi Zero based digital camera made using off the shelf components
blurb: A pocketable Raspberry Pi Zero digital camera built from off-the-shelf parts, with full 3D-print files, BOM, and build guide in the repo.

title: Apple and Tesla trade secrets reportedly exposed following a Tata Electronics cyberattack
blurb: Tata Electronics confirmed a cyberattack on some of its systems that may have exposed Apple and Tesla trade secrets, including component designs and iPhone production data tied to its India manufacturing operations. The World Leaks ransomware group claims it stole over 200,000 files totaling 630GB, posted samples on the dark web, and sent Tata a ransom demand, while Apple has opened an investigation.

title: Bold move, Cotton: Trump administration tells US techies it expects American quantum computer by 2028
blurb: The Trump administration told US technology firms it expects a domestically built, working quantum computer by 2028, setting a hard national deadline for a milestone the field still considers years out. The target pressures labs and hardware makers to accelerate error-correction and qubit-scaling work, and signals that quantum has joined chips and AI as a strategic-competition priority with China.

title: Meta debuts new, cheaper smart glasses under its own brand
blurb: Meta released Meta Glasses, a new smart eyewear line starting at $299 that drops the Ray-Ban and Oakley branding while keeping its EssilorLuxottica manufacturing partnership. The display-less glasses pack a camera, speakers, a dedicated Meta AI button, and over eight hours of battery, landing a week after Snap's $2,195 Specs and reinforcing Meta's 80%-plus grip on the category.

title: Samsung announces ultra-fast UFS 5.0 storage to supercharge mobile AI
blurb: Samsung announced UFS 5.0, a mobile storage standard hitting 10.8GB/s read and 9.5GB/s write speeds, roughly double UFS 4.1. The chip targets on-device AI, where slow storage bottlenecks local models, and is 40% more power efficient and 16.7% smaller than its predecessor, with Samsung planning rollout across phones, wearables, and XR devices.

title: Valve finally confirms Steam Machine prices, starts at $1049 for 512GB option
blurb: Valve confirmed Steam Machine pricing, with its all-in-one SteamOS PC starting at $1,049 for the 512GB base model and topping out at $1,428 for a 2TB configuration bundled with the new Steam Controller. The company blamed memory price hikes and component shortages for abandoning its original price target, with pre-orders opening June 25 and shipping June 29.

title: Instagram looks to take on streaming services with longer-form, episodic, and live formats for its TV app
blurb: Instagram is expanding its TV app with longer-form videos, episodic series, and live creator experiences, moving beyond short clips to compete with Netflix and Amazon Prime Video for living room viewers. The app is rolling out to Samsung TVs alongside existing Amazon Fire and Google TV support, and gaining channels, phone-to-TV casting, Stories playback, and a dedicated section for horizontal video.

title: Qualcomm is about to spend $4B on a startup that makes no chips — because Nvidia’s real moat was never the silicon, and Cristiano Amon finally figured out the shortcut
blurb: Qualcomm is in talks to acquire Modular Inc. for roughly $4B, a startup that makes no chips but builds a compiler and inference runtime that lets AI models run across different silicon without rewrites. The deal targets the software layer behind Nvidia's CUDA lock-in rather than the hardware, giving Qualcomm's stalled data center roadmap a way to make non-Nvidia accelerators viable ahead of its Investor Day.

title: AI Engineer Claims to Have Cracked Linear A
blurb: Tom Di Mino, a self-taught AI engineer and amateur linguist, claims to have deciphered Linear A, the Bronze-age Minoan script that has resisted translation for over a century. Di Mino argues the script encodes an extinct Semitic language ancestral to Hebrew, Arabic, and Aramaic, and his solution is now under review by linguists at Rutgers and Cambridge, with the underlying claim still unverified.

title: Inspired by musical greeting cards, DARPA demands tiny, cheap, self-modifying systems
blurb: DARPA's new program asks engineers to build tiny, low-cost electronic systems that can rewrite their own hardware behavior after deployment, drawing inspiration from the disposable chips in musical greeting cards. The agency wants devices cheap enough to be expendable yet capable of self-modifying in the field, a combination it hopes will reshape how sensors and edge electronics are designed and discarded.

title: Google Home will soon get better at recognizing you
blurb: Google Home will use non-biometric signals like body size and clothing color to identify tagged people when their faces aren't visible.

title: Meta’s Cox Sought Shah’s WhatsApp Advice — Then Made Him Leader
blurb: Meta named Kunal Shah, the Cred founder and prominent Indian angel investor, as head of WhatsApp after a cold-email exchange with Chief Product Officer Chris Cox. Cox had been canvassing entrepreneurs across India, Brazil, and Mexico for advice on the role, markets where WhatsApp is central to commerce, and Shah's pitch on the app's future won him the job.

title: GM installs robots at flagship EV factory after laying off 1,300 workers
blurb: General Motors installed about 50 FANUC robot arms at its Factory Zero EV plant in Detroit while roughly 1,000 workers laid off in March remain out of work. The United Auto Workers union pushed back, arguing GM could recall idled members instead of automating, as Ford, Stellantis, and Hyundai also expand assembly-line robotics across US plants.

title: Valve's Steam Machine ships June 29 for $1,049, but you probably won't be able to buy one yet
blurb: Valve's Steam Machine, a TV-friendly gaming PC running Linux-based SteamOS, goes on sale June 29 starting at $1,049, with a 2TB model at $1,349 and an optional $79 Steam Controller. Valve says its original sub-$1,000 vision is "no longer viable" after AI-driven RAM and storage price spikes, and warns pricing could rise in future batches as component costs climb.

title: NHTSA investigating alleged Tesla Autopilot crash that killed woman in her home
blurb: NHTSA opened an investigation into a fatal crash in which a Tesla Model 3 driver, who told police he had the automated driver-assistance system engaged, left the road at high speed and struck a Texas home, killing 76-year-old Martha Avila. Police say the driver was not intoxicated and is cooperating, and it remains unclear whether Autopilot or the driver caused the crash.

title: Morgan Stanley doubles China humanoid robot shipment forecast as commercialization accelerates
blurb: Morgan Stanley doubled its forecast for China's humanoid robot shipments to 50,000 units this year, the bank's second upgrade in 2026 after an initial January projection of 14,000. It expects the market to reach $2B in 2026 and $15B by 2030, with annual shipments hitting 446,000 units as Chinese manufacturers deploy robots in factories, convenience stores, and restaurants under heavy state subsidies.

title: Blackstone plans $30B for Japan AI data centres, and is unbothered by bubble talk
blurb: Blackstone plans to invest roughly $30B in AI data centres in Japan over the next three to five years, targeting facilities with combined capacity above one gigawatt. President Jonathan Gray dismissed bubble concerns, arguing the risk of building too little compute outweighs overbuilding, and paired the push with a record $13.1B Asia private-equity fund raised earlier this month.

title: Odd police video shows drone removing knife from motionless suspect
blurb: The Sacramento County Sheriff's Office used a magnet-equipped quadcopter to pull a knife from the hand of a motionless, unresponsive felony suspect during a SWAT standoff, in what it called a nationwide first. A first drone located the suspect hiding in a garage, while a second, piloted via FPV goggles, snagged the blade and flew it outside, part of a broader trend of US departments deploying drones for first-responder tasks.

title: NASA Watchdog: Launch Infrastructure Nearing Capacity
blurb: NASA's inspector general warned that the agency's launch infrastructure will hit capacity by 2030, as annual launches at Kennedy Space Center climb to a projected 268 from 109 in 2025. Aging power, gas, and road systems would cost at least $1B to upgrade, but construction budgets have been cut 11% to 47% over five years, and NASA is barred from charging the commercial users behind 70% of its launches.

title: Investing in Probook
blurb: a16z led Probook's $34M Series A, bringing the home-services AI startup's total funding to $40M after a Sequoia-led seed.

title: Walmart-backed Flipkart expands quick-commerce push as Amazon ramps up in India
blurb: Flipkart said its Minutes quick-commerce service has built 1,000 micro-fulfillment centers less than two years after launch, with plans to reach 1,500 by the end of 2026. The buildout would make the Walmart-backed retailer India's second-largest quick-commerce network behind Blinkit's 2,243 centers, as Amazon expands Amazon Now to over 500 centers and races into the same market.

title: Ten years of ClickHouse in open source
blurb: ClickHouse marks ten years as open source, having grown into the most popular open source analytical database with more than 2,000 contributors since its June 2016 release. The project's creator details its approach to building fully in the open, treating pull requests as experiments tested with production-level scrutiny and crediting every contributor in the changelog and a system.contributors table.

title: Americans and AI 2026: Chatbots, Smart Devices, and Views on Impact
blurb: About half of U.S. adults now use AI chatbots, up sharply from summer 2024, with roughly one in four using them daily, according to a new Pew Research Center survey of 5,119 adults. Adoption is rising for smart speakers and AI summaries too, but sentiment tilts negative: more Americans, including younger adults, expect AI to harm rather than help society and think it is advancing too fast.

title: BlackRock-Backed AIP, KKR Said to Court Stack Asia Data Centers
blurb: Stack Infrastructure is preparing to sell its Asia-Pacific data centers as soon as July, drawing interest from BlackRock-backed AIP, Brookfield, KKR, IFM Investors, and DigitalBridge. The sale reflects intensifying competition among infrastructure investors for AI-ready capacity in the region, where DigitalBridge is itself being acquired by SoftBank.

title: South Korea is in talks with Samsung and SK Hynix over a second chip cluster
blurb: South Korea's government is in talks with Samsung and SK Hynix over a second large-scale chip cluster, with a presidential adviser saying AI demand could pull next-phase fab construction forward by more than a decade to 2034 or 2035. No investment figure has been confirmed, though Korean outlets peg potential commitments in the hundreds of trillions of won, with the southwest region weighed as a likely site.

title: An off-grid power project gets a major proof of concept. What it means for GE Vernova
blurb: Chevron will build a 2.7-gigawatt natural-gas power plant in Reeves County, Texas, called Project Kilby, to supply a planned Microsoft data center under a long-term energy purchase agreement. The behind-the-meter facility runs on GE Vernova gas turbines, validating off-grid power as a way to feed AI workloads without straining local grids, and strengthening GE Vernova's backlog as turbine demand and pricing climb.

title: A PhD student’s nerve-healing polymer just raised €60M to take on the US operating room
blurb: TISSIUM, a Paris-based medtech spun out of MIT and Harvard research, raised €60M to scale COAPTIUM CONNECT, the only FDA-authorized sutureless nerve repair system, across US operating rooms. The device wraps a 3D-printed polymer chamber around severed nerve ends and seals it with light-cured bioresorbable polymer; in a 12-patient study, all participants regained full function and reported no pain at one year.

title: Boost Inference Performance up to 15x on NVIDIA Blackwell Using DFlash Speculative Decoding
blurb: NVIDIA released DFlash, an open-source lightweight block-diffusion drafter model for speculative decoding that generates an entire block of candidate tokens in a single forward pass, turning sequential drafting into block-parallel GPU work while preserving output quality through verification. DFlash boosts inference for gpt-oss-120b on Blackwell by up to 15x at the same interactivity level and nearly doubles interactivity for Llama 3.1 8B versus EAGLE-3, with 20 checkpoints released on Hugging Face.

title: Moebius: 0.2B image inpainting model with 10B-level performance
blurb: Moebius is a 0.2B-parameter image inpainting model that the authors report matches the quality of 10B-level systems. It uses a latent diffusion framework with restructured U-Net blocks and an adaptive multi-granularity distillation strategy that aligns the lightweight model with a high-capacity teacher to offset the accuracy lost to extreme compression.

title: Mistral OCR 4
blurb: Mistral released OCR 4, a compact document model that returns bounding boxes, typed-block classification, and inline confidence scores alongside extracted text across 170 languages. The model tops OlmOCRBench at 85.20 with a 72% average win rate over competing OCR and document-AI systems, runs in a single self-hosted container, and feeds ingestion for RAG and enterprise search pipelines.

title: Interactions API: our primary interface for Gemini models and agents
blurb: Google announced that its Interactions API has reached general availability and is now the primary interface for calling Gemini models and running agents, replacing the old role-based schema with typed steps. The GA release adds Managed Agents that provision a remote Linux sandbox per call, background execution via background=True, mixed built-in and custom tools, and Flex and Priority tiers that trade cost for latency, with Flex cutting costs by 50%.

title: China's LineShine supercomputer dethrones US' El Capitan, secures first place in Top 500 list — first machine in the rankings to sustain more than 2 ExaFLOPS of double-precision performance using only CPUs
blurb: China's LineShine supercomputer took the top spot on the Top 500 list, hitting 2.198 FP64 ExaFLOPS on Linpack and becoming the first ranked machine to sustain over 2 ExaFLOPS of double-precision performance using only CPUs. Built with 13.79 million Armv9-based LX2 cores, it trails El Capitan on mixed-precision HPL-MxP, limiting its usefulness for AI training while excelling at traditional HPC workloads.

title: Midjourney Medical
blurb: Midjourney launched Midjourney Medical, a new health division built around a radiation-free full-body Ultrasonic CT scanner that uses 500,000 transducers to image the body in 60 seconds. The company plans to open its first scanning spa in San Francisco in 2027 and aims for a fleet of over 50,000 scanners handling a billion scans per month by 2031.

title: Fake AI Agent Skill Passed Security Scans and Reportedly Reached 26,000 Agents
blurb: Security firm AIR built a fake AI agent skill called brand-landingpage, pushed it through a skill marketplace with around 36,000 GitHub stars and an Instagram ad, and says it reached roughly 26,000 agents, including some on corporate accounts. Every scanner it tested cleared the skill because the malicious instructions sat behind an external link AIR controlled and swapped only after the skill was widely installed.

title: Anthropic’s Fable 5 Model Jailbroken Within Days
blurb: Anthropic's Fable 5, the safety-restricted version of its Mythos Preview model built with guardrails meant to block the generation of cyberattacks, was jailbroken within days of release. The bypass defeated the classifiers intended to enforce those restrictions, adding to a pattern of frontier safety models falling quickly to determined red-teamers and public jailbreak efforts.

title: Amazon S3 annotations: attach rich, queryable context directly to your objects
blurb: Amazon S3 introduced annotations, a metadata capability that lets you attach up to 1,000 named annotations per object, each up to 1 MB and totaling 1 GB, in JSON, XML, YAML, or plain text. Annotations can be modified or deleted without rewriting objects, travel with objects during copy and replication, and flow into managed tables that are queryable with Amazon Athena when S3 Metadata is enabled.

title: Announcing Amazon EC2 G7 instances accelerated by NVIDIA RTX PRO 4500 Blackwell Server Edition GPUs
blurb: AWS made Amazon EC2 G7 instances generally available, becoming the first major cloud provider to offer NVIDIA RTX PRO 4500 Blackwell Server Edition GPUs paired with custom sixth-generation Intel Xeon Scalable processors. The instances deliver up to 4.6x AI inference and 2.1x graphics performance over G6, with 32GB of memory per GPU, 700 Gbps of EFA networking, and up to 7.6TB of local NVMe storage.

title: Researchers Detail DifyTap Flaws in Dify That Could Expose AI Chats Across Tenants
blurb: Zafran Security disclosed four vulnerabilities, collectively named DifyTap, in Dify, an open-source agentic workflow platform with over 146,000 GitHub stars, that could let attackers read AI conversations from other tenants' applications without authentication. Two were rated critical and three carried cross-tenant impact, exposing private chats, model responses, and uploaded documents across customers on Dify's multi-tenant cloud service.

title: Unlimited OCR: One-Shot Long-Horizon Parsing
blurb: Baidu released Unlimited-OCR, a document parsing model that extends Deepseek-OCR to handle long-horizon, multi-page inputs in a single pass. It runs on Hugging Face transformers with separate single-image and multi-page modes, supports 32K-token outputs, and converts full PDFs to images for end-to-end parsing.

title: China’s LineShine dethrones El Capitan as the world’s fastest supercomputer
blurb: China's LineShine debuted atop the 67th TOP500 list at 2.198 exaflops, ending El Capitan's reign and becoming the first China-based system to lead since Sunway TaihuLight in 2017. It reached No. 1 with a CPU-only domestic design built on China's LingKun platform, LingQi interconnect, and Kylin OS, while U.S. systems still rely on AMD and Intel accelerators.

title: MolmoMotion: Language-guided 3D motion forecasting
blurb: MolmoMotion is a language-guided model from Allen AI that predicts the future 3D trajectories of object points given a video frame, query points, and a written action description, outperforming existing motion forecasting methods. Alongside the model weights, the team released MolmoMotion-1M, a dataset of 3D point trajectories paired with action descriptions drawn from 1.16M videos, and PointMotionBench, a human-validated benchmark of 2.7K clips.

title: Daybreak: Tools for securing every organization in the world
blurb: OpenAI expanded its Daybreak program to focus on patching vulnerabilities at machine speed, launching an updated Codex Security plugin and the full version of GPT-5.5-Cyber for trusted defenders. The new model reaches 85.6% on CyberGym versus 81.8% for GPT-5.5, and OpenAI also started Patch the Planet with Trail of Bits to move open-source projects like cURL, Go, and Python from findings to fixes.

title: Data Exposure Flaws Threaten Dify AI Platform Used by 1 Million Apps
blurb: Zafran Security disclosed four vulnerabilities, collectively called DifyTap, in the open source LLMOps platform Dify, which powers over 1 million applications across more than 50 industries. The flaws, two rated critical, let attackers in multi-tenant cloud setups read other customers' private chats, trigger cross-tenant internal API calls, and access files uploaded by other tenants. Dify version 1.14.2 patches the issues.

title: Qwen-AgentWorld: Language World Models for General Agents
blurb: Qwen-AgentWorld is a pair of language world models, at 35B-A3B and 397B-A17B, trained to simulate agentic environments across 7 domains via long chain-of-thought reasoning over 10M+ real-world interaction trajectories. The authors show it outperforms frontier models on their AgentWorldBench, works as a decoupled simulator that beats real-environment training for agentic RL, and serves as a warm-up that lifts downstream performance across 7 agentic benchmarks.

title: DREAM: Dense Retrieval Embeddings via Autoregressive Modeling
blurb: DREAM is a method for training dense retrieval embeddings using an LLM's next-token prediction loss instead of contrastive learning, removing the need for costly labeled positive and negative document pairs. It injects retriever-generated query-document similarity scores into selected attention heads of a frozen LLM, and outperforms existing baselines on the BEIR and RTEB benchmarks across embedding backbones from 0.5B to 3B parameters.

title: Claude Tag
blurb: Anthropic released Claude Tag, a beta feature for Claude Enterprise and Team customers that lets teams add Claude to Slack channels as a taggable member with access to chosen tools, data, and codebases. Once tagged, Claude breaks down delegated tasks and works through them autonomously, building context from its channels over time, and now generates 65% of Anthropic's product team's code internally.

title: The text in Claude Code’s “Extended Thinking” output
blurb: The author found that Claude Code's on-disk "thinking blocks" contain only a 600-character encrypted signature, not readable reasoning. Per Anthropic's docs, extended thinking is encrypted with a key Anthropic holds, and the API returns only a summary of the model's reasoning rather than the reasoning itself, with full access gated behind an enterprise agreement. The author warns that this breaks any promise of a genuine audit trail, since the logic that actually drove an agent's actions can't be reconstructed from local files.

title: OpenAI launches new security tools and updates GPT-5.5-Cyber
blurb: OpenAI launched a suite of defensive security tools, including an updated Codex Security plugin, a limited release of its GPT-5.5-Cyber model, and Patch the Planet, an open-source initiative built with Trail of Bits, HackerOne, and project maintainers. The push extends Daybreak from finding bugs to landing fixes, with GPT-5.5-Cyber scoring 85.6% on CyberGym versus 81.8% for GPT-5.5 and gated to verified defenders.

title: Anthropic’s powerful Mythos AI reportedly breached ‘almost all’ NSA classified systems within a few hours during red-team test — report sheds more light on the U.S. government's sudden ban on the flagship models
blurb: The Economist reported that Anthropic's Mythos model breached "almost all" of the NSA's classified systems within hours during an authorized red-team test, according to NSA director Gen. Joshua Rudd. The disclosure explains the June 12 US directive barring foreign nationals from accessing the Fable 5 and Mythos 5 models, which prompted Anthropic to disable both globally rather than enforce nationality-based access.

title: Show HN: Oak – Git alternative designed for agents
blurb: Oak is an open-source version control system built for AI coding agents, released in public beta (v0.99.0) as a Cargo workspace pairing a reusable VCS library with a CLI that agents like Claude Code, Codex, and Cursor drive. It uses branch-per-session as the unit of work, branch descriptions instead of per-commit messages, and content-addressed lazy mounts that hydrate on demand for faster performance than git on agent workloads.

title: Build your own vulnerability harness
blurb: Cloudflare's Project Glasswing post details how to build a model-agnostic vulnerability scanning harness that treats frontier models as interchangeable components, using one model for discovery and a different one for validation so flaws are cross-checked by distinct reasoning. The architecture traces vulnerabilities across cross-repo dependencies and filters thousands of raw candidates into a triaged queue, addressing why generic coding agents fail at persistence, deduplication, and surviving context compaction.

title: OpenAI Launches Daybreak To Automate Vulnerability Patching
blurb: OpenAI launched Daybreak, an agent that automatically detects and patches software vulnerabilities across codebases. The system is positioned to find and remediate security flaws at scale, generating fixes for known weaknesses before they can be exploited and reducing the manual workload on security teams.

title: CrossPool: Efficient Multi-LLM Serving for Cold MoE Models through KV-Cache and Weight Disaggregation
blurb: CrossPool is a serving engine for cold MoE models that splits FFN weights and KV-cache into two separate GPU memory pools, consolidating weights across rarely-used models while a shared KV-cache pool serves active demand. It pairs a KV-cache planner and virtualizer with a layer-wise pipeline scheduler and persistent kernels, cutting P99 time-between-tokens by up to 10.4x over the state-of-the-art kvcached-based multi-LLM system.

title: Copilot 'SearchLeak' Attack Allows 1-Click Data Theft
blurb: Researchers disclosed "SearchLeak," a three-stage prompt-injection attack against Microsoft Copilot that enabled one-click theft of user data through hidden URLs and other manipulated variables. Microsoft has patched the critical flaw, but it belongs to a growing class of AI prompt-injection issues that exploit indirect inputs to exfiltrate sensitive information.

title: MosaicLeaks: Can your research agent keep a secret?
blurb: MosaicLeaks is a benchmark that tests whether deep-research agents leak private enterprise data through their external web queries, where an adversary watching only the cumulative query log can reassemble fragments into sensitive facts. The authors found that agents frequently leaked private information, and their privacy-aware RL method, PA-DR, cut answer leakage from 34.0% to 9.9% while raising strict chain success from 48.7% to 58.7%.

title: 7,000 Langflow servers are under attack. LangGraph and LangChain have the same holes
blurb: Researchers disclosed remote code execution and credential-theft flaws across three widely deployed AI agent frameworks, with roughly 7,000 Langflow servers already under active in-the-wild attack. Check Point chained a SQL injection in LangGraph's SQLite checkpointer to RCE, Tenable and VulnCheck tracked a path traversal in Langflow's file upload endpoint, and Cyera documented a LangChain-core flaw that reads secrets off disk.

title: Grad Detect: Gradient-Based Hallucination Detection in LLMs
blurb: Grad Detect is a gradient-based method that flags LLM hallucinations by analyzing layer-wise gradient patterns from a single forward-backward pass at inference. Across eleven models from four architectural families, it outperforms confidence-based and sampling-based baselines on Q&A benchmarks, with the final five layers carrying over 97% of the discriminative signal for efficient deployment.

title: Prompt Injection as Role Confusion
blurb: This post argues that prompt injection stems from a fundamental flaw in how LLMs perceive roles, since everything a model receives, including system prompts, user messages, tool outputs, and its own reasoning, arrives as one continuous token stream partitioned only by role tags. From this framing the authors construct new attacks, explain existing mechanistic interpretability results, and predict when injections succeed, then sketch a research agenda for a science of roles.

title: SpaceX to acquire Cursor for $60B in stock, days after blockbuster IPO
blurb: SpaceX agreed to acquire Cursor for $60B in stock, days after Cursor's IPO, in a move aimed at reviving SpaceX's struggling AI division. The company has told IPO investors it sees a $26 trillion addressable market in AI, and the all-stock structure ties the acquisition's value to SpaceX's recently public shares.

title: Agentic coding and persistent returns to expertise
blurb: Anthropic analyzed roughly 400,000 Claude Code sessions from October 2025 to April 2026 to study how people direct agentic coding. Users typically make the planning decisions while Claude handles execution, and the more domain expertise someone brings, the more work Claude does per instruction and the more often the session succeeds. Over the seven months, time spent debugging fell by nearly half, usage shifted toward end-to-end agentic work, and the estimated value of a typical task rose about 25%.

title: Predicting model behavior before release by simulating deployment
blurb: OpenAI introduced a deployment simulation method that predicts how a model will behave in production before it ships. The approach runs models through simulated deployment scenarios to surface failure modes, misalignment, and unexpected behaviors early, giving safety teams a way to catch problems that standard benchmarks miss.

title: Lighthouse agentic browsing scoring
blurb: Lighthouse added an Agentic Browsing category that runs deterministic audits on how well a site supports machine interaction, checking WebMCP tool registration via the Chrome DevTools Protocol and a filtered subset of accessibility-tree signals like programmatic names, role integrity, and visibility. Rather than a 0-100 score, it reports a fractional pass ratio plus pass/fail status, since standards for the agentic web are still emerging.

title: OpenThoughts-Agent: Data Recipes for Agentic Models
blurb: OpenThoughts-Agent is a fully open data curation pipeline for training agentic language models, built from over 100 controlled ablation experiments on task sources and diversity. Fine-tuning Qwen3-32B on its 100K-example training set reached 44.8% average accuracy across seven agentic benchmarks, a 3.9 point gain over the strongest prior open model, Nemotron-Terminal-32B, with the data outperforming alternatives at every training set size.

title: Reinforcement Learning for Computer-Use Agents with Autonomous Evaluation
blurb: This paper proposes an RL fine-tuning framework that uses a vision-language model to judge task completion from a final screenshot, supplying terminal rewards for computer-use agents without handcrafted reward functions or manual labels. Modeling the imperfect evaluator as a noisy binary reward channel and correcting for it, the method lifts success rates by 12.6 points over zero-shot and 5.1 points over raw evaluator rewards across macOSWorld, Windows Agent Arena, and OSWorld.

title: Bayesian control for coding agents
blurb: This paper formulates coding agent orchestration as cost-sensitive sequential hypothesis testing, using a Bayesian controller that maintains a belief over candidate correctness and dynamically decides whether to gather more evidence, refine, verify, or stop. Across six generators and nine benchmarks, the approach helps most when verification is costly and critics are informative but imperfect, and its belief state yields a correctness score that beats token-probability and tool-success baselines for uncertainty quantification.

title: Understanding the (In)Security of Vibe-Coded Applications
blurb: This paper presents a systematic security study of vibe-coded applications, where users build software primarily through natural-language interactions with AI agents. Analyzing a large corpus of real-world apps, the authors find recurring vulnerability patterns distinct from conventional development, including placeholder logic, unfiltered input, and exposed secrets, rooted in agent limitations like memory loss and insufficient security knowledge. Better models and prompting reduce these flaws but do not eliminate the underlying risks.

title: Circle Publishes Official USDC Spec for Machine Payments Protocol, Enabling Crosschain Agent-to-Agent Commerce
blurb: Circle published a formal USDC method specification for the Machine Payments Protocol, standardizing how AI agents settle USDC payments across EVM-compatible chains and Solana. The spec adds a standardized agent payment interface, the first crosschain profile in MPP via Circle Gateway, and support for USDCx on Stacks, extending coverage to Bitcoin-layer settlement.

title: 0x Opens Swap API to AI Agents Paying $0.01 Per Request in USDC
blurb: 0x opened its Swap API to AI agents, who can now pay $0.01 per request in USDC from their own wallets with no API key or account setup required. Built with Alchemy AgentPay on the HTTP 402 standard, the integration follows the x402 flow where an agent's on-chain USDC payment is verified by a proxy before swap data is released, accepted on Base and Solana.

title: GLM-5.2 Raises the Bar for Open Models
blurb: GLM-5.2 released last week as likely the strongest open model available, a substantial step up from GLM-5.1 and closer to the frontier than DeepSeek R1 was at its peak. The author argues it sits roughly four to seven months behind the absolute frontier at a lower price, though missing features like vision and weak generalization make finding its practical niche difficult.

title: AI coding agents taught robots how to install GPUs and cut zip ties
blurb: Researchers at NVIDIA's GEAR lab, Carnegie Mellon, and UC Berkeley released ENPIRE, an agent harness that lets AI coding agents autonomously direct robot training, teaching robotic arms to cut zip ties and insert GPUs into motherboard sockets. The framework was tested with OpenAI's Codex, Anthropic's Claude Code, and Moonshot's Kimi Code, which independently developed and retained training approaches that raised success rates over repeated self-directed cycles.

title: AI Startup Midjourney Pivots to Health With Ultrasound Machine
blurb: Midjourney announced its first hardware project, an ultrasound machine, marking the image-generation startup's unexpected move into personal health and medical devices. The company shared the news at a San Francisco event, signaling a pivot beyond the consumer AI image tools that built its business.

title: Anthropic’s Mythos found flaws in classified US systems during a government test
blurb: Anthropic's most capable model, Mythos, surfaced vulnerabilities in classified US government systems within hours during an intelligence-agency red-team exercise run under the controlled-access Project Glasswing program. The model was built to find and exploit software flaws, having earlier turned up thousands of zero-days across major operating systems and browsers, though finding a weakness is not the same as exploiting it and no real system was breached.

title: How GPT-5 helped immunologist Derya Unutmaz solve a 3-year-old mystery
blurb: OpenAI published an account of how GPT-5 Pro helped immunologist Derya Unutmaz resolve a three-year-old question about T cell behavior that had stalled his lab's work. The model's reasoning produced a mechanistic hypothesis that the team is now pursuing for cancer and autoimmune research applications.

title: Scaling Laws for Task-Specific LLM Distillation
blurb: This paper derives empirical scaling laws for task-specific LLM distillation, quantifying how in-domain and general-knowledge performance respond to dataset size, compression ratio, supervision format, and iterative pruning schedule. Using quantitative finance as the test domain, the authors find that general-knowledge benchmarks collapse well before in-domain quality degrades, and that chain-of-thought supervision actively recovers general knowledge that pruning erases.

title: Evaluating the Interpretability of Sparse Autoencoders with Concept Annotations
blurb: This paper introduces a human-grounded framework for evaluating how well sparse autoencoder (SAE) latents align with human-annotated concepts in vision and vision-language models, using two synthetic benchmarks (synCUB and synCOCO), a many-to-one matching procedure called Fully-Binary Matching Pursuit, and a perturbation-based TAPAScore. Testing SAEs trained on CLIP and DINOv2 embeddings, the authors find that greater overcompleteness can reduce interpretability, suggesting moderate dictionary sizes offer the best trade-off.

title: Cyclic Denoising Reveals Ultrastable Memories in Diffusion Models
blurb: This paper introduces cyclic denoising, an extraction attack that runs repeated forward and reverse diffusion at controlled noise amplitudes to surface memorized training images, including stock photos, brand watermarks, and web-crawl artifacts. The attack needs only sampler-level control, with no gradients, weights, prompts, or prior knowledge of training data, and the authors demonstrate it on Stable Diffusion v1.4 and a pixel-space DDPM.

title: A public Sentry key is all it takes to hijack Claude Code, Cursor, and Codex
blurb: Tenet Security's Threat Labs documented an attack it calls agentjacking, in which a single forged Sentry error report can turn AI coding agents like Claude Code, Cursor, and Codex into code-execution engines on a developer's own machine. The attack exploits Sentry's public, write-only DSN credential and the inability of models reading MCP output to distinguish data from instructions, a limit no config change can fix.

title: How We Made Cloud Browsers 3x Cheaper and Faster
blurb: Browser Use rebuilt its cloud browser infrastructure on Firecracker microVMs, cutting session costs from $0.06 to $0.02 per browser hour while starting new sessions in under a second. To lower customer costs, the team runs Firecracker nested inside regular EC2 instances rather than on bare-metal servers, replacing an earlier Unikraft setup that idled cheaply but scaled poorly during traffic spikes.

title: Chinese supercomputer using local processors heads TOP500 list
blurb: A Chinese supercomputer built on domestically designed processors took the top spot on the latest TOP500 list of the world's most powerful systems. The result marks a shift away from US chips in elite high-performance computing and signals China's progress in building leading-edge hardware without access to foreign components.

title: Large-Language-Model Discovery of Quantum LDPC Codes through Structured Concept Evolution
blurb: This paper introduces structured concept evolution (SCE), a search framework that pairs a large language model with an algebraic mutation grammar to discover lifted-product quantum LDPC codes. Rather than asking the model to design codes from scratch, SCE evolves algebraic specifications paired with executable programs, surfacing competitive abelian and non-abelian code families using lightweight models like GPT-5.4-mini and GPT-5.4-nano.

title: SHERLOC: Structured Diagnostic Localization for Code Repair Agents
blurb: SHERLOC is a training-free framework that pairs a reasoning LLM with compact repository tools and self-recovery to localize faults for code repair agents, without fine-tuning or multi-agent orchestration. It reaches 84.33% accuracy@1 on SWE-Bench Lite and 81.27% recall@1 on SWE-Bench Verified, and feeding its locations and diagnostic findings into repair agents lifts resolve rate by 5.95 pp while cutting total tokens by 23.1%.

title: Agentic Generation of AST Transformation Rules for Fixing Breaking Updates
blurb: BigBag is an agentic framework that generates reusable AST transformation rules to fix breaking dependency updates, encoding repair logic at the API level so a single transformation transfers to any client broken by the same update. Evaluated on 157 compilation failures from the BUMP benchmark, its best configuration reached a 94.3% compilable transformation rate, a 78.6% fix rate, and a 33.3% cross-project fix rate.

title: U.S. Presses Meta to Agree to A.I. Reviews
blurb: Federal officials are pressing Meta to permit government safety evaluations of its AI models, leaving it the only major lab that has not agreed to such reviews. The push follows an order weeks earlier that forced Anthropic to pull its latest model.

title: N.S.A. Lost Access to Powerful A.I. Model Amid Anthropic Dispute
blurb: The N.S.A. temporarily lost access to a powerful Anthropic AI model used for cybersecurity work amid an ongoing dispute between the company and the Trump administration. The episode highlights the government's growing reliance on frontier AI systems for security operations even as it remains in conflict with one of the leading U.S. developers behind those tools.

title: Nobel laureate John Jumper is leaving DeepMind for rival Anthropic
blurb: Nobel laureate John Jumper is leaving Google DeepMind for Anthropic after nearly nine years leading the AlphaFold team. His exit follows Character AI co-founder Noam Shazeer's departure from DeepMind to OpenAI this week, marking back-to-back losses of senior researchers to rival labs.

title: Decentralised AI Training and Inference with BlockTrain
blurb: Spheroid BlockTrain is a decentralized training protocol that partitions a model into independently trainable blocks, each optimized on a local objective derived from the same global target and composed into one model at inference. On byte-level WikiText it reaches cross entropy 1.359, within about 0.04 CE of an end-to-end Transformer baseline, while each worker trains only one block and avoids full-model optimizer state.

title: Poisoned Playbooks: Demystifying Knowledge Poisoning Effects on AI Security Agents
blurb: This paper studies how a single poisoned security write-up injected into RAG knowledge sources can systematically alter the behavior of AI security agents, turning retrieved misinformation into incorrect exploit actions. Across 11 CTF challenges, 3 frontier LLM families, and 11 real-world CVEs, the authors introduce a Verification Boundary classification and show that verification prompting and multi-source retrieval help only when strong refuting evidence exists, weakening under sparse-evidence and zero-day conditions.

title: InSight: Self-Guided Skill Acquisition via Steerable VLAs
blurb: InSight is a framework that lets vision-language-action robot models autonomously acquire new manipulation skills by making them steerable at the primitive-action level, such as "lift upward" or "pour the bottle." A VLM-guided data flywheel identifies missing primitives for a novel task, attempts and labels its own demonstrations, and integrates the successful ones, learning skills like block flipping, sweeping, and twisting without human demonstrations of the target tasks.

title: Elevated error rate across multiple models
blurb: Anthropic reported an elevated error rate affecting multiple Claude models, according to an incident posted to its status page. The disruption underscores how dependent agentic coding tools and production AI workflows have become on a handful of model providers, where a single provider's degraded API can stall downstream applications until the incident is resolved.

title: The Serialized Bridge: Understanding and Recovering LLM Serving Performance under Blackwell GPU Confidential Computing
blurb: This paper finds that LLM serving under Intel TDX plus NVIDIA Blackwell GPU Confidential Computing loses 13-27% of throughput even though BF16 matmul on a B300 runs at 0.998x of non-confidential speed. The authors trace the gap to the confidential VM-GPU bridge serializing host/device transfers, and show that a scheduling flag plus a worker-thread drain recover up to 92% of the lost performance in high-concurrency runs.

title: How we built an internal data analytics agent
blurb: GitHub built Qubot, an internal Copilot-powered analytics agent that lets any employee query the company's data warehouse in plain language and get answers within seconds. Accessible through Slack, VS Code, and the Copilot CLI, it spawns a Copilot Cloud Agent per question, targets exploratory analysis rather than dashboards, and stores each result as a markdown report in a pull request for reuse.

title: Posterior Refinement: Fast Language Generation via Any-Order Flow Maps
blurb: FMLM+ is a non-autoregressive language generation framework that adds masking-style noise schedules to Flow Map Language Models, letting it produce a full sequence in one step while scoring each token's global consistency after the fact. The authors use this for Posterior Refinement, an inference-time strategy that self-corrects outputs and matches discrete diffusion baselines with 32x fewer function evaluations, improving the speed-quality tradeoff over both MDM and FMLM families.

title: Lift4D: Harmonizing Single-View 3D Estimation for 4D Reconstruction In-the-Wild
blurb: Lift4D is a test-time optimization framework that reconstructs complete dynamic 3D objects from monocular video, combining temporally consistent per-frame predictions with deformable 3D Gaussian Splatting. It adapts a single-view reconstruction model via causal latent conditioning for initialization, then sculpts the representation through occlusion-aware optimization that recovers visible surfaces and completes unseen regions using a view-conditioned diffusion prior.

title: DifyTap Bugs Let Attackers 'Wiretap' AI Chat Histories
blurb: Security firm Zafran disclosed DifyTap, a set of four vulnerabilities in Dify, the popular open-source platform for building and managing AI applications, that let attackers wiretap private chat histories and exfiltrate data across tenants. The most severe flaw, a Plugin Daemon path traversal bug rated CVSS 9.4, has been merged on GitHub, and all four are addressed in the latest build.

title: Securing LLM-Agent Long-Term Memory Against Poisoning: Non-Malleable, Origin-Bound Authority with Machine-Checked Guarantees
blurb: This paper formalizes memory poisoning in LLM agents, where an attacker stores untrusted content in one session to steer a future action like a payment or data exfiltration. It proves that content- and lineage-based defenses fail under laundering, then introduces TMA-NM, a non-malleable information-flow control scheme that reaches 0% attack success across eight frontier models at full legitimate utility.

title: Meta ships first smart glasses powered by its Superintelligence Labs’ Muse Spark model
blurb: Meta partnered with EssilorLuxottica to launch a new line of AI smart glasses starting at $299, about $80 below the last-gen Ray-Ban Meta line, powered by Muse Spark, the first multimodal reasoning model from Meta Superintelligence Labs. The glasses run live translation across 14 new languages, offer an eight-hour battery, and will add turn-by-turn pedestrian navigation that speaks directions aloud.

title: Why American data centers can't plug in
blurb: This piece argues that the main bottleneck to America's AI buildout isn't a shortage of electricity but the backlogged process of connecting new data centers and power plants to the grid. The median power plant waited under 20 months for interconnection in 2005 but 55 months by 2023, a delay the author attributes to rigid first-come, first-served queues that the AI era will force grid operators to overhaul.

title: Using Codex for Long-Running Projects
blurb: This OpenAI guide covers techniques for using Codex on long-running, multi-step projects rather than one-off prompts. It walks through scoping work into persistent tasks, structuring context so agents maintain state across sessions, and setting up workflows that let Codex continue making progress over extended periods without constant supervision.

title: Sources: Qualcomm is in talks to design custom chips for ByteDance, based partly on connectivity tech from Alphawave Semi, which Qualcomm acquired in 2025 (Reuters)
blurb: Qualcomm is in talks to design custom chips for ByteDance, drawing partly on connectivity technology from Alphawave Semi, which Qualcomm acquired in 2025. The deal would extend Qualcomm's push beyond smartphone modems into custom silicon for large data-center customers, deepening its competition with Broadcom and Marvell for AI infrastructure business.

title: Unsloth GLM-5.2 – How to Run Locally
blurb: Unsloth released Dynamic GGUF quantizations of Z.ai's GLM-5.2, a 744B-parameter mixture-of-experts model with 40B active parameters and a 1M-token context window that can run locally. The 2-bit quant fits in 245GB of total memory and retains about 82% accuracy while being 84% smaller, letting the model run on a 256GB unified-memory Mac or a single 24GB GPU with RAM offloading.

title: AWS enters the context layer race with a graph that learns from agents, not manual curation
blurb: AWS announced a context intelligence stack for AI agents, centered on AWS Context, a knowledge graph service that automatically maps relationships across existing data and refines itself based on how agents query it. The launch also includes general availability of Amazon S3 Annotations and a preview of skill assets in AWS Glue Data Catalog, with all metadata published in Apache Iceberg format and queries inheriting IAM and Lake Formation permissions.

title: Red-Teaming after Mythos — Zico Kolter & Matt Fredrikson, Gray Swan
blurb: Gray Swan cofounders Zico Kolter and Matt Fredrikson argue that AI security is a distinct discipline from traditional cybersecurity, because agents like Codex and Claude Code introduce a new exploit class through indirect prompt injection. They detail their red-teaming stack, including Shade, a tool Anthropic used to test model robustness, and explain why frontier models do not get automatically safer as they scale and why specialized red-teaming models can now beat humans at breaking AI systems.

title: Anthropic’s Claude Tag is learning your company, one Slack message at a time
blurb: Anthropic introduced Claude Tag in research preview, an always-on Slack teammate that learns from a channel over time and can be tagged to answer questions or assigned tasks it breaks into stages. Available to Claude Enterprise and Team customers, each Claude identity stays scoped to admin-defined channels and offers an ambient mode that proactively flags items and follows up on forgotten threads.
