Genomic foundation models like Evo 2, Genos, and DeepMind's AlphaGenome sidestep that complexity by learning correlations from known cases rather than mechanisms. The piece contends this will likely prove useful for prediction while leaving the deeper question of how the genome works inside a computational black box.

title: Agon: An Autonomous Large-Scale Omnidisciplinary Research System Built on Prompt Economy
blurb: Agon is a research orchestrator that validates checkable claims inside its workflow and leaves the remaining judgments to human scientists, built on six principles including Prompt Economy, Massive Parallelism, and Zero-Code. The authors ran it for 444 Prompt Economy loops across domains using only small starting topics and no human-written experimental code, then organized the resulting failures into a taxonomy by severity, fixability, visibility, and capability locus.

title: On the Semantics of Generative SPARQL
blurb: This paper extends SPARQL with GenOp, a generative query construct that calls a language model and returns typed solution mappings while preserving the fixed-dataset assumption underlying SPARQL's formal semantics. The authors define a compatibility relation supporting similarity-based matching, characterize acyclic and stratified fragments with fixpoint semantics, and show that data and combined complexity coincide with standard SPARQL.

title: GitHub joins coalition advocating for fixes to California AI Transparency Act to protect open source
blurb: GitHub joined a coalition with Black Forest Labs, Hugging Face, and Mozilla calling for targeted amendments to California's AI Transparency Act (SB 942, as proposed to be amended in SB 1000). The group argues the bill's license revocation provisions conflict with the perpetual, irrevocable nature of open source licenses, and proposes aligning with the EU AI Act's transparency approach instead.

title: When I reject AI code even if it works
blurb: The author argues that as AI accelerates implementation, the real bottleneck shifts to reviewing code the developer never reasoned through, producing cognitive overload that makes him reject working changes and start over. He rejects AI code when he can't explain the approach in his own words, when the diff exceeds the problem, or when it adds unproven abstractions, advocating mandatory human review alongside AI checks.

title: Do AI Assistants Actually Render Your JavaScript when Grounding? We Put It to the Test
blurb: A test of 12 AI assistants found that most read only raw HTML and miss JavaScript-rendered content when grounding on a pasted link.

title: 🎙️ How I AI: How to write AI agent loops in Claude Code and Codex + How Claude Mythos found a 15-year-old bug in Mozilla Firefox
blurb: A How I AI tutorial breaking down heartbeats, crons, hooks, and goal-based agent loops, with real automations built in Claude Code and Codex.

title: Anthropic launches Claude Tag enterprise collaborative tool for agentic workflows
blurb: Anthropic launched Claude Tag, a beta tool for Enterprise and Team customers that lets people delegate tasks to Claude by tagging it as a team member in Slack channels. Described as an evolution of Claude Code, the tool builds context from the channels it joins, can schedule and automate tasks, and replaces the existing Claude in Slack integration on August 3.

title: SpaceX to buy Cursor AI coding agent operator Anysphere for $60B
blurb: SpaceX agreed to acquire Anysphere, the company behind the Cursor AI coding agent, for $60B. The deal would fold one of the most widely used AI coding tools into Elon Musk's space and satellite empire, signaling SpaceX's push to build out internal AI software capabilities and developer infrastructure.

title: Report: GKE Inference Gateway delivers up to 92% faster AI responses
blurb: Google's GKE Inference Gateway uses prefix caching and model-aware routing to direct LLM requests to accelerators already holding the relevant KV cache in memory, skipping recomputation of repeated prompt prefixes. An independent benchmark found it delivered 15.7% higher throughput, 92.8% shorter wait times, and 62.6% lower inter-token latency than the next leading managed Kubernetes service, with Snap reporting prefix cache hit rates of 75-80%.

title: Stripe and AWS enable AI agent payments for content owners and publishers
blurb: Stripe and AWS introduced infrastructure that lets AI agents make payments on behalf of users while compensating the content owners and publishers whose material the agents access. The integration combines Stripe's payment rails with AWS Bedrock and AgentCore, giving publishers a way to meter and monetize agent traffic rather than have their content scraped for free.

title: Evals: the strategic IP that will define the next era of AI
blurb: Garrett Lord argues that internal evaluations, not models, are becoming the strategic IP that will determine which companies win the next era of AI. Drawing on conversations with hundreds of execs frustrated by weak AI ROI, he contends that proprietary evals encoding a company's quality standards are what turn general-purpose models into reliable, differentiated production systems.

title: Blackstone plans $30B for Japan AI data centres, and is unbothered by bubble talk
blurb: Blackstone plans to invest roughly $30B in AI data centres in Japan over the next three to five years, targeting facilities with combined capacity exceeding one gigawatt. President Jonathan Gray dismissed bubble warnings, arguing the risk of building too little compute outweighs the risk of building too much, and said the firm would also accelerate its Japanese private-equity push after raising $13.1B for its largest-ever Asia fund.

title: QC-SMOTE: Quality-Controlled SMOTE for Imbalanced Classification
blurb: QC-SMOTE is a quality-controlled oversampling framework for imbalanced classification that scores minority-sample reliability using local density, safe-level, and isolation from the majority class, then generates synthetic points with an IPQ-guided best-of-K strategy that checks midpoint purity. Across 30 datasets, it posts the strongest average AUC-ROC and Macro F1 among compared methods, with the clearest gains under moderate and severe imbalance.

title: IV-CoT: Implicit Visual Chain-of-Thought for Structure-Aware Text-to-Image Generation
blurb: IV-CoT is a latent visual reasoning framework that improves structure-aware text-to-image generation by splitting visual conditioning into a structural-to-semantic cascade, where structural queries first form a latent plan and semantic queries then render appearance on top of it. The method uses training-only sketch supervision to guide the structural queries, runs implicit chain-of-thought in a single forward pass, and beats prior approaches on GenEval and T2I-CompBench.

title: The African Language Tax: Quantifying the Cost, Latency, and Context Penalty of Tokenizing African Languages in Frontier LLMs
blurb: This paper measures the "tokenization tax" that frontier LLMs impose on 20 African languages, showing every one carries a premium above English, with a median 1.88x on GPT-5's o200k_base and up to 8.92x for N'Ko. Since models bill, scale latency, and budget context per token, this translates to as much as 8.9x inference cost and as little as 11% of English's effective context window, and the authors release afri-fertility, a public leaderboard, and a results dataset.

title: 21,000 Oracle jobs vanish amid Big Red's big bets on AI
blurb: Oracle's headcount fell from 162,000 to 141,000 over the past year, a cut of about 21,000 jobs, even as the company pours billions into datacenter expansion. The spending and the layoffs point the same direction: Oracle is reallocating payroll into AI compute capacity, betting that GPUs and datacenters generate more return than the workers it shed. For founders, it's the clearest signal yet that incumbents will trade headcount for capex when they think AI infrastructure is the higher-leverage bet.

title: The minimum viable unit of saleable software
blurb: LLMs flipped the buy-vs-build calculus, so the cheap internal package that replaces your $400/mo SaaS is now a real threat to anyone selling software. But cheap isn't zero, and the author's point is that LLM-built systems still demand dozens of feedback loops to get right plus an open-ended maintenance bill for every new feature and bug. The opening for saleable software is the work LLMs make easier but not free, where the ongoing cost of owning the thing yourself still outweighs the price of buying it.

title: Trump says he no longer views Anthropic as a national security threat after G7 meeting with CEO
blurb: Trump told Axios he no longer sees Anthropic as a national security threat and may ease the June 12 Commerce directive forcing the company to seek US approval before foreign nationals can touch its Fable 5 and Mythos 5 models. The reversal came days after a G7 meeting with Dario Amodei, capping a three-month standoff that started when the Pentagon flagged Anthropic as a supply-chain risk over its refusal to strip military guardrails. The thaw lands right as Anthropic sits on a confidential IPO filing, where regulatory overhang is exactly the kind of risk that scares public-market buyers.

title: [AINews] Claude Tag: Multiplayer, Proactive, Persistent Agents in Slack
blurb: Anthropic launched Claude Tag, a Slack-native way to delegate work to Claude as if it were a teammate, with access to chosen channels, tools, and codebases. You tag it into a thread to hand off a task, and it can pull in the coworker who owns related code, wait on git webhooks for days to clear blocking dependencies, and summarize threads into docs with action items. In ambient mode it watches channels untagged, syncs information across them, and triggers on thresholds to investigate when something breaks.

title: Nvidia backs AI networking switch silicon startup Upscale in $190M raise
blurb: Upscale AI raised $190M to build networking switch silicon aimed only at AI workloads, bringing total funding to $500M and its valuation to $2B barely a year after launching. The round was led by Premji Invest, but the notable name is Nvidia, which joined the extension even as it sells its own AI networking gear. A frontier supplier funding a startup that competes in part of its own stack signals how acute the cluster-scale networking bottleneck has become, and how much room there still is below the incumbents.

title: AI's Affordability Crisis
blurb: The economics under AI usage are upside down: a $200-a-month subscription lets a heavy user burn roughly $8,000 in Anthropic tokens or $14,000 in OpenAI tokens, meaning the labs subsidize some enterprise customers by 40 to 70 times. Estimates put the platforms at spending $8 to $14 to generate every $1 of revenue, and one analysis found gross margins go negative once a user touches just 25% of their rate limit. The demand is real because the price is fake, and that gap is being filled by equity raises rather than working economics.

title: U.S. Presses Meta to Agree to A.I. Reviews
blurb: Federal officials are pressing Meta to accept government safety evaluations of its AI models, leaving it the last major lab to refuse a review regime the others have already accepted. The move comes weeks after regulators forced Anthropic to pull its latest model, a signal that the government now expects pre-release oversight as the cost of operating at the frontier. For founders building on these labs, the supplier you depend on is becoming a regulated entity whose release timeline answers to Washington, not just its own roadmap.

title: Sources: Qualcomm is in talks to design custom chips for ByteDance, based partly on connectivity tech from Alphawave Semi, which Qualcomm acquired in 2025 (Reuters)
blurb: Qualcomm is in talks to design custom chips for ByteDance, drawing partly on connectivity tech from Alphawave Semi, the chip-IP firm it acquired in 2025. The move pushes Qualcomm deeper into custom silicon for a single large customer, a hedge against its smartphone-modem business as Apple builds its own. For founders, it signals where the chip market is heading: the biggest buyers increasingly want bespoke designs, and the established players are reorganizing through acquisition to supply them rather than sell off-the-shelf parts.

title: Algorithmic Monocultures in Hiring
blurb: Ninety percent of US employers now run candidates through AI screening tools, and most rely on the same few third-party vendors. A new Stanford study following 3.4 million applicants found that when one algorithm screens for many employers, the same people get rejected everywhere they apply, and 40,000 more Black and Asian applications would have advanced if the AI recommended them at the top group's rate. The monoculture turns one vendor's bias into a systemic lockout.

title: Anthropic Customer Sues US Over Loss of Fable AI Access
blurb: Export controls now reach inside your engineering org, not just the chip supply chain. Legion, a US litigation-tech company, sued the federal government after Anthropic disabled its top models, Fable 5 and Mythos 5, to comply with a Trump administration order keeping the technology away from foreign nationals. Legion's problem is that some of its developers are Canadians working from Canada, which the rule appears to cut off. The signal for founders is that where your team sits and who you hire can now determine which frontier models you are legally allowed to run.

title: SK Hynix overtaking Samsung to become South Korea's most valuable company is the culmination of 14 years of bets on HBM, which paid off as the AI boom took off (Reuters)
blurb: SK Hynix passed Samsung to become South Korea's most valuable company, the payoff on a 14-year bet on high-bandwidth memory that few rivals took seriously until the AI boom made HBM the bottleneck for every GPU. The win went to the supplier that committed early to a niche, not the incumbent with the broader product line. For founders, it is a reminder that the durable advantage often comes from a contrarian bet placed years before the market proves you right.

title: After betting the firm on Anthropic, Menlo Ventures raises victorious $3B fund
blurb: Menlo Ventures raised $3B, the largest fund in its 50-year history, on the back of an AI portfolio whose Anthropic stake alone is now worth around $14 billion. The win traces to a 2024 bet-the-firm move, when Menlo led Anthropic's Series D with $750M, structuring about $500M of it as an SPV at a time when almost no one was writing nine-figure checks. AI SPVs are commonplace now, with Anthropic such a frequent target that it has called unauthorized ones scams, but Menlo's authorized early position is what funded this raise.

title: SK Hynix says it is seeking to raise ~$29.4B in a US listing, expects trading to start on July 10, and intends to use the funds for building additional capacity (Bloomberg)
blurb: SK Hynix is seeking to raise about $29.4B in a US listing, with trading set to begin July 10 and the proceeds earmarked for additional manufacturing capacity. A raise that size signals memory makers expect AI demand to keep outrunning supply, and they are committing capex years ahead of the orders. For founders building on AI infrastructure, it is another sign that the compute and memory bottleneck is being treated as structural, not a passing spike.

title: SpaceX Strikes $6 Billion Deal With AI Startup for Data-Center Space
blurb: SpaceX signed a $6B deal to rent data-center capacity to Reflection AI, which will use it to train open-source models. The move puts a rocket company in the compute-landlord business and gives a young lab access to scale without building its own infrastructure. It is another sign that the AI buildout is being financed through long-term capacity contracts, where whoever controls power and data-center space increasingly sets the terms for the labs renting it.

title: Micron invests in Anthropic and grants it a supply deal
blurb: Micron invested in Anthropic's Series H round and signed a multi-year deal to supply it with HBM, DRAM, and SSDs, with Samsung and SK Hynix also joining the round. The flow runs backwards from the usual buyer-supplier relationship: the memory vendor is putting capital into its own customer to lock in the relationship as Anthropic scales compute toward an IPO. Securing memory allocation, not just GPUs, is now a frontier-lab bottleneck, and suppliers are paying for a seat at the table rather than waiting to be paid.

title: ByteDance Seeks $20 Billion in Its Largest-Ever Global Loan
blurb: ByteDance is in early talks with banks for a roughly $20B offshore loan, the largest in its history, as it ramps up AI investment. The new-money facility would carry a three-year term with an option to extend to five, though how the funds get deployed isn't yet clear. The TikTok parent borrowing at this scale signals that the capital intensity of frontier AI now reaches even the most cash-generative private companies, which are turning to debt rather than draining reserves to fund the compute race.

title: Drones startup Stark confirms €500m raise backed by Sequoia and Founders Fund
blurb: Berlin-based Stark confirmed a €500m raise to scale European defence manufacturing, backed by Sequoia, Founders Fund, the NATO Innovation Fund, and Advent. More than 80% of the capital goes straight into R&D and production, including new electronic warfare facilities and a target of thousands of drone systems per month. Founded in 2024 and already past unicorn status, Stark has signed a €268m German contract and expanded into the UK and Sweden. The signal for founders is that European defence has become a venture category that scales like software, with frontier funds writing nine-figure checks.

title: Sources: prices for Nvidia's AI chips on China's black market have more than doubled amid a US export crackdown; its flagship DGX B300 server has risen to $1.1M (Eleanor Olcott/Financial Times)
blurb: Nvidia's top AI chips are now selling for more than double on China's black market, with the flagship DGX B300 server reaching $1.1M as the US export crackdown tightens. The premium is the clearest signal yet that demand inside China is running far ahead of what sanctioned supply can meet. For founders building on scarce compute, it is a reminder that chip access, not model quality, is becoming the binding constraint, and that geography increasingly determines who can afford to train at the frontier.

title: SpaceX raises $25 billion in debt sale less than two weeks after IPO
blurb: SpaceX raised $25 billion in senior unsecured notes less than two weeks after an IPO that already netted nearly $86 billion, drawing close to $90 billion in orders across tranches maturing from 2031 to 2056. The company now sits on more than $100 billion in cash despite $41.3 billion in accumulated losses, with Starlink the only profitable unit. The capital backs Starship, Starlink, and a widening AI push, including revamped Grok models and a $60 billion all-stock acquisition of coding startup Cursor, signaling that the largest private bets now run on public-market-scale balance sheets.

title: Alphabet added to Dow Jones Industrial Average, replacing Verizon
blurb: Alphabet will replace Verizon in the Dow Jones Industrial Average before Monday's open, joining Nvidia, Amazon, Apple, and Microsoft in the 30-stock index. The blue-chip average that once tracked industrial America is now a concentrated bet on AI, cloud, and advertising, with a handful of mega-caps setting its direction. The swap lands as Alphabet pours capital into the theme, raising $141 billion in debt and equity since October to prove its vertically integrated AI stack can earn a return, even after closing its worst trading day in more than a year on Monday.

title: Anthropic Backer Menlo Ventures Raises $3B In New Funds To Back AI Startups Across Stages
blurb: Menlo Ventures raised $3B across two AI funds, the largest raise in its 50-year history, split between a seed and Series A vehicle and a Series B-and-later growth fund. The firm is leaning entirely on its early Anthropic bet, which it made in 2023 when the company was pre-product and pre-revenue and which now anchors a portfolio whose lead holding just hit a $965B valuation. With Anthropic targeting a $1T-plus IPO in 2026, Menlo is raising against a single position that could become its largest exit by far.

title: Publicité : WALMART rachète VIBE pour 1,2 milliard d’euros afin d’accélérer sa riposte face à Amazon
blurb: Walmart is buying French adtech company Vibe.co for around $1.4B to accelerate its ad business against Amazon, two years after picking up Vizio for $2.3B. The strategy is to turn transactional data from hundreds of millions of shoppers into ad inventory through Walmart Connect, and to plant a flag in connected TV, which now accounts for nearly 45% of US television viewing. The real fight between the retail giants has moved off the shelves and onto the screen, where Amazon Ads already pulls in tens of billions a year.

title: Humanoid robot maker Agility plans to go public via a merger with Michael Klein's SPAC, in a deal valuing it at ~$2.5B and listing under ticker symbol AGLT (Lauren Thomas/Wall Street Journal)
blurb: Agility Robotics agreed to go public through a merger with Michael Klein's SPAC, a deal valuing the humanoid maker at roughly $2.5B and listing it under the ticker AGLT. The move pulls a still-pre-revenue category onto public markets while private capital for hard tech stays expensive and slow to exit. Klein is the dealmaker behind the Churchill SPACs and the Lucid Motors listing, so a marquee sponsor is again betting that humanoids reach commercial scale before the financing math turns against them.

title: India’s MoEngage bets that the future of marketing is millions of AI agents
blurb: MoEngage acquired San Francisco-based Aampe in an all-cash deal worth tens of millions, betting that marketing's future is a dedicated AI agent for every customer rather than audience segments and campaign rules. Aampe grew ARR 150% over the past year and shares enterprise customers like Swiggy and Grab with its new parent. The strategic read is migration: MoEngage already wins multimillion-dollar deals off Salesforce Marketing Cloud and Adobe Experience Cloud, and per-customer agents are the wedge it thinks pulls more of those accounts off the incumbents.

title: Meta announces new smart glasses starting at $299, as Zuckerberg keeps pushing wearables
blurb: Meta released a $299 smart glasses model, at least $80 below its second-gen Ray-Ban entry price, dropping the Ray-Ban and Oakley branding while keeping the EssilorLuxottica partnership. Meta and EssilorLuxottica already own more than 80% of a still-small market, and the cheaper, screenless camera-and-speaker device is a deliberate step toward lens-display computing like the $799 Ray-Ban Display. The contest is now forming around price tiers, as Google builds Gemini eyewear with Warby Parker and Snap pitches $2,195 Specs as the smartphone's successor.

title: AI memory startup focused on cutting token costs raises $98 million
blurb: Engram, an 8-month-old startup that builds a "learned memory" layer for AI, raised $98 million from General Catalyst, Kleiner Perkins, Sequoia, and Andrej Karpathy. It recalls organization-specific workflows and context to match or beat frontier labs using up to 100 times fewer tokens, and already counts Microsoft, Notion, and Harvey as clients. The bet runs against the old assumption that scale lowers cost, because newer models keep getting pricier, and the money is moving to the layer that makes existing models cheaper rather than the labs racing to build bigger ones.

title: SpaceX signs computing power deal with open-source AI startup Reflection worth up to $6.3 billion
blurb: SpaceX signed a computing deal with open-source startup Reflection worth up to $6.3 billion, giving the $25 billion lab immediate access to Nvidia GB300s for $150 million a month through 2029. The deal turns Colossus, built to train Musk's Grok, into a compute landlord that already counts Anthropic, Google, and Cursor as customers. The timing matters: open-source momentum has spiked since Anthropic cut off Fable and Mythos, and Reflection is betting that governments and enterprises wary of closed-model lock-in will pay for models they can inspect, customize, and run themselves.

title: Superhuman acquires AI detection startup GPTZero, which has 19M+ registered users and $30M in annual recurring revenue; PitchBook: GPTZero is valued at $88M+ (Geoff Weiss/Business Insider)
blurb: Superhuman acquired GPTZero, the AI-detection startup that built 19M+ registered users and $30M in annual recurring revenue against a PitchBook valuation north of $88M. The deal pairs an email client with a tool for spotting machine-written text, a signal that AI detection is becoming a feature inside larger workflows rather than a standalone product. For founders, it marks how quickly defensive AI tooling is consolidating into the platforms where the writing actually happens.

title: 7 Mistakes Founders Make When Chasing Their First Deals
blurb: Most early founders stall on the same two mistakes: waiting for a perfect product before selling, and pricing themselves into irrelevance. Selling is a contact sport, so the faster move is to ship a hypothesis about a real problem and let customer conversations correct your aim, because early signals are what get you to product-market fit. On price, charging one or two thousand a month is pocket change to a serious buyer. If the number is that small, run it as a design partnership and charge real money once you have proof.

title: Oracle shed about 20k roles globally in the last year
blurb: Oracle cut about 21,000 roles in the past year, roughly 13% of its workforce, ending May 2026 with 141,000 full-time staff after explicitly tying the reductions to AI deployment across its operations. The move cost $1.8B in severance and restructuring, nearly five times the prior year's bill, as the company pours at least $50B into data centers for customers like OpenAI and Meta. More than 100,000 tech workers have been laid off in the past year, and the pattern is consistent: the same firms funding the AI buildout are shrinking headcount to pay for it.

title: World model maker Odyssey nabs $1.45B valuation backed by Amazon and other big names
blurb: Odyssey, a world model startup founded by self-driving veterans Oliver Cameron and Jeff Hawke, raised a $310M Series B at a $1.45B valuation, led by Natural Capital with Amazon, AMD Ventures, and GV in. World models gather data from the physical world and simulate it with accurate physics, the bet for what comes after text and chat, and Odyssey already ships models for video-game creation, robotics, and interactive video from prompts. Part of the deal is strategic: AWS becomes its preferred cloud and Odyssey will optimize for Amazon's Trainium chips, a direct shot at Nvidia.

title: Legion LegalTech sues US over Anthropic Fable 5 and Mythos 5 shutdown
blurb: Legion LegalTech sued the federal government over a June 12 Commerce Department directive that forced Anthropic to disable Fable 5 and Mythos 5 for every customer worldwide. The order barred access for any foreign national, and because Anthropic could not verify nationality in real time, the only way to comply was to shut both models off for everyone the same day. Legion, an American legal-tech firm whose dev team includes Canadians working from Canada, calls the harm "immediate, irreparable, and existential," a reminder that a single export ruling can vaporize a startup's core dependency overnight.

title: SpaceX Sells $25 Billion of Bonds, Cuts Interest Costs
blurb: SpaceX sold $25B of investment-grade bonds on Tuesday, the final move to refinance the expensive debt behind Musk's X acquisition and the loans and bonds xAI issued to cover its cash burn. The deal folds the riskiest parts of Musk's empire into SpaceX's far cheaper credit, lowering interest costs across the group. The signal for founders is that a single anchor asset with strong cash flow can underwrite the rest of a portfolio, turning balance-sheet strength into a financing weapon rivals can't match.

title: Building a ‘digital twin’ 10,000 feet underground: PNNL, Nvidia and Fervo team up on geothermal AI
blurb: Geothermal just got a serious compute backer: PNNL, Nvidia, and Fervo are building a publicly available digital twin that models reservoirs 10,000 feet down to optimize how operators drill, monitor, and inject water. Today's models are too slow to guide real-time decisions, which leaves heat in the ground and capacity stranded. The bet matters because data centers are starving for clean baseload power, and Fervo, fresh off $2.17B raised, is bringing a 500-megawatt Utah plant online this year while Nvidia supplies the infrastructure and Fervo the proprietary field data.

title: SK Hynix Seeks $29 Billion With US Listing to Fund AI Boom
blurb: SK Hynix is planning to raise $29.4B in a US listing, one of the largest share sales ever and comparable to Saudi Aramco's 2019 record IPO. The money funds a rush to expand memory chip capacity as the AI supply chain strains to keep up with demand. The signal for founders is that the AI buildout has moved past chips into the components that feed them, and the capital chasing that capacity now rivals the biggest offerings in market history.

title: SpaceX lands $6.3B compute deal with open-source AI startup Reflection and Nvidia is on both sides of the trade
blurb: SpaceX just became one of the largest compute landlords in AI, signing Reflection AI to a $6.3B deal that pays $150M a month for Nvidia GB300 chips at its Colossus 2 site through 2029. That pushes SpaceX's committed compute revenue from outside clients past $80B, alongside Anthropic, Google, and the Cursor team it is acquiring for $60B. Nvidia sits on both sides of the trade, selling the GPUs that fill Colossus and holding a stake in the labs renting them, which is how the same chips get paid for twice.

title: GEO is following the same path as early SEO
blurb: GEO, the practice of getting your brand mentioned in AI-generated answers, is running the same playbook early SEO did, and the same shakeout is coming. The durable tactics are the boring ones: high-quality content that answers real prompts, FAQs, TL;DR summaries, question-based headlines, and clean schema. The black hat shortcuts that work right now, like AI spam, fake reviews to game E-E-A-T, and cloaking content for crawlers, are the ones SEO history says get penalized out of existence. Build for the version of GEO that survives the cleanup, not the gold rush that funds it today.

title: Cerebras falls 10% after chipmaker forecasts shrinking margin in first earnings report since IPO
blurb: Cerebras nearly doubled revenue to $193.4M in its first earnings report since May's IPO, yet the stock fell 10% after the company guided core gross margin down to 36-38% in Q2 from 46.5%. The market is signaling that for AI infrastructure plays, margin trajectory now outweighs raw growth, even when revenue is climbing 92% year over year. Cerebras is spending hard to challenge Nvidia and feed deals like its $20B OpenAI supply agreement, and the cost of buying that scale is showing up in the numbers before the payoff does.

title: Tesla plans to sell modular AI data center hardware called ‘Megapod'
blurb: Tesla filed an intent-to-use trademark for "Megapod," a self-contained modular AI data center system bundling servers, networking, power distribution, and cooling into a single turnkey unit. The move comes less than a year after Tesla killed Dojo, its only in-house AI training computer, and pushes it into a market Nvidia already dominates with the GB200 NVL72 and DGX SuperPOD, the reference designs Dell and Supermicro both build on. Tesla would be selling the rack-and-room around chips it doesn't make, against the company whose silicon powers essentially all of it.

title: Agility, Maker of Humanlike Robots, to Go Public in $2.5 Billion SPAC Deal
blurb: Agility Robotics, maker of the Digit humanoid robot already working in Amazon warehouses, agreed to go public through a SPAC deal valuing it at $2.5B. The listing makes Agility one of the first humanoid-robot companies to reach public markets, putting hard revenue and deployment numbers in front of investors who have so far funded the category on promise alone. Public scrutiny will test whether warehouse and manufacturing pilots are turning into durable contracts, and the valuation sets a reference point every other humanoid startup raising private money will now be measured against.

title: MoEngage buys Aampe to bet enterprise marketing’s future belongs to per-customer AI agents, not segments
blurb: MoEngage acquired Aampe in an all-cash deal worth tens of millions, betting that enterprise marketing moves from segment-based campaigns to a per-customer model where every user is shadowed by a dedicated AI agent. Aampe's agents test thousands of message variants in parallel, deciding push, in-app, and email timing per user instead of running cohort A/B tests, and its tech already runs at Swiggy, Grab, and Taxfix. The wedge is against Salesforce Marketing Cloud and Adobe Experience Cloud, where MoEngage has been winning mid-contract migrations on multi-million-dollar deals while both incumbents still sell the segmentation model this thesis discards.

title: The Fund That Backed Revolut at £300K. Now Raising $320M for What Comes Next
blurb: Seedcamp closed $320M for Fund VII, the largest raise in its nearly 20-year history, and published its full return data, something almost no European VC does. The numbers explain why LPs showed up: Fund III turned a €20M vintage that backed Revolut at a £300K pre-seed check into a 50.3% net IRR, has already returned 13x in distributions, and is tracking toward 20x net across 125 companies. The new fund leans into a physical AI thesis, a signal of where one of the continent's most consistent early-stage investors thinks the next decade of returns sits.

title: Mark Zuckerberg wants Meta to launch its own prediction market
blurb: Meta is building a standalone Polymarket-style prediction app, internally called "Arena," that Zuckerberg has greenlit as experimental but a top priority. The first version is points-based rather than real-money, more video game than betting platform, with cash possibly added later and Meta's social apps funneling users in. Prediction markets have pulled tens of billions in trading volume across Polymarket and Kalshi and drawn matching legal heat, from insider-trading cases to states suing over gambling laws. Meta is moving into a category that is growing fast and getting regulated in real time.

title: Backblaze gets $335 million CoreWeave bonanza
blurb: Backblaze signed a five-year, multi-exabyte deal with neocloud CoreWeave worth $335M to supply HDD-based storage tiers in CoreWeave's AI Object Storage. The deal tracks a quiet economic shift under the AI buildout: flash now runs about 10 times more expensive per terabyte than hard drives, and neoclouds storing data at exabyte scale can't sustain all-flash architectures. The labs and their suppliers are discovering that the unglamorous layer, cheap durable storage, is what keeps expensive compute from sitting idle, and the storage vendors are moving to claim it.

title: London-based Isometric, which develops AI agents that work with human verifiers to automate industrial certification, raised a €34M Series A led by AVP (David Cendon Garcia/EU-Startups)
blurb: Isometric, a London startup building AI agents that pair with human verifiers to automate industrial certification, raised a €34M Series A led by AVP. The bet is that compliance-heavy industries are a near-perfect fit for agents, since certification work is high-volume, rules-bound, and expensive to staff with experts. Keeping a human in the loop is the wedge that makes regulated buyers comfortable handing the repetitive verification to software.

title: My Vibe Coding Adventure, The App and the Experience, Ten Takeaways
blurb: Ben Thompson vibe-coded an app he actually plans to use daily and pulled ten lessons from the experience. The takeaway is that the value showed up not in a polished product but in building software tailored to one person's exact workflow, something that was never worth a developer's time before. The constraint has shifted from whether you can build the thing to whether you know precisely what you want it to do.

title: AppsFlyer Reportedly Lands $1B At $2.7B Valuation To Help Companies Track Digital Ads
blurb: AppsFlyer reportedly raised more than $1B at a $2.7B valuation, its first round since 2020 and a step the company says is on its path to the public markets. The telling detail is who wrote the checks: Moloco, Google, Meta, and Unity each took a minority stake, meaning the ad platforms being measured are now funding their own independent referee. As AI takes over how ads get bought and optimized, the attribution signals feeding those systems become infrastructure worth owning, which is why the measurement layer is suddenly drawing nine-figure rounds.

title: Superhuman acquires AI detection startup GPTZero
blurb: Superhuman acquired GPTZero, the three-year-old AI detection startup that hit $30M in ARR and more than 19 million registered users on just $13.5M raised. The deal is a reminder that capital efficiency still produces real outcomes, with a profitable company built from a Princeton senior thesis exiting on a fraction of the funding most startups burn before Series B. It also signals consolidation in AI detection, since Superhuman already shipped its own detector and is now buying a direct competitor rather than out-building it.

title: Bristol fusion startup raises £23M to produce cancer-fighting isotopes while the rest of the industry is still 20 years away
blurb: Bristol-based Astral Systems raised £23M in a Series A led by Mercia Ventures to scale production of medical isotopes for cancer diagnosis and treatment. While most fusion companies are still two decades from generating power, Astral already runs reactors and has booked more than £3M from research contracts, betting that revenue today beats the long wait for fusion electricity. The market is real and underserved, as more than 50 million nuclear medicine procedures a year depend on a fragile global isotope supply chain with no reliable UK domestic source.

title: Cerebras Drops After 2026 Sales Outlook Disappoints Investors
blurb: Cerebras shares fell about 10% after the newly public chipmaker issued a 2026 sales forecast that came in below what investors wanted. The problem isn't weak numbers, it's that Nvidia and a handful of AI chipmakers have trained Wall Street to expect every quarter to blow past estimates, so solid-but-unspectacular guidance now reads as a miss. For founders, it's a reminder that in a hyped category, beating your own plan isn't enough. You get priced against the fastest mover in the field, and merely good growth gets punished.

title: Sources: Zhipu is considering a multibillion-dollar Hong Kong share sale after its stock surged 2,000% since its January IPO, pushing its market cap above $128B (Dong Cao/Bloomberg)
blurb: Chinese AI lab Zhipu is weighing a multibillion-dollar Hong Kong share sale after its stock climbed 2,000% since January's IPO, lifting its market cap above $128B. The run-up signals how hungry public markets have become for Chinese frontier-model exposure, even as Western labs stay private and lean on venture money. For founders, it's a reminder that the AI funding map now has two distinct exit playbooks, and the one with the richest near-term liquidity is increasingly the one in Hong Kong.

title: Sequoia and Founders Fund just backed a drone startup whose drones failed: report
blurb: Stark, the Berlin maker of autonomous strike drones, raised €500 million at a valuation above €3.5 billion, roughly €200 million more than it sought, from Sequoia, Founders Fund, and the NATO Innovation Fund. The round closed months after the company's Virtus drones missed every target across four live-fire trials with British and German forces, one veering off course and catching fire. Investors wrote larger checks anyway, mirroring a German parliament that approved a €269 million Bundeswehr contract regardless. European defence startups pulled in a record $8.7 billion in 2025, and capital is no longer waiting for proof of concept.

title: SpaceX’s Shortest-Dated Debt Is Biggest Lure in $25 Billion Sale
blurb: SpaceX is raising $25B in its debut bond sale across five tranches, paying a relatively wide premium over Treasuries even after pulling in roughly $89B in demand. The shortest-dated notes drew the most interest, a sign that investors will lend to Musk's rocket-satellite-AI conglomerate but want to stay liquid rather than commit long. Coming weeks after the biggest-ever IPO, the deal shows the equity market's enthusiasm doesn't transfer cleanly to credit, where buyers price the actual cash flows and ask harder questions about a capital-hungry business.

title: Main Capital doubles down on enterprise software with €5.25 billion fund close
blurb: Main Capital Partners closed over €5.25 billion across two funds, more than double their predecessors, pushing the European software investor's assets under management past €12 billion. The raise lands in a market where venture's paper wealth far outruns its cash, yet Main pulled a re-up rate above 120% from existing LPs like Hamilton Lane plus new sovereign and pension money from the US, Asia, and the Middle East. The pitch is unfashionably plain: profitable, resilient lower mid-market software, a 4.7x weighted-average gross return across 38 exits, and a loss rate under 0.5%.

title: Lama AI raises $10M to accelerate automated loan originations
blurb: Lama AI raised $10M in a Series A led by EJF Ventures, bringing its total to more than $20M after tripling revenue in the past year. The startup builds an AI-native loan origination platform that points autonomous agents at intake, document collection, underwriting, approvals, and portfolio monitoring, aimed at community and regional banks. The bet is small-business lending, where underwriting a small loan costs about as much as a large one, so most banks cap their volume and leave borrower demand unmet. Lama AI argues automation changes that math.

title: Défense : la startup allemande STARK, soutenue par Peter Thiel, lève 500 millions d’euros
blurb: Germany's Stark, which builds loitering-munition attack drones, raised €500M at a €3.2B valuation from Founders Fund, Sequoia, the NATO Innovation Fund, Project A, and Döpfner Capital. The round follows a roughly €300M Bundeswehr contract and tracks a lesson from Ukraine, where drones costing tens of thousands of euros routinely destroy equipment worth millions, making the ability to produce fast and at scale a strategic edge. The bigger shift is a new class of European defense manufacturers funded on Silicon Valley terms rather than legacy procurement cycles.

title: Bristol’s Astral Systems secures €26 million Series A to support medical isotope commercialisation
blurb: Astral Systems raised €26 million in a Series A led by Mercia Ventures to commercialise medical isotopes from its compact multi-state fusion reactors by early 2027. The Bristol DeepTech firm is betting fusion's near-term payoff is not energy but the radioisotope supply chain, where global shortages routinely stall cancer diagnosis and treatment. It plans to run next-generation reactors at full capacity at the former Berkeley Power Station by the end of 2026, aiming to revive domestic UK isotope production rather than chase grid power.

title: SoftBank’s Son Sets Bold Net Asset Value Target on Promise of ‘Super AI’
blurb: Masayoshi Son set a target to grow SoftBank's net asset value to $6.189 trillion within a decade, betting the gap gets closed by what he calls "super AI". The figure is roughly ten times SoftBank's current level and would rank it among the largest entities on earth by that measure. Son has made outsized long-horizon calls before, and this one ties the firm's entire valuation thesis to AI compounding faster than any prior technology wave.

title: Why Europe is rebuilding its tech stack
blurb: European CTOs are now under board-level pressure to rip US technology out of their stacks, and a single event in June made the threat concrete. The Trump administration ordered Anthropic to block foreign nationals from its most powerful models, a live demonstration of the "digital kill switch" that turned a policy debate into an operational risk. The dependency runs deep: a Proton survey found 74% of publicly listed European companies rely on US-made email alone, and Denmark signups doubled after Trump's Greenland threats. Sovereignty has moved from white paper to migration plan.

title: Revolut, Wise and UiPath-backer Seedcamp raises $320m
blurb: Seedcamp, the European early-stage firm behind Revolut, Wise, and UiPath, raised $320m across two new funds to keep writing first checks into the region's startups. The firm is leaning into the sovereignty wave sweeping European tech, but drawing a hard line: it won't back companies whose only differentiator is being local. The signal for founders is that capital is flowing again to European pre-seed, but the bar is the same as anywhere, and "we're sovereign" is a feature, not a business.

title: Masayoshi Son Calls AI Bubble Talk an ‘Insult,’ Delays Retiring
blurb: SoftBank's Masayoshi Son dismissed AI bubble talk as an "insult" to a revolution he says has only just begun, and scrapped his plan to retire in his sixties to ride the boom. At 68, he intends to stay at the helm for another 10 to 15 years and rebuild SoftBank into a leading AI-powered robotics company. The man who lost the most paper wealth of anyone when the dot-com bubble burst is now betting his final act that this cycle is different.

title: Built by a tradesman backed by a16z and Sequoia, Probook raises $40M to reinvent dispatch for America’s home service businesses
blurb: Probook raised $40M to automate the unglamorous core of home services: dispatch. The $34M Series A was led by a16z, on top of a $6M seed led by Sequoia, and the pitch is an AI operating system that handles scheduling, customer communication, and routing for plumbing, HVAC, and electrical operators. While most startups chased lead generation and customer acquisition, Probook went after operational execution, and the early numbers show why, with some customers running technician-to-dispatcher ratios as high as 100:1.

title: Superhuman has acquired AI authenticity service GPTZero
blurb: Superhuman, maker of an AI writing assistant, acquired AI-detection service GPTZero and plans to fold it into Superhuman Go.

title: Qualcomm is about to spend $4B on a startup that makes no chips — because Nvidia’s real moat was never the silicon, and Cristiano Amon finally figured out the shortcut
blurb: Qualcomm is in talks to spend $4B on Modular, a startup that makes no chips, just a compiler and inference runtime that lets AI models run across different silicon without being rewritten. The bet is that Nvidia's real moat was never the chips but CUDA, the software layer and years of engineer muscle memory that keep customers locked in. Most challengers attack the silicon and lose. Qualcomm is buying the bridge instead, betting that a non-Nvidia accelerator stops looking like a science project once the software gap closes.

title: AirPods Maker Luxshare Gauges Interest for $3 Billion Hong Kong Listing
blurb: Luxshare, the Chinese contract manufacturer that assembles AirPods, has started gauging investor interest for a Hong Kong listing that could raise about $3 billion, one of the city's biggest deals this year. The move signals Apple's supply chain pushing to diversify funding away from the mainland as US-China tensions reshape where hardware gets built and financed. For founders building physical products, it is a reminder that the contract manufacturers behind the biggest brands are now large enough to court global capital on their own terms.

title: Stark bags €500M in new funding
blurb: Stark, the German maker of weaponized drones, raised €500M at a €3.5B valuation, with Sequoia, Founders Fund, the NATO Innovation Fund, and Peter Thiel among the backers. More than 80% of the capital goes straight into R&D and manufacturing, funding electronic warfare research and production scale-up. Founded in 2024, Stark has become the poster child for a European defense tech surge driven by governments ramping up military spend.

title: Musk’s SpaceX Adds Billions in Debt While Cutting Interest Costs
blurb: SpaceX sold $25B of investment-grade bonds on Tuesday, retiring the costly debt from Musk's 2022 X acquisition and the loans xAI raised last year to cover its cash burn. The trade adds billions to the empire's balance sheet while cutting its annual interest bill, because investment-grade pricing is far cheaper than the rescue financing it replaces. The signal for founders is that scale and a strong enough name can refinance an entire cash-burning portfolio into cheaper capital, turning a debt problem into a cost-of-capital advantage.

title: Meta’s Cox Sought Shah’s WhatsApp Advice — Then Made Him Leader
blurb: Meta handed leadership of WhatsApp to Kunal Shah, the Cred founder and prominent Indian angel investor, after a cold email turned into the deciding pitch. Chief Product Officer Chris Cox had been calling entrepreneurs across India, Brazil, and Mexico, where WhatsApp dominates commerce and daily life, looking for someone who understood the app from the markets that actually run on it. The pick signals that Meta sees WhatsApp's future in emerging-market business behavior rather than Silicon Valley product instincts, and it puts an outsider operator in charge of one of the company's largest user bases.

title: BlackRock-Backed AIP, KKR Said to Court Stack Asia Data Centers
blurb: Stack Infrastructure is preparing to sell its Asia-Pacific data centers as soon as July, drawing bidders including BlackRock-backed AIP, Brookfield, KKR, IFM Investors, and DigitalBridge. The lineup signals how aggressively infrastructure capital is moving into the physical layer of AI, where the constraint is increasingly power and real estate rather than chips. For founders building on cloud compute, the takeaway is that the data center landlords are consolidating into a handful of deep-pocketed financial owners, which over time shapes where capacity gets built and what it costs.

title: Astral Systems raises £23M to tackle the global shortages of medical radioisotopes
blurb: Astral Systems raised £23M, led by Mercia Ventures, to mass-produce medical radioisotopes from compact fusion reactors rather than chase long-term power. The distinction is the bet: while most fusion ventures sell a decade-out promise, Astral already runs multiple TRL9 reactors and plans to ship critical medical isotopes by early 2027. The supply chain for these isotopes is fragile and prone to global shortages that stall cancer diagnosis and treatment, so a continuously operable private neutron source at the old Berkeley Power Station is a near-term commercial product, not a science project.

title: JUPUS raises €13M to power the next generation of AI-driven law firms
blurb: JUPUS raised €13M in a Series A led by Semapa Next to build AI secretarial software for law firms. The pitch tracks a structural gap: over the past three decades, newly trained legal assistants in Germany fell more than 70%, while practising lawyers tripled and admin workloads kept climbing, hitting the small and mid-sized firms that make up most of Europe's legal market. JUPUS automates calls, intake, case prep, and drafting for more than 2,000 lawyers and 2,000 daily cases, and quadrupled ARR in 2025 as it sells the staffing shortfall as the wedge.

title: Austria’s Ora Computing secures €3.5 million to make AI models smaller and faster
blurb: Ora Computing raised €3.5M in a Seed round, led by Constructor Capital and Greencode Ventures, to compress AI foundation models that run up to four times faster with up to 80% less memory. The Vienna startup is betting that the next wave of adoption comes from compact, use-case-specific models rather than large general-purpose ones. The round fits a broader 2026 pattern: capital is flowing not just into raw compute and data centers, but into the software layer that makes AI cheaper and more deployable, where local, on-device inference becomes viable.

title: Is there an AI bubble? VCs on valuations and ARR inflation
blurb: Three investors debate whether AI valuations and ARR growth signal a bubble, recorded live at StrictlyVC LA. The sharper question for founders isn't whether the boom pops but what survives it, since soaring multiples and inflated ARR say little about which companies last. Their answer points to durable fundamentals over headline growth in an increasingly crowded field.

title: Ora Computing raises €3.5M to build the efficiency layer of the AI stack
blurb: Ora Computing raised €3.5M in seed funding led by Constructor Capital and Greencode Ventures to compress AI foundation models by up to 80% while keeping accuracy losses under 5%. The bet is that the next wave of adoption runs on compact, application-specific models rather than ever-larger cloud ones, which matters when inference bills now hit tens of millions of euros a month and edge deployment on vehicles and industrial hardware stays out of reach. Ora compressed a 70B-parameter model in hours for under $1,000, against industry benchmarks that run into the hundreds of thousands.

title: A PhD student’s nerve-healing polymer just raised €60M to take on the US operating room
blurb: TISSIUM raised €60M to scale COAPTIUM CONNECT, the only FDA-authorized sutureless nerve repair system, into US operating rooms.

title: Boost Inference Performance up to 15x on NVIDIA Blackwell Using DFlash Speculative Decoding
blurb: NVIDIA released DFlash, an open-source block-diffusion drafter for speculative decoding that boosts gpt-oss-120b inference up to 15x on Blackwell.

title: How we found a bug in the hyper HTTP library
blurb: Cloudflare engineers spent six weeks tracing intermittent truncation in their Images binding, where larger transformation requests returned a 200 status but delivered only a fraction of the expected image data. The root cause was a race condition in the hyper Rust HTTP library affecting how processed image data streamed back to the client, and the fix came down to four lines of code.

title: Interactions API: our primary interface for Gemini models and agents
blurb: Google moved its Interactions API to general availability and made it the primary interface for calling Gemini models and running agents, replacing the December 2025 beta. The GA release adds Managed Agents that provision a remote Linux sandbox per call, background execution via background=True, mixed built-in and custom tools in one request, and Flex/Priority tiers that cut cost by up to 50%.

title: Researchers Detail DifyTap Flaws in Dify That Could Expose AI Chats Across Tenants
blurb: Zafran Security disclosed four vulnerabilities, collectively named DifyTap, in Dify, the open-source agentic workflow platform with over 146,000 GitHub stars. Two were critical and two required no authentication, with cross-tenant flaws that let attackers read other customers' private AI chats and uploaded documents on the multi-tenant cloud service using only a file UUID.

title: Run isolated sandboxes with full lifecycle control: AWS Lambda introduces MicroVMs
blurb: AWS Lambda introduced MicroVMs, a serverless primitive that runs untrusted user- or AI-generated code in isolated, stateful environments powered by Firecracker, the same virtualization behind Lambda functions. Each MicroVM gives a session VM-level isolation with near-instant launch and resume, retains memory and disk state for the session, and pauses to low idle cost when the user steps away.

title: ShapedPlugin WordPress Pro Plugins Backdoored in Supply Chain Attack
blurb: Attackers compromised ShapedPlugin's build and distribution pipeline, injecting backdoor code into Pro versions of Product Slider Pro for WooCommerce, Real Testimonials Pro, and Smart Post Show Pro delivered through the vendor's official licensed update channels. The malware fetches a remote payload on every admin page load, captures plaintext credentials and 2FA codes, exfiltrates wp-config.php and recent WooCommerce order data, and carries a CVSS 10.0 rating, while free WordPress.org builds are unaffected.

title: Unlimited OCR: One-Shot Long-Horizon Parsing
blurb: Baidu released Unlimited-OCR, a document-parsing model that extends Deepseek-OCR to handle long, multi-page inputs in a single pass. It runs through HuggingFace transformers on CUDA GPUs, supports single-image, multi-page, and PDF parsing with a 32K-token output window, and ships with weights on both HuggingFace and ModelScope alongside an arXiv paper.

title: Steam Machine launches today
blurb: Valve's Steam Machine ships today, a compact living-room PC running SteamOS on Linux with Proton handling Windows game compatibility. The launch extends Valve's push to make Linux a viable gaming target, building on the Steam Deck's Proton layer that has steadily closed the compatibility gap with native Windows titles.

title: Show HN: Oak – Git alternative designed for agents
blurb: Oak is an open-source version control system built for AI coding agents, structured as a Rust Cargo workspace with a reusable VCS library and a CLI that agents drive. It uses branch-per-session as the unit of work, branch descriptions instead of per-commit messages, and content-addressed lazy mounts that hydrate on demand, making it faster than git for agent workloads.

title: Making secret scanning more trustworthy: Reducing false positives at scale
blurb: GitHub partnered with Microsoft Security & AI's Agents Offense team to add LLM-based contextual verification to its secret scanning, borrowing the in-context reasoning approach from Agentic Secret Finder to judge whether a detected value is a real exposure rather than just pattern-matching it. The goal is to bring AI-detected generic secrets up to the same precision as provider-pattern detections, cutting low-value alerts that erode developer trust without sacrificing coverage.

title: Grad Detect: Gradient-Based Hallucination Detection in LLMs
blurb: Grad Detect predicts LLM hallucinations from layer-wise gradients in a single forward-backward pass, with the final five layers holding 97% of the signal.

title: Adopting AV1 for Real-Time Communication (RTC) at Scale
blurb: Meta rolled out the AV1 video codec across the majority of mobile devices in its Messenger and WhatsApp real-time calling apps, after introducing it on high-end hardware in 2023. The post details the rate control and error resilience work behind the deployment, citing at least a 20% bitrate reduction over H.264/AVC on low- and mid-range devices and noticeably sharper video at the sub-100 kbps bitrates common in emerging markets.

title: RFC 9958 - Post-Quantum Cryptography for Engineers
blurb: RFC 9958 is an informational IETF document explaining post-quantum cryptography for working engineers, covering why a cryptographically relevant quantum computer would break today's public key algorithms and what the migration to PQC entails. Unlike past cryptographic upgrades, it argues the transition may demand significant protocol redesign because post-quantum algorithms carry different size and performance properties than the RSA and elliptic-curve schemes they replace.

title: Bayesian control for coding agents
blurb: This paper formulates coding-agent orchestration as cost-sensitive sequential hypothesis testing, with a Bayesian controller that tracks a belief over candidate correctness and decides whether to gather diagnostics, refine, verify, or stop. Tested across six generators and nine benchmarks, the approach pays off most when verification is expensive and critics are informative but imperfect, and its belief state doubles as a correctness score that beats token-probability and tool-success baselines.

title: Prompt Injection as Role Confusion
blurb: The author argues that prompt injection is fundamentally a role-confusion problem: because an LLM receives system prompts, user messages, tool outputs, and its own reasoning as one undifferentiated token stream, it cannot reliably tell its own thoughts from injected text. Framing roles as the core abstraction lets the post derive new attacks, explain existing mech-interp results, and predict when an injection will succeed.

title: Lighthouse agentic browsing scoring
blurb: Chrome's Lighthouse added an Agentic Browsing category that runs deterministic audits on how well a site supports machine interaction, checking WebMCP tool registration, accessibility-tree integrity, and layout stability. Rather than a 0-100 score, it reports a fractional pass ratio plus per-audit pass/fail status, and the CDP-driven checks are reproducible enough to wire into CI/CD.

title: Vulnerability reports are not special anymore
blurb: The author, former lead of the Go Security team, argues that vulnerability reports no longer warrant the special responsiveness and attribution maintainers have traditionally owed researchers, because LLMs have made the underlying insight and confidentiality cheap. With anyone able to generate plausible findings, the bottleneck has shifted from discovery to triage, leaving a security@ inbox with roughly the same signal-to-noise ratio as raw LLM output.

title: GitHub Updates actions/checkout to Block Common Pwn Request Attack Patterns
blurb: GitHub's actions/checkout v7 now refuses to fetch fork pull request code in `pull_request_target` and `workflow_run` workflows by default, blocking the most common pwn request pattern that lets untrusted forks steal the privileged GITHUB_TOKEN and secrets. Effective June 18, 2026, with a backport to all supported major versions due July 16, workflow authors who need the old behavior must explicitly opt in via the `allow-unsafe-pr-checkout` flag.

title: The text in Claude Code's “Extended Thinking” output is not authentic
blurb: The author found that Claude Code's on-disk "thinking blocks" contain only an encrypted 600-character signature plus a summary, not the model's actual reasoning, since Anthropic holds the decryption key and gates the full output behind an enterprise agreement. The takeaway for engineers: local session logs can't serve as an audit trail of the logic that drove an agent's actions, and the docs' phrasing ("returns a summary of Claude's full thinking process") makes that easy to miss.

title: Understanding the (In)Security of Vibe-Coded Applications
blurb: This paper systematically audits a large corpus of real-world vibe-coded applications and finds recurring vulnerability classes distinct from conventional development, including placeholder logic, unfiltered input, and exposed secrets. The authors trace these to structural limits of AI agents like memory loss, locally optimized objectives, and weak security knowledge, and show that stronger models and better prompting reduce but never eliminate the risk.

title: GLM-5.2 Raises the Bar for Open Models
blurb: GLM-5.2 released last week as likely the strongest open model available, a clear step up from GLM-5.1 and closer to the frontier than DeepSeek R1 was at its peak. The author argues benchmarks act as a ceiling rather than a point estimate for open models, and that GLM-5.2 sits roughly four to seven months behind frontier closed models at a lower price, though missing features like vision make its practical niche hard to find.

title: Scaling Laws for Task-Specific LLM Distillation
blurb: This paper derives empirical scaling laws for task-specific LLM distillation, quantifying how in-domain and general-knowledge performance degrade with dataset size, compression ratio, supervision format, and iterative pruning schedule. The authors show that general-knowledge benchmarks collapse well before in-domain quality does, with chain-of-thought supervision actively recovering knowledge that pruning erases, and they release the FinHeadlineMix dataset and scaling-law results.

title: 'Cordyceps': Mushrooming Malicious Pull Requests Threaten Developer Workflows
blurb: Security researchers at Novee disclosed "Cordyceps," a class of CI/CD weakness that lets attackers use malicious pull requests to hijack the high-privilege automated workflows sitting between a PR and its merge, reaching signing keys and access tokens for command injection and supply chain compromise. A single scan flagged 654 repositories as potentially exploitable and confirmed 300 fully exploitable, including Azure Sentinel, Google's Agent Development Kit, Apache Doris, Cloudflare's Workers SDK, and Python's Black.

title: A public Sentry key is all it takes to hijack Claude Code, Cursor, and Codex
blurb: Tenet Security's Threat Labs documented "agentjacking," an attack that uses a public Sentry DSN to plant a forged error report and turn Claude Code, Cursor, or Codex into a code-execution engine on a developer's machine. The DSN is a write-only credential Sentry designed to be public, but agents reading error logs over MCP cannot distinguish data from instructions, so the planted command simply runs with no malware or stolen password involved.

title: FUTO Swipe – A new swipe typing model
blurb: FUTO released a new swipe typing model trained on over 1 million QWERTY English swipes collected from volunteers in 2024. The underlying dataset, filtered for quality and published under the MIT license on HuggingFace, is available alongside the model for anyone building or benchmarking gesture typing systems.

title: F3
blurb: F3 is a research-prototype columnar file format that aims to fix the layout limitations of Parquet and ORC while staying interoperable, using embedded Wasm decoders so readers can support custom encodings without a format rewrite. The project is a proof-of-concept tied to a 2025 paper, currently tested only on Debian and explicitly not meant for production use.

title: Elevated error rate across multiple models
blurb: Anthropic's status page reported an elevated error rate affecting multiple Claude models, degrading API responses for applications built on those endpoints. Teams routing production traffic through Claude saw failed or retried requests during the window, a reminder that single-provider LLM integrations inherit the provider's uptime and benefit from fallback routing or graceful degradation.

title: Swift Package Index joins Apple
blurb: Swift Package Index, the community-run directory and metadata service for open-source Swift packages, is joining Apple, with the site continuing to operate as before. The move puts a piece of the Swift ecosystem's discovery infrastructure under Apple's control, raising the usual questions about long-term independence and governance for a tool many Swift developers rely on daily.

title: DifyTap Bugs Let Attackers 'Wiretap' AI Chat Histories
blurb: Researchers disclosed four Dify vulnerabilities, dubbed DifyTap, that let attackers wiretap AI chat histories and exfiltrate cross-tenant data; patched in 1.14.2.

title: Fired by Google for creating the Google workspace CLI
blurb: A Google engineer says he was fired after building an unofficial Google Workspace CLI that hit #1 on Hacker News and drew thousands of GitHub stars and users in days. The termination landed two days after Google announced an official Workspace CLI at Cloud Next, and he attributes the move to internal fear over what agents mean for Workspace rather than the tool itself.

title: Unsloth GLM-5.2 – How to Run Locally
blurb: Unsloth released Dynamic GGUF quants of Z.ai's GLM-5.2, a 744B-parameter MoE with 40B active parameters and a 1M context window, letting the open model run on local hardware down to a 239GB 2-bit build that fits a 256GB unified-memory Mac. The 2-bit quant holds ~82% accuracy at 84% smaller than the full 1.5TB model, with 4-bit and 5-bit dynamic quants measuring near-lossless on KL divergence.

title: SHERLOC: Structured Diagnostic Localization for Code Repair Agents
blurb: SHERLOC is a training-free framework that pairs a reasoning LLM with compact repository tools and self-recovery to localize faults for code repair agents, reaching 84.33% accuracy@1 on SWE-Bench Lite and 81.27% recall@1 on SWE-Bench Verified. Feeding its locations and diagnostic findings into repair agents raises resolve rate by 5.95 points on SWE-Bench Verified while cutting localization and total tokens by 36.7% and 23.1%.

title: Using Codex for Long-Running Projects
blurb: OpenAI published a guide on structuring long-running, multi-hour coding work with Codex, covering how to break large tasks into checkpointed steps, keep context scoped, and review agent output before it compounds. The patterns center on giving Codex clear task boundaries and verification gates so it can run autonomously on bigger projects without drifting off the intended change.

title: ClickHouse Ingestion at Scale: An Open-Source Zepto Engineering Story
blurb: Zepto rewrote core parts of the open-source ClickHouse Kafka Connect connector to add buffering and batching logic, boosting ingestion throughput by 45% while eliminating GC pauses and cutting ClickHouse insert pressure. The post traces how Confluent Cloud's managed black box blocked low-level broker and connector tuning at a sustained 10 MB/s, and details the two pull requests Zepto merged back upstream to fix the hidden performance killers.

title: Cloudflare teams up with Chrome, Firefox, and Edge on a privacy-first anti-bot protocol
blurb: Cloudflare, Mozilla, Google, Microsoft, and Shopify are building PACT, a protocol that issues anonymous tokens to prove a visitor is a human or an authorized bot, replacing CAPTCHAs and forced logins without tracking. The tokens are designed so they cannot reconstruct browsing history, and the group plans to submit them for formal standardization as automated systems now make up roughly 58% of HTTP requests worldwide.

title: Build real agentic apps using CUGA: two dozen working examples on a lightweight harness
blurb: CUGA, IBM's open-source agent harness, handles the planning, execution loop, tool calls, and state management so building an agent comes down to a tool list and a prompt, installed via pip install cuga. The companion cuga-apps repo ships two dozen single-file FastAPI apps wrapping one CugaAgent each, with configurable Fast, Balanced, and Accurate reasoning modes and a choice of local, Docker, or E2B sandboxes.

title: Accelerating Presto with GPUs
blurb: Meta's engineers extended Presto to be GPU-aware, tackling two bottlenecks: moving data from storage into GPU operators and exchanging data between operators across a distributed query without leaving GPU memory. Built on NVIDIA's cuDF library and shipped in open-source Presto/Velox, the changes deliver up to 6x cost/performance gains over CPU Presto on TPC-H analytical benchmarks and already run customer production workloads.

title: Agentic Generation of AST Transformation Rules for Fixing Breaking Updates
blurb: BigBag is an agentic framework that generates reusable AST transformation rules to repair the breaking API changes a dependency update introduces, encoding the fix at the API level so it transfers to any client broken by the same update. On 157 compilation failures from the BUMP benchmark, the best configuration hit a 78.6% fix rate and transferred across projects at 33.3%, rising above 80% when all clients invoke the affected API uniformly.

title: The Coming Loop
blurb: The author argues that agentic engineering is shifting from the in-agent tool loop to a harness-level loop, where a queue feeds tasks to a machine and an outer harness decides whether the work is actually finished or needs another session. He resists adopting it for code he cares about, citing models that write overly defensive, locally-reasoned code and his own unwillingness to ship systems he cannot fully explain.

title: The minimum viable unit of saleable software
blurb: The author argues that LLMs have shifted the buy-vs-build calculus but not eliminated the case for selling software, since LLM-built systems still demand dozens of feedback loops to reach quality and carry ongoing maintenance cost. His bet on turning the River side project into a business rests on cheap not meaning free, leaving room for polished, well-maintained packages that internal LLM clones rarely match.

title: Announcing DuckDB 1.5.4
blurb: DuckDB 1.5.4 (Variegata) is the fifth patch release in the 1.5 line, shipping bugfixes, security patches, and performance work alongside a parallel v1.4.5 LTS (Andium) release. Fixes include correctness bugs in VARIANT casts and MERGE INTO binding, several crash and memory-leak repairs in Arrow GeoArrow serialization and gzip writes, and allocator and Parquet stats-pruning improvements.

title: CCCL Runtime: A Modern C++ Runtime for CUDA
blurb: NVIDIA released CCCL runtime, a set of header-only C++ APIs (`<cuda/stream>`, `<cuda/buffer>`, `<cuda/launch>`) that reimplements core CUDA functionality like stream management, memory allocation, and kernel launches on top of the driver API. It targets the same role as the classic CUDA runtime but with modern C++ idioms for safer resource management and explicit dependencies, plus compatibility helpers for incremental adoption without rewriting surrounding CUDA runtime code.

title: Raspberry Pi Pico W as USB Wi-Fi Adapter
blurb: This project turns a Raspberry Pi Pico W into a USB Wi-Fi adapter, presenting the board's onboard CYW43 wireless chip to a host machine as a standard USB networking device. The firmware bridges the Pico's Wi-Fi stack over USB, giving older laptops or single-board computers wireless connectivity for the cost of a sub-$10 microcontroller.

title: Detecting AI Coding Agents in Open Source: A Validated Multi-Method Census of 180 Million Repositories
blurb: This paper introduces a multi-method framework for detecting AI coding agent activity across World of Code's 180M+ Git repositories, combining config-file scanning, commit-message analysis, author-identity matching, and bot-signature lookup. Bot-account lookup alone, the signal most adoption studies rely on, recovers only 3.3% of the 850,157 detected Claude Code commits, a 30x relative-recall gap, and PR censuses and commit detection capture nearly disjoint agent populations doing different kinds of work.

title: The SEC has a free financial data API that nobody talks about
blurb: The SEC's EDGAR system exposes every US public company's quarterly financials since 2009 through a free, key-less JSON API at data.sec.gov, the same XBRL-tagged data Bloomberg charges $24k/year for. The post walks through the companyfacts and ticker-to-CIK endpoints and the main gotcha: GAAP concept names vary by company, so revenue or net income needs an alias map that tries each tag in order until one returns data.

title: Mistral launches OCR 4 for multilingual document extraction
blurb: Mistral released OCR 4, a document understanding model that extracts structured content with bounding boxes, typed block classification, and per-region confidence scores across 170 languages. The model is available via API, Mistral Studio, SageMaker, and Microsoft Foundry, or as a self-hosted single container for data-residency needs, with early users reporting lower cost and latency than prior versions feeding RAG and compliance pipelines.

title: Codex-maxxing for long-running work
blurb: In this OpenAI whitepaper, Jason Liu lays out strategies for running Codex as a persistent workspace that preserves context and sustains progress across multi-prompt projects. The guide covers breaking goals into verifiable steps, maintaining continuity across parallel workstreams, and deciding when to delegate execution to Codex versus when to keep a human in the loop.

title: Experimenting with the proposed Cross-Origin Storage API in Transformers.js
blurb: The post experiments with the proposed Cross-Origin Storage API to let browser-based ML apps share cached model files across origins, instead of re-downloading identical weights every time a new site loads the same Transformers.js model. The author shows that two apps on different origins each fetch the same 177 MB Whisper model separately, and demonstrates how the API would deduplicate that storage by content hash with explicit user permission.

title: AWS Continuum offers devs help with securing code
blurb: AWS released Continuum, a service that continuously discovers, investigates, and remediates vulnerabilities across first-party and third-party code, generating remediation recommendations and proposing fixes that route through existing review workflows. It adds new capabilities like STRIDE-format threat modeling on top of penetration testing and code scanning borrowed from Security Agent, plus an opt-in "enforce mode" that autonomously fixes code lapses once it has learned a team's guardrails.

title: BluTrain: A C++/CUDA Framework for AI Systems
blurb: BluTrain is a from-scratch C++/CUDA training framework with every layer implemented natively, including a typed tensor module with reverse-mode autograd, a caching allocator, distributed execution, and an MLIR-based compiler. Training a 124M-parameter GPT-2 in FP32 on 8 6000 Ada GPUs, it sustains 407K tokens/s against PyTorch's 395K and cuts memory footprint by up to 22% while converging to a marginally lower validation loss.

title: Show HN: TikZ Editor – WYSIWYG editor for figures in LaTeX
blurb: TikZ Editor is a browser-based WYSIWYG tool for building TikZ figures, letting you draw diagrams visually while it generates the corresponding LaTeX code. It targets the usual friction of hand-writing TikZ coordinates and node positioning, with the canvas and emitted code staying in sync as you edit.

title: Rhombus Language 1.0
blurb: The Racket team released Rhombus 1.0, a general-purpose dynamic language that pairs a conventional, non-parenthetical syntax with Racket-level macro extensibility. It targets developers who want Lisp-tradition metaprogramming without s-expression notation, shipping with static abstraction constructs, ellipsis-based repetitions, and practical libraries for scaling from scripts to larger systems.

title: "Fix" MacBook Neo Cursor Lag: Record 1 Pixel of the Screen Every 10 Seconds
blurb: A developer works around MacBook Neo cursor lag by recording one screen pixel every 10 seconds to force the slower software cursor path.

title: Latitude launches open-source platform to monitor AI agents
blurb: Latitude released an open-source, MIT-licensed platform for monitoring AI agents in production, clustering thousands of live conversations into usage patterns, collapsing repeated failures into named signals, and exposing it all to coding agents through an MCP server. Teams can self-host it, promote saved semantic searches into monitors that flag patterns before they spread, and turn production conversations into datasets to verify a fix holds before shipping.

title: p99 0 ms* autocomplete for 240 million domain names
