blurb: The author achieves "p99 0 ms" autocomplete over 240 million domain names by prefetching suggestions on keyDown and rendering on keyUp, so results are usually ready before the user releases the key. The measured budget is about 121 ms per keystroke at p99, met by a two-tier API that searches the Tranco top-1M list as the head before falling back to the full gTLD set from CZDS.

title: JupOtter: Cell-Level Bug Detection in Jupyter Notebooks
blurb: JupOtter is a bug detection system built specifically for Jupyter Notebooks, using a tokenization strategy that preserves cell structure to flag bugs at the individual cell level rather than across the whole file. It ships with OtterDataset, 21,000+ notebooks annotated for cell-level bugs, and beats both static analyzers and LLMs on F1 in two of three evaluation datasets.

title: Don't rely on instructions, use Agent Hooks to enforce guardrails
blurb: The author argues that AGENTS.md and CLAUDE.md instructions are unreliable guardrails because agents ignore them, and shows how Agent Hooks enforce rules deterministically instead. The post walks through two Claude Code hooks: a PreToolUse hook that blocks raw `<input>` tags by exiting with code 2 to feed stderr back as agent feedback, and a Stop hook that prevents the agent from claiming it's done while a design-system ratchet test fails.

title: ComputeFHE: A Privacy-Preserving General-Purpose Computation Library
blurb: ComputeFHE is an open-source C++ library built on OpenFHE that lets developers write privacy-preserving programs over encrypted data using familiar imperative code, with encrypted integer and fixed-point types plus arithmetic, comparison, conditional, and oblivious array-access operations. Its FHE-friendly ALU architecture cuts bootstrapping operations for up to 3.9x speedups on selected operations, and a simulation mode reports circuit complexity and bootstrapping costs without running actual cryptography.

title: The Flat Curve Society
blurb: The author argues that as frontier models cross into dangerous capability, access to them will be restricted like nuclear weapons, with governments clamping down on the compute supply chain rather than the models themselves. Most companies and engineers will be left with capped, mediocre models or buy intelligence vending-machine style, sending specs to vendors who run the work on their own servers.

title: Using Visual Studio Code’s ‘air-gapped’ AI model mode
blurb: VS Code 1.122 added "Use BYOK without a GitHub sign-in," which lets chat, tools, and MCP servers run in air-gapped or restricted environments using local models like Ollama or LM Studio, no GitHub account required. Inline and next-edit suggestions still can't be driven by a local LLM without extra tooling, but the rest of the AI workflow now runs fully offline against a coding model that fits your VRAM.

title: Kops: Safely Extending the eBPF Compilation Pipeline with Native Operations
blurb: Kops is an extension interface that lets userspace compilers and kernel modules add new eBPF operations without touching the kernel core, pairing each operation with a verifier-checked proof sequence and a native code emit that is the only addition to the trusted computing base. Built on it, EInsn maps seven CPU idioms like rotate and conditional select to single instructions, speeding up microbenchmarks by 24% and production apps by 12%.

title: window.showDirectoryPicker opens up a whole new world
blurb: Chrome's window.showDirectoryPicker() lets a website request read/write access to a directory on the user's filesystem, enabling local-first apps that operate on the user's own files instead of cloud storage. The author used it with Claude to build browser-based photo management and a node-based compositing tool inspired by Apple's Shake, all reading and writing source files directly on disk.

title: Why Drawing Tablet Brands Won't Collaborate on Linux Floss Drivers
blurb: The author, who reviews drawing tablets on Linux, documents his failed push to get brands like Huion, XpPen, Gaomon, and Ugee to share hardware specs with the kernel's hid/input maintainers for open-source drivers. After reaching a technical contact who manages drivers across all four brands, he traces the wall to a business reality: the brands treat their proprietary driver structure as a competitive asset and see no upside in upstream FLOSS collaboration.

title: Metric Semantic Layer: How Lyft Governs and Scales Key Data Definitions
blurb: Lyft built an internal Metric Semantic Layer, a centralized Python package that stores every metric's plain-English description and definitive SQL in YAML configs and Jinja templates, ending the problem of teams using divergent definitions for the same metric. Updating a definition once propagates the change to every downstream application, with governance baked in through clear ownership, defined scope, and accountability that survives org changes and attrition.

title: Optimizing Models to Be Fast at Codegen
blurb: Morph trains workload-specific speculative drafters on coding output, lifting acceptance from 1.93x to 3.07x and serving open models like Qwen and DeepSeek faster.

title: TypeScript Performance in TanStack Table V9
blurb: TanStack Table V9 makes features modular through a new TFeatures generic, so you register only the features you want and both the runtime and types are assembled from that selection. The refactor cut TypeScript type-checking work by 62-86% across packages between alpha and the latest beta, replacing V8's single baked-in Table type and global declaration merging with per-table inference and proper tree-shaking.

title: I am dreading our LLM-written incident report future
blurb: The author argues that using LLMs to write incident reports, not just gather their inputs, removes the thinking-while-writing step that forces engineers to confront whether their explanation actually fits the evidence. The result is a plausible, unchecked narrative that may invent system couplings or miss real interactions, which the author considers more dangerous than LLM coding because there is no test to catch the fabrication.

title: The Low-Tech AI of Elden Ring
blurb: FromSoftware implements the AI behind its punishing Soulsborne bosses not with machine learning but with a pushdown automaton, a stack of "Goals" written in Havok Script that push attack sub-goals each frame and unwind on failure. The post walks through the decompiled code to show how a CoolBossBattle goal stacks parameterized attacks, with each goal's update returning Continue, Success, or Failure to drive what executes next frame.

title: Optimization catalog. How 4 bytes of padding make array clearing 49% faster
blurb: A Go developer found that inserting 4 bytes of struct padding makes array clearing 49% faster on Intel chips, because misaligned data forces the compiler away from the fast REP STOSQ path. Benchmarks show a clear zebra pattern where any offset divisible by 8 hits full throughput, with the effect dropping to ~9% on AMD and disappearing entirely on ARM.

title: Maestro Order: A Model-Agnostic Orchestration Harness
blurb: Maestro Order is a model-agnostic orchestration harness that treats any LLM as a black-box solver and composes four primitives (decompose, ensemble, verify, recurse) under a budget-aware controller that spends compute where reliability gains are cheapest. A Monte Carlo simulation shows verification stacking amplifies reliability geometrically (0.55 to 0.98 with two gates, 0.999 with four), while voting only helps above chance and is capped by correlated errors.

title: Don't verify email addresses by sending spam to them
blurb: The author dissects a sign-up form that "validates" email addresses by firing off a POST request that triggers an unsolicited bulk message, complete with Precedence: Bulk headers and hidden-div filler text. The piece argues this pattern is both technically pointless and a deliberate abuse of inbox delivery, reaffirming that the only sane verification is sending a real confirmation link and letting bounces sort themselves out.

title: Swift Package Index joins Apple, pledges to remain open source
blurb: Swift Package Index, the community-run search engine and metadata index for Swift packages, is joining Apple and has pledged to remain open source with no immediate changes to how packages are indexed or documentation is hosted. Apple engineers will now contribute alongside the community, with planned work on package signing and identity, and native Xcode integration for searching and adding dependencies as a likely next step.

title: JSON-LD Explained for Personal Websites
blurb: JSON-LD adds Schema.org structured data to your `<head>`, helping crawlers parse your site for richer link previews and better search ranking.

title: Why doesn't MQTT have its own Express? Introducing mqttkit
blurb: mqttkit is an Elysia-style application framework for MQTT with typed topic routes, middleware, MQTT 5 RPC, and auto-generated AsyncAPI docs.

title: What Fivetran + dbt Labs brought to Databricks Data + AI Summit (and what you can take home)
blurb: dbt Labs and Fivetran, now merged into one company, used Databricks Data + AI Summit to pitch Open Data Infrastructure for agents that query the warehouse continuously and need governed, tested, traceable context. The concrete takeaway is a customer pattern from Integral Ad Science that wires Databricks to dbt through MCP servers, letting an agent pull model metadata and lineage to answer why a BI number is wrong.

title: Building Jaeger’s ClickHouse backend: 8.6× compression on 10 million spans
blurb: Jaeger v2.18.0 ships a native ClickHouse tracing backend, hitting 8.6× compression on 10M spans via columnar storage.

title: AWS enters the context layer race with a graph that learns from agents, not manual curation
blurb: AWS announced a context intelligence stack for AI agents, led by AWS Context, a knowledge graph service that automatically maps relationships across existing data and refines itself based on which sources agents actually use. It publishes all metadata as Apache Iceberg to S3 Tables queryable via Athena, Redshift, or Spark, and ships alongside GA of S3 Annotations and a preview of skill assets in Glue Data Catalog.

title: Boost BigQuery with Python: Managed Python UDFs now generally available
blurb: Google made BigQuery's Managed Python UDFs generally available, letting teams run custom Python with libraries like NumPy, pandas, and scikit-learn directly inside SQL queries or BigFrames on serverless, auto-scaling compute. BigQuery handles container builds, security patching, and execution, so procedural logic, scientific computation, and external API enrichment that used to need separate infrastructure now run next to the data without managing images or compute services.

title: The SEC has a free financial data API that nobody talks about
blurb: data.sec.gov exposes every US public company's quarterly XBRL financials back to 2009 as free, well-documented JSON, with no API key or rate limit for normal use. The companyfacts endpoint returns every reported GAAP concept per period, though concept names vary by filer, so pulling a clean metric like revenue means trying aliases such as Revenues, RevenueFromContractWithCustomerExcludingAssessedTax, and SalesRevenueNet until one returns data.

title: The analytics engineer in 2026: system designer, governance owner, AI context provider
blurb: dbt argues the analytics engineer's job has shifted from writing models to designing the system around them, as AI now handles scaffolding, first-draft docs, and boilerplate tests for the 72% of analytics engineers who code with AI assistance. The remaining work is the judgment AI can't do: defining metric grain, drawing domain boundaries, and structuring the semantic layer so downstream AI queries stay consistent. Governance outputs like contracts, tests, and column-level lineage become the role's measured deliverable.

title: The semantic debt crisis no one is talking about
blurb: The author argues that organizations carry "semantic debt" alongside technical debt: every metric built without a canonical definition, every filter hardcoded into a dashboard instead of the data layer, is a withdrawal that compounds until three teams present three defensible-but-conflicting numbers for the same metric. The fix is encoding business meaning into the data itself through a semantic layer, which the piece frames as the foundation that makes AI reliable in production rather than a modeling nicety.

title: Accelerating Presto with GPUs
blurb: Meta and Presto's maintainers extended Presto to run query operators on GPUs, using NVIDIA's cuDF library to keep data in GPU memory across distributed exchanges instead of round-tripping through the CPU. On TPC-H-derived benchmarks across a multi-GPU cluster, the team reports up to 6x cost/performance gains over CPU Presto. The code is upstream in open-source Presto/Velox and already running some customer production workloads, making it a real option for analytics teams weighing GPU spend against warehouse compute.

title: Model selection with proper scoring rules on data sets of time series
blurb: This paper shows mean score, not median or mean rank, reliably identifies the true probabilistic model when test sets per time series are short.

title: JupOtter: Cell-Level Bug Detection in Jupyter Notebooks
blurb: JupOtter is a bug-detection system built specifically for Jupyter Notebooks, using a tokenization strategy that preserves cell structure and a cell-level prediction model trained on OtterDataset, a new corpus of over 21,000 notebooks annotated for fine-grained bugs. It beats both static analyzers and LLMs on F1 in two of three evaluation datasets, targeting the rise of buggy notebooks shipped as production code rather than throwaway prototypes.

title: Mistral OCR 4 Ships Bounding Boxes for 170-Language Document AI
blurb: Mistral released OCR 4, adding bounding boxes, block labels, and per-word confidence scores to its document extraction API, with 170-language support and availability through the Mistral API, SageMaker, and Microsoft Foundry. The block view returns coordinates for regions like tables, equations, and signatures so search and compliance systems can preserve page structure, and pricing runs $4 per 1,000 pages, or $2 through the Batch API.

title: The new database world according to Google: Inexact queries and AI in everything
blurb: Google's database leadership laid out a roadmap built on AI-embedded tooling and "inexact" queries, with a product exec predicting that humans won't directly use data platforms within three to five years. The bet is that agents, not analysts, become the primary query interface, pushing vendors toward natural-language and approximate-result systems rather than the exact, schema-bound SQL that data teams build on today.

title: EDB converges analytics on Postgres to support AI agents
blurb: EDB introduced converged analytics for its managed EDB Postgres AI service, keeping Postgres as the operational source of truth and using Apache Iceberg as a shared catalog across ClickHouse, WarehousePG, and Spark so analytical engines query the same data without copies or ETL. The move follows Databricks' Neon-based LTAP from the opposite direction, betting that enterprises focused on data sovereignty want AI and analytics running on infrastructure they own and control.

title: One Index for Subsumption and Roll-up across Time, Geography, and Ontology
blurb: This paper introduces OEH, a single index that handles both subsumption testing ("is x under y?") and hierarchical roll-up across time, geospatial, and ontology hierarchies, which today each live in separate silos. It encodes trees as nested-set order-embeddings and low-width DAGs as chain decompositions, picked by a cheap structural probe. On five real hierarchies it matched a 2-hop index on latency using half the space and building 6-7x faster, while matching TimescaleDB's continuous aggregates and adding roll-up that 2-hop cannot.

title: Unified Dominance Graph for Interval-Predicate Approximate Nearest Neighbor Search
blurb:
This paper introduces the Unified Dominance Graph (UDG), a graph index for approximate nearest neighbor search constrained by interval predicates like containment and overlap. Treating an object's interval endpoints as two independent scalar filters bloats intersection overhead, so UDG maps both endpoints into a normalized 2D dominance space and reuses one construction across predicates. On standard benchmarks it beats hybrid search baselines while keeping indexing overhead low.

(72 words)

title: Grading the Grader: Lessons from Evaluating an Agentic Data Analysis System
blurb: This paper tackles how to reliably grade agentic data analysis systems, whose outputs mix code, numbers, and verbal diagnostics that single-turn eval methods can't handle. Running LAMBDA on 153 numerical QRData tasks, the authors built a three-layer cascade of strict regex matching, lenient LLM grading, and human snippet inspection, both automated graders hitting 100% precision with 97% lenient recall. An iterative nudge mechanism that cues the answer template lifted grading run success from 36% to 97%.

title: Entity Resolution via Batched Oracle Queries
blurb: This paper formalizes batched entity resolution, where an oracle clusters only a limited batch of records at a time and no batch is guaranteed to hold all of an entity's records. The authors prove that selecting optimal batches is NP-hard, give an optimal solution under a natural constraint on entity sizes, and frame the work as pay-as-you-go, trading oracle consults for recall at every step. Across six datasets, their method beats state-of-the-art baselines on recall per query budget.

title: FlowPipe: LLM-Enhanced Conditional Generative Flow Networks for Data Preparation Pipeline Construction
blurb: FlowPipe reframes automated data-prep pipeline construction as conditional probabilistic flow generation over a DAG, using Conditional GFlowNets with a Trajectory Balance objective to tie terminal validation rewards back to early operator choices. LLM-derived priors condition the policy through Feature-wise Linear Modulation so dataset semantics steer the search, while failure awareness steers it away from invalid states. Across 74 real-world datasets it beats Multi-DQN baselines by 11.96% on accuracy and converges 12.5x faster.

title: Real-Time AI Feature Engineering with Spark Structured Streaming and Databricks Feature Store
blurb: This walkthrough builds a production feature pipeline from Kafka events to millisecond online serving using Spark Structured Streaming and Databricks Feature Store, now folded into Unity Catalog as Feature Engineering. The core argument is that most ML failures are data problems, not model problems: training-serving skew from online/offline logic drift, future-data leakage, and stale features. The fix is storing feature computation alongside the data and enforcing point-in-time lookups, so any model version can reproduce the exact training set that produced it.

title: Here’s what’s new with iOS 27 beta 2
blurb: Apple released iOS 27 beta 2 to developers, the first follow-up since the WWDC debut, with refinements to Liquid Glass, a faster Siri AI, and a "Write with Siri" keyboard suggestion that replaces Writing Tools. The build feels further along than typical second betas, signaling that Apple is treating stability as a headline feature ahead of the July public beta and September release.

title: A New Era of Midjourney
blurb: Midjourney announced a pivot beyond image generation into medical hardware, building a full-body Ultrasonic CT scanner that uses a ring of roughly 500,000 grain-sized sensors to produce MRI-quality 3D maps in a 60-second scan. The company plans to open a spa-styled scanning room in San Francisco in 2027 and scale to a fleet of 50,000 scanners handling a billion scans a month by 2031.

title: iOS 27 beta 2 includes Write with Siri, the new version of Apple Intelligence writing tools
blurb: Apple's iOS 27 beta 2 replaces Writing Tools with Write with Siri, swapping the old panel of preset buttons like 'Friendly' and 'Professional' for a natural-language input that expands from the Dynamic Island and pulls in personal context. The new system handles generation, proofreading, and rewrites with a before/after review panel, and works by voice or keyboard across iOS, iPadOS 27, and macOS Golden Gate ahead of a fall release.

title: Why Drawing Tablet Brands Won't Collaborate on Linux Floss Drivers
blurb: Artist David Revoy explains why Huion, XPPen, Gaomon, and Ugee won't help build open-source Linux drivers: the four brands share a single technical team in Shenzhen, and contributing specs to the kernel's HID/input maintainers offers them no commercial upside while exposing how interchangeable their hardware is. The piece is a clear-eyed look at why community driver work still falls to volunteers dumping specs by hand, the way Wacom's decades of upstream support remains the lonely exception.

title: Enterprise-grade AI image generation in 2 seconds is here: Krea 2 Raw and Turbo available as open weights under custom license
blurb: Krea released its frontier image model Krea 2 as open weights on Hugging Face in two variants, Raw and Turbo, with the Turbo version generating images in about 2 seconds via Trajectory Distribution Matching. The custom license keeps usage free for small teams but requires firms over 50 seats to pay for enterprise access, and mandates technical safeguards against illegal content, NCII, and CSAM across all users.

title: Clap Studio creates "the most music-respectful room" in Spook nightclub
blurb: Clap Studio renovated the El Cubo room at Valencia's Spook nightclub by borrowing acoustic principles from "dead rooms," using sound-absorbing surfaces and minimal reflective materials to prioritize music clarity over visual spectacle. The project, a follow-up to the studio's work on sister club Oven, treats acoustics as the primary design constraint rather than an afterthought layered onto a finished interior.

title: Vibe Architects: Agentic Vibe Coders
blurb: Nielsen Norman Group's study of seven nontechnical people using agentic tools like Claude Cowork found "vibe architects" building automation pipelines, multiagent orchestration systems, and team operating systems without understanding how the systems work. The takeaway for designers is that traditional UIs are becoming optional, with several participants treating vibe-coded dashboards as afterthoughts and living instead in chat and Markdown files.

title: AI creative tools startup Krea, which has raised $83M and claims to have 30M users, releases the open weights for its image model Krea 2 under a custom license (Carl Franzen/VentureBeat)
blurb: Krea released the open weights for Krea 2, its image generation model, under a custom license, opening up a tool the startup says reaches 30M users. The move pressures other hosted image models on cost and customization, since teams can now run and fine-tune Krea 2 locally rather than paying per-generation API fees.

title: Adobe’s AI Assistant Wants to Give Photographers More Time for Actual Creative Tasks
blurb: Adobe shipped its Firefly-powered AI Assistant across Premiere, Photoshop, Illustrator, InDesign, and Frame.io, letting users describe edits in plain language and hand off repetitive work like batch background removal, bulk resizing, and clip organization. The agent keeps the user in control of creative decisions while automating production tasks, and its step-by-step guidance could double as a teaching tool for newcomers facing Photoshop's steep learning curve.

title: Curves and colours transform Indian home into "sculptural installation"
blurb: Neel Buddhadev completed Villa Corsica, a 280-square-metre house in suburban Mumbai built around curved walls and saturated color blocks tuned to the city's tropical climate. Designed with Virtue – Architect Cleon Colaco & Associates for a multi-generational family with accessibility needs, the project shows how single-level circulation and sculptural form can coexist without one compromising the other.

title: Heinz blacks out its logo in World Cup stunt
blurb: Heinz Canada responded to FIFA's strict World Cup sponsorship rules by releasing "Unofficial Stadium Ketchup," a bottle with its name and strapline blacked out yet still instantly recognizable as Heinz. The stunt follows similar logo-obscuring plays from Levi's and Gillette, and the covered-up brands are arguably drawing more attention than the official sponsors who paid for placement.

title: ‘Hard Fork’ Live Part 2: Dylan Field on Standing Out in the A.I. Era
blurb: Figma CEO Dylan Field argued on a live "Hard Fork" episode that designers and writers with a distinct creative voice should take public risks now, framing the AI era as an opening rather than a threat. His pitch reframes the standout-or-fade anxiety around AI tooling as a case for putting personal work out there, betting that human voice becomes more valuable as generation gets cheaper.

title: Tátil Design gives global consultancy Vanto Group a seismic rebrand
blurb: Brazilian studio Tátil Design completed a full rebrand for Vanto Group, the global management consultancy formerly known as Insigniam, spanning a new name, identity system, and visual language. The work signals the consultancy's repositioning around organizational transformation, with Tátil leaning on bold geometry and a refreshed wordmark to signal the shift to existing clients.

title: Visualizing "We the People": Bridging the Perception Gap through Pluralistic Data Storytelling
blurb: This paper argues that binary, contrast-heavy data visualizations deepen political polarization by erasing intra-group nuance, and that distribution-focused, interactive "opinion landscapes" are a cheaper, more honest alternative. The authors point to Jigsaw's September 2025 We the People deliberation, which used AI to synthesize input from 2,400 Americans across all 435 districts into maps that surfaced hidden consensus rather than staging us-versus-them conflict.

title: Reader Submitted: An Assistive Communication Ecosystem Enabling Real-Time Deaf-Hearing Conversation
blurb: Dari is a student-designed assistive communication ecosystem built over a 26-week, co-design process with the Deaf community, combining an EMG armband (Woori) that reads sign-language gestures, smart glasses (Sari) that caption speech in the wearer's field of view, and a companion app. The concept's value is its insistence on two-way, real-time exchange that keeps both parties in their preferred mode rather than routing everything through an interpreter or one-way text.

title: The hidden UX of payments
blurb: The author breaks down the invisible design decisions in payment flows, from retry logic on declined cards to the timing of confirmation states, arguing that perceived speed and trust hinge on details users never consciously notice. The piece is a useful reminder that checkout conversion is often won or lost in error handling and microcopy rather than the visible layout.

title: FIFA's World Cup Typography Foul: UI Design Learnings
blurb: FIFA's 2026 World Cup uses a custom display typeface, FWC26 by Monolith, in an ultra-condensed black weight that works for promotional headlines but fails on the broadcast scoreboard, where tight squarish letterforms collapse country tricodes and a slashed zero reads as an eight. The author's fix is a reminder for UI work: reserve condensed display faces for large sizes, and pair them with a wider, legible face for the information layer.

title: Delightful new Warner Bros Animation logo reveal is the perfect antidote to CGI saturation
blurb: Warner Bros debuted a new Animation logo at the Annecy festival, swapping the textured gold WB plate for a flat, matte design with a script "animation" tag and Tweety Pie perched alongside. The accompanying reveal animation traces the mark from hand-drawn sketch to filled color, framing the rebrand as a deliberate lean toward traditional 2D craft over CGI gloss.

title: With its gorgeous screen tailor-made for drawing, the Huawei MatePad Pro Max might just be the tablet market outlaw's finest creation yet
blurb: Huawei released the MatePad Pro Max, a 13.2-inch OLED tablet built for drawing, though Western app-store blocks keep it niche outside creative use.

title: FFmpeg fixes PixelSmash flaw in widely used video decoder
blurb: FFmpeg patched PixelSmash (CVE-2026-8461, CVSS 8.8), a heap out-of-bounds write in the MagicYUV decoder triggered by a malicious AVI, MKV, or MOV file processed through libavcodec. JFrog achieved full RCE against a Jellyfin 10.11.9 server via its automatic library scan; Kodi, OBS, PhotoPrism, Nextcloud, and desktop thumbnail generators are also exposed, with RCE viable when ASLR is disabled or chained around.

title: Eight-Year-Old Samsung KNOX Flaw Exposed Millions of Galaxy Devices to Kernel Attacks
blurb: Samsung patched CVE-2026-20971 (CVSS 7.8), an eight-year-old use-after-free in KNOX affecting nearly every Galaxy device from the S9 to S25, including A-series and both Exynos and Qualcomm variants. LucidBit Labs triggered the race condition from an untrusted app by loading a non-ELF file to bypass KCFI protections, achieving controlled kernel memory corruption; the fix shipped in Samsung's January 2026 update.

title: FFmpeg PixelSmash Flaw Allows RCE on Video Players, Media Servers, NAS Appliances
blurb: FFmpeg's libavcodec contains a heap out-of-bounds write in the MagicYUV decoder (CVE-2026-8461, CVSS 8.8), dubbed PixelSmash by JFrog, exploitable for unauthenticated RCE via a crafted 50KB AVI, MKV, or MOV file with no user privileges required. Because FFmpeg backs most media pipelines, the bug triggers when video players, file-manager thumbnailers, upload-processing servers, NAS appliances, or smart TVs decode the file, enabling zero-click attacks through torrent clients that auto-scan media folders.

title: LastPass confirms data breach in Klue supply chain attack
blurb: LastPass confirmed attackers accessed customer data in its Salesforce environment after the Icarus extortion group stole OAuth tokens from Klue, a third-party market intelligence platform integrated with LastPass's go-to-market systems. Exposed data includes names, phone numbers, email and physical addresses, and CRM records, but products, infrastructure, and customer vaults were unaffected; LastPass has rotated the exposed tokens and warns of phishing from attacker-controlled domains.

title: Anthropic’s Fable 5 Model Jailbroken Within Days
blurb: Anthropic's Fable 5, the guardrailed variant of its Mythos Preview intended to refuse cyberattack-related requests, had those safety restrictions jailbroken within days of release. The bypass undercuts Anthropic's claim that the model could not be used to generate offensive cyber capabilities, and teams evaluating Fable 5 for restricted environments should treat its content filters as defeatable rather than a security control.

title: Researchers Detail DifyTap Flaws in Dify That Could Expose AI Chats Across Tenants
blurb: Zafran Security disclosed four vulnerabilities, collectively named DifyTap, in Dify, the open-source agentic workflow platform, two of them critical (CVE-2026-41948 at CVSS 9.4, CVE-2026-41947 at 9.1) and two requiring no authentication, that allow cross-tenant reads of other customers' AI chats and uploaded files on Dify's multi-tenant cloud. Operators on affected versions should patch immediately and treat prior cross-tenant conversation and document exposure as possible, since the flaws created a covert exfiltration channel.

title: Fake AI Agent Skill Passed Security Scans and Reportedly Reached 26,000 Agents
blurb: Security firm AIR built a fake AI agent skill, brand-landingpage, that passed every scanner it tested (Cisco's, NVIDIA's, skills.sh) and reached roughly 26,000 agents, some on corporate accounts. The skill pointed agents at an external setup page AIR controlled, clean at scan time, then swapped it post-install to deliver a script-running payload that the scanners never saw.

title: ShapedPlugin WordPress Pro Plugins Backdoored in Supply Chain Attack
blurb: Threat actors compromised ShapedPlugin's build and EDD distribution pipeline, backdooring Pro releases of Product Slider Pro for WooCommerce (CVE-2026-49777, CVSS 10.0), Real Testimonials Pro, and Smart Post Show Pro (incident-wide CVE-2026-10735, CVSS 9.8). The loader fetches a self-erasing payload that captures plaintext credentials and 2FA codes, exfiltrates wp-config.php and admin accounts, and drops a web shell; only Pro builds from account.shapedplugin.com are affected.

title: FortiBleed Attackers Turn Firewalls Into Credential Stealers as Heists Persist
blurb: The FortiBleed campaign weaponizes the FortiOS built-in "diagnose sniffer packet" command via a Golang tool called FortigateSniffer, turning compromised FortiGate firewalls into passive credential collectors across 24 authentication protocols. SOCRadar attributes the financially motivated, likely Russian operation to an initial access broker that has harvested 110 million credentials from 430,000 exposed appliances since February, breaching a NATO-aligned defense contractor and prompting a CISA advisory.

title: Microsoft fixes AutoGen Studio flaw that enabled code execution
blurb: Microsoft patched AutoJack, a three-flaw chain in AutoGen Studio that let a malicious webpage drive a developer's browsing agent into running arbitrary PowerShell, Bash, or executables on the host via the unauthenticated localhost MCP WebSocket. Exposure was limited to those building from the main GitHub branch between the MCP plugin commit and the hardening fix; PyPI releases, including autogenstudio 0.4.2.2, were never affected.

title: Jaredfromsubway.eth, Ethereum's Most Active Sandwich Bot, Drained for $7.5M Over the Weekend
blurb: An attacker drained over $7.5 million in WETH, USDC, and USDT from jaredfromsubway.eth, the Ethereum address responsible for roughly 70% of all sandwich attacks on the network, using what Blockaid called a counter-MEV honeypot rather than phishing or a private-key compromise. It is the largest single-event loss publicly tied to an MEV operator, and some proceeds have already moved through Tornado Cash.

title: Cisco Unified CM flaw CVE-2026-20230 now exploited in attacks
blurb: Cisco Unified Communications Manager and Unified CM SME contain an unauthenticated SSRF flaw (CVE-2026-20230, CVSS 8.6) in the WebDialer component that lets attackers write arbitrary files via file:// URIs and escalate to root. Cisco patched it on June 3, and threat-intel firm Defused now reports active exploitation from a single IP; the flaw is not yet in CISA KEV, so patch affected appliances immediately rather than waiting for the listing.

title: Daybreak: Tools for securing every organization in the world
blurb: OpenAI expanded its Daybreak program with new defensive tooling, including an updated Codex Security plugin that discovers and patches vulnerabilities, and the full release of GPT-5.5-Cyber, which scores 85.6% on CyberGym versus 81.8% for GPT-5.5, available through a limited release to trusted defenders. It also launched Patch the Planet with Trail of Bits and HackerOne, with cURL, Go, Python, and 30-plus open-source projects committed to AI-assisted patching.

title: Vulnerability reports are not special anymore
blurb: Filippo Valsorda argues that vulnerability reports no longer warrant the special treatment maintainers have long given them, because LLMs have made the underlying insight and confidentiality cheap. Anyone can run a model to find potential bugs, so the bottleneck has shifted from discovery to triage, where unvetted external reporters add little signal and embargoes buy less time against attackers running the same tooling. The operational takeaway for security teams: weight responsiveness and attribution toward established trust relationships, and invest in triage capacity over inbound report handling.

title: Data Exposure Flaws Threaten Dify AI Platform Used by 1 Million Apps
blurb: Four vulnerabilities dubbed DifyTap in the open-source LLMOps platform Dify, which powers over 1 million applications, enable cross-tenant data theft in multi-tenant deployments, including the critical CVE-2026-41948 (CVSS 9.4) in the plugin daemon and CVE-2026-41947 (CVSS 9.1) in tracing that creates a persistent message-exfiltration channel. Dify 1.14.2 patches all four flaws; operators running multi-tenant instances should update immediately and apply WAF rules to block exploitation.

title: Nearly half of LG smart TV apps contain residential proxy SDKs
blurb: Researchers at Spur scanned 6,038 LG and Samsung smart TV apps and found 2,058 of them, nearly half on LG, bundling residential proxy SDKs from vendors like Bright Data that route strangers' internet traffic through the TV's home connection. The SDKs ask for consent once during setup and keep proxying after the app is closed, often with the proxy company itself acting as publisher, making these devices long-lived, unaudited exit nodes on home networks.

title: 29-Year-Old Squid Proxy Bug 'Squidbleed' Can Leak Cleartext HTTP Requests
blurb: Squidbleed (CVE-2026-47729), a 29-year-old heap over-read in Squid's FTP directory-listing parser, lets any client already permitted to use the proxy leak another user's cleartext HTTP request, including Authorization headers and session tokens, by pointing the proxy at an attacker-controlled FTP server. The bug is live in Squid's default config and proof-of-concept code is public; patch and verify the guard lands in FtpGateway.cc rather than trusting version numbers, since distros ship their own backports.

title: RFC 9958 - Post-Quantum Cryptography for Engineers
blurb: RFC 9958 is a new IETF informational document that explains post-quantum cryptography to engineers, detailing how a cryptographically relevant quantum computer would break today's RSA and ECC public-key algorithms and what the migration requires. Unlike prior crypto updates, the shift to PQC may demand significant protocol redesign rather than a drop-in swap, given the larger key sizes and different operational properties of the new algorithms. Useful grounding for teams scoping their own crypto-agility and migration roadmaps.

title: OpenAI launches new security tools and updates GPT-5.5-Cyber
blurb: OpenAI launched a suite of defender tools, expanding Daybreak from bug discovery into patch automation via an updated Codex Security plugin and a limited release of GPT-5.5-Cyber, gated to verified defenders. Codex Security scans full codebases, commits, or diffs and exports findings as SARIF and CodeQL; GPT-5.5-Cyber scored 85.6% on CyberGym and 39.5% on ExploitGym, beating GPT-5.5 on both.

title: Anthropic’s powerful Mythos AI reportedly breached ‘almost all’ NSA classified systems within a few hours during red-team test — report sheds more light on the U.S. government's sudden ban on the flagship models
blurb: Anthropic's Mythos 5 model breached "almost all" NSA classified systems within hours during an authorized June 11 red-team exercise, according to NSA chief Gen. Joshua Rudd as relayed by Sen. Mark Warner and reported by The Economist. The result preceded a June 12 federal directive barring foreign nationals from Fable 5 and Mythos 5, prompting Anthropic to disable both models globally rather than enforce nationality-based access controls.

title: Cisco Unified CM Flaw Exploited After PoC Reveals File-Write Path to Root
blurb: CVE-2026-20230 (CVSS 8.6), an unauthenticated SSRF in Cisco Unified CM and Unified CM SME, is under active exploitation after a public PoC showed how to chain file:// writes into root code execution, with Defused Cyber observing file-write payloads landing on its decoys. Exploitation requires the non-default WebDialer service; patch to 14SU6 or 15SU5, or disable WebDialer until you can.

title: OpenAI Launches Daybreak To Automate Vulnerability Patching
blurb: OpenAI launched Daybreak, a system that automatically finds and patches software vulnerabilities by generating and validating fixes before they reach production. The tool aims to close the gap between disclosure and remediation, though security teams will want to gate its auto-generated patches behind human review and regression testing before trusting them on production code.

title: Secret Network's Axelar Bridge Drained $4.67M via Infinite-Mint Flaw
blurb: Secret Network's Axelar bridge lost $4.67M after an attacker exploited a CW20-ICS20 contract that had two validation checks commented out, letting forged IBC packets mint unbacked wrapped tokens that were then redeemed for real escrowed assets. The flaw dated to the contract's March 2023 deployment and survived a March 2026 migration; Secret's default transaction encryption hid the depletion for seven days until a failed transfer surfaced it.

title: FortiBleed campaign used custom FortiGate sniffer to steal credentials
blurb: SOCRadar reports that the FortiBleed campaign has targeted more than 430,000 FortiGate firewalls since February 2026, deploying a Golang tool called FortigateSniffer that abuses the legitimate FortiOS "diagnose sniffer packet" command to harvest credentials from RADIUS, NTLM, Kerberos, and LDAP traffic. The initial-access broker behind it gains admin access via credential stuffing and brute-force, so defenders should rotate FortiGate credentials and audit SSH sessions for sniffer activity.

title: White House drastically shortens deadline for dropping quantum-vulnerable crypto
blurb: A new White House executive order, "Securing the Nation against Advanced Cryptographic Attacks," requires high-value assets and high-impact systems to adopt post-quantum key establishment by December 31, 2030, and quantum-safe signatures by December 31, 2031. The deadlines move the previous 2035 target up four to five years, following recent research showing cryptographically relevant quantum computers are cheaper to build than estimated and tightened timelines from Google and Cloudflare.

title: New macOS ClickFix attack silently mounts DMGs to push infostealer
blurb: A new macOS ClickFix campaign tricks users into pasting a Terminal command that silently downloads, mounts via hdiutil, and auto-launches a DMG carrying the Atomic macOS Stealer (AMOS), per Palo Alto Networks Unit 42. The malware harvests browser credentials, Keychain entries, crypto wallet data, and messaging app contents, so defenders should alert on `hdiutil attach -nobrowse` and curl-to-/tmp activity and block svs-verificationdate[.]beer.

title: Fortinet: Oracle and other goliaths in firewall fallout as GPU 'democratization' blamed for mass hack
blurb: The "Fortibleed" credential-harvesting campaign compromised roughly 74,000 public-facing FortiGate devices, about half of all internet-exposed Fortinet firewalls, leaking enterprise sign-in details from Oracle, Comcast, Samsung, Foxconn, Siemens, PwC, Accenture, government agencies, and a NATO defense contractor. Attackers used a rented 45-GPU cluster to crack stolen passwords and generate credential variants at scale, so affected orgs should terminate all admin sessions and rotate every credential touched by exposed appliances.

title: Microsoft discovers new lightweight backdoor that steals cryptocurrency
blurb: Microsoft disclosed Crypto Clipper, a self-propagating worm that spreads via .lnk files on USB drives and monitors clipboards for wallet addresses and seed phrases, exfiltrating stolen credentials and timed screenshots over Tor through a local SOCKS5 proxy. By bundling a portable Tor client and IP-less C2 with remote code execution, the malware doubles as a lightweight backdoor, so defenders should restrict USB autorun, block unauthorized Tor traffic, and monitor for clipboard-hijacking behavior.

title: FortiBleed Targeted FortiGate Firewalls in 110 Million-Credential Harvesting Operation
blurb: FortiBleed, a credential-harvesting campaign attributed to a financially motivated Russian-speaking access broker, has targeted over 430,000 FortiGate firewalls since February 2026 using FortigateSniffer, a Golang tool that abuses the FortiOS diagnose sniffer packet command to capture cleartext and hashed authentication traffic. SOCRadar estimates 659 harvesting pipelines yielded over 110 million credentials between May 31 and June 15, with SMBs and IT-services providers in the US and India hit hardest.

title: Prompt Injection as Role Confusion
blurb: This writeup argues prompt injection is fundamentally a role-confusion problem: an LLM receives system prompts, user messages, tool outputs, and its own reasoning as one undifferentiated token stream, and the role tags meant to partition that stream are a weak signal the model can be made to misread. The authors use this framing to construct new attacks, explain mechanistic-interpretability findings, and predict when injections succeed, arguing that hardening role separation matters more than patching individual payloads.

title: Scattered Spider Hackers Plead Guilty on Day 1 of Trial
blurb: Two members of the cybercrime group Scattered Spider, Thalha Jubair, 20, and 18-year-old Owen Flowers, pleaded guilty in the UK to charges tied to the August 2024 cyberattack that crippled Transport for London. Jubair also faces a US indictment alleging involvement in 120 network intrusions across 47 entities that netted at least $115M in ransom payments.

title: GitHub Updates actions/checkout to Block Common Pwn Request Attack Patterns
blurb: GitHub updated actions/checkout to refuse fork pull request code by default in pull_request_target and workflow_run workflows, blocking the most common pwn request pattern that lets attackers steal the privileged GITHUB_TOKEN and repository secrets. The default-deny behavior shipped in v7 on June 18 and backports to all supported major versions on July 16. Workflows that genuinely need fork checkout must explicitly set allow-unsafe-pr-checkout to true, so audit any pipeline relying on that combination before the backport breaks it.

title: AryStinger Malware Infects 4,300 Legacy Routers to Build Reconnaissance Proxy Network
blurb: AryStinger malware has infected at least 4,300 legacy routers, exploiting CVE-2013-3307 in Linksys and CVE-2016-5681 in D-Link models to build a distributed reconnaissance and proxy network rather than a DDoS botnet. QiAnXin's XLab reports the pool is mostly D-Link DIR-850L devices, skewing to South Korea and China, with a second Go-based strain hitting QNAP NAS boxes via the patched CVE-2025-11837.

title: BeyondTrust, LastPass Impacted by Klue-Salesforce Incident
blurb: A threat actor calling itself Icarus used a compromised legacy credential to access sales-intelligence vendor Klue, mint OAuth tokens, and bulk-exfiltrate connected Salesforce instances, hitting LastPass, BeyondTrust, and over a dozen other firms. Exposure was limited to CRM business-contact and sales data with no internal systems breached; Salesforce and Gong have disabled the Klue integration, and affected orgs should rotate exposed OAuth tokens and audit third-party SaaS connections.

title: Understanding the (In)Security of Vibe-Coded Applications
blurb: This paper systematically audits a large corpus of real-world vibe-coded applications, those built primarily through natural-language interaction with AI agents, and finds recurring vulnerability classes distinct from conventional development: placeholder logic, unfiltered input, and exposed secrets. The authors trace these to systematic agent limitations such as memory loss, locally optimized objectives, and insufficient security knowledge. Better models and prompting reduce vulnerability incidence but do not eliminate the underlying risk, so defenders should treat AI-delegated code as needing full review and secret scanning before deployment.

title: Hackers Exploiting Cisco Unified CM Vulnerability
blurb: CVE-2026-20230, a critical unauthenticated flaw in Cisco Unified Communications Manager, is now being exploited in the wild, with Defused observing file:// write payloads landing on its decoys and SSD Secure Disclosure publishing RCE PoC code. Cisco patched it on June 3; exploitation requires the non-default WebDialer service, so admins should patch, confirm WebDialer status, and treat exposed on-prem call managers as targeted.

title: Tata Electronics confirms cyberattack as hackers leak data
blurb: Tata Electronics, the Tata Group division that manufactures iPhones and components for Apple, confirmed a cybersecurity incident after the World Leaks extortion group leaked data allegedly stolen from its systems, including internal Apple component schematics, PCB designs, material specifications, and SDK files. World Leaks, a rebrand of the defunct Hunters International ransomware group that now runs pure data-extortion, has also hit Dell and Nike, so affected suppliers should assume exfiltration and review shared proprietary data exposure.

title: The post-quantum EO is an important milestone. Now it’s time to get to work
blurb: Trump signed Executive Order 14409 on June 22, setting a December 31, 2030, deadline for federal agencies to migrate sensitive systems to post-quantum encryption and a 2031 deadline for post-quantum authentication. Cloudflare, which moved its own full-PQ target to 2029 after recent research breakthroughs, notes that encryption migration is well underway but authentication has barely started. Defenders should inventory RSA and ECC dependencies now and prioritize the authentication transition.

title: Linux and Secure Boot certificate expiration (2025)
blurb: The Microsoft 2011 key used to sign the shim first-stage UEFI bootloader expires in September, after which Microsoft will stop signing shim with it, breaking Secure Boot installs of new Linux distributions on affected systems. A replacement key has existed since 2023 but may not be present in firmware and can require a vendor firmware update that may never ship. Defenders running Secure Boot fleets should audit firmware key databases now and push fwupd/LVFS updates before the cutover.

title: Trump sets post-quantum crypto deadlines, launches broader federal quantum initiative
blurb: Trump signed two executive orders Monday setting federal migration deadlines for post-quantum cryptography, directing agencies to inventory cryptographic assets, and signaling future compliance requirements for government contractors. The orders cite a "harvest now, decrypt later" threat in which adversaries collect encrypted data today to decrypt once cryptographically relevant quantum computers exist, pressuring contractors and critical infrastructure operators to demonstrate PQC readiness.

title: Scattered Spider members plead guilty to hacking Transport for London
blurb: Two Scattered Spider members, Thalha Jubair (20) and Owen Flowers (18), pleaded guilty at Woolwich Crown Court to the September 2024 breach of Transport for London, which stole Oyster refund data and forced password resets for all 28,000 staff. The NCA pegged damages at £29M ($38.3M) and linked Flowers to additional intrusions at SSM Health and Sutter Health, underscoring that early law-enforcement engagement aided the prosecution.

title: JaredFromSubway MEV bot hacked in $15 million crypto theft
blurb: The JaredFromSubway MEV bot lost $15M after an attacker deployed fake liquidity pools and tokens that appeared as profitable opportunities, tricking the bot's automated execution logic into granting ERC-20 spending approvals to attacker-controlled helper contracts. The attacker accumulated up to 92.16 WETH in unused allowances over staged test transactions, then drained WETH, USDC, and USDT via transferFrom; bounty offers of up to $7.5M for partial return have gone unanswered.

title: Following user outcry, AMD reinstates memory encryption in consumer CPUs
blurb: AMD will reinstate Transparent Secure Memory Encryption (TSME) on consumer Ryzen processors after silently stripping the full-memory encryption via firmware update, drawing widespread user backlash. The protection defends against cold boot and other physical-access attacks, and its quiet removal was undetectable on Windows and required significant technical effort to confirm on Linux.

title: Anthropic’s Mythos found flaws in classified US systems during a government test
blurb: Anthropic's Mythos surfaced vulnerabilities in classified US government systems within hours during an NSA red-team exercise under Project Glasswing, though officials confirmed the model found the flaws rather than exploited them or breached any live network. The episode arrives amid contradictory signals from Washington, which authorized NSA use of Mythos on classified networks while forcing Anthropic to disable Mythos and Fable 5 worldwide on June 12.

title: 'Cordyceps': Mushrooming Malicious Pull Requests Threaten Developer Workflows
blurb: Researchers at Novee disclosed "Cordyceps," a class of CI/CD weakness in which malicious pull requests trigger privileged automated workflows to steal signing keys and access tokens, enabling command injection and supply-chain compromise. A single scan flagged 654 repositories as potentially exploitable and confirmed 300 fully exploitable, including Azure Sentinel, Google's AI Agent Development Kit, Apache Doris, Cloudflare's Workers SDK, and PSF's Black.

title: A public Sentry key is all it takes to hijack Claude Code, Cursor, and Codex
blurb: Tenet Security's Threat Labs disclosed agentjacking, an attack that abuses Sentry's public DSN write-only credential to plant a malicious fake error report that AI coding agents like Claude Code, Cursor, and Codex execute as instructions over MCP, yielding code execution on a developer's machine. Because the agent cannot distinguish attacker-controlled data from a command, no config change fixes it. Scope MCP tool output as untrusted and sandbox agent execution.

title: Cyclic Denoising Reveals Ultrastable Memories in Diffusion Models
blurb: Researchers introduced cyclic denoising, an unconditioned extraction attack that repeatedly noises and denoises image diffusion model outputs to surface "ultrastable" attractors, many of which are memorized training images including stock photos, brand watermarks, and web-crawl artifacts. The technique works on Stable Diffusion v1.4 and a pixel-space DDPM using only sampler-level control, with no gradients, weights, prompts, or prior knowledge of training data, raising privacy and copyright-compliance exposure for deployed generative models.

title: Mythos discovers 'Squidbleed,' a memory leak that's gone undetected since Clinton era
blurb: Mythos, Anthropic's AI security agent, discovered "Squidbleed," a long-dormant memory-disclosure flaw in the Squid caching proxy that leaks adjacent heap contents to remote requesters and has sat unpatched in the codebase since the 1990s. Defenders running Squid on internet-facing caches should apply the upstream fix once available and treat any exposed instance as a potential source of leaked credentials, session tokens, and cached response data.

title: Red-Teaming the Agentic Red-Team
blurb: This paper presents the first in-depth security analysis of widely used agentic offensive-security tools and finds that most share design flaws letting an active adversary exfiltrate API keys, establish persistence, and fully compromise the operator's machine, even inside a sandboxed container. The authors map a full kill chain from LLM manipulation through lateral movement, guardrail bypass, and sandbox escape, then propose architectural design principles that mitigate the disclosed paths. Treat your red-team agent as attacker-reachable infrastructure.

title: Poisoned Playbooks: Demystifying Knowledge Poisoning Effects on AI Security Agents
blurb: This paper shows that a single poisoned write-up injected into public-style security knowledge sources can systematically alter the behavior of RAG-based AI security agents, steering them toward incorrect exploit reasoning across 11 CTF challenges, 11 real-world CVEs, and three frontier LLM families. The authors find that verification prompting and multi-source retrieval reduce poison adoption only when stronger refuting evidence exists, leaving agents exposed under sparse-evidence and zero-day conditions.

title: N.S.A. Lost Access to Powerful A.I. Model Amid Anthropic Dispute
blurb: The NSA lost access to an advanced Anthropic AI model used for cybersecurity work amid an ongoing dispute between the company and the Trump administration. The episode highlights the government's growing reliance on frontier AI for defensive operations even as it remains at odds with one of the leading U.S. developers over how its models can be deployed.

title: DifyTap Bugs Let Attackers 'Wiretap' AI Chat Histories
blurb: Zafran disclosed DifyTap, four vulnerabilities in the open-source Dify AI orchestration platform, including a tracing hijack (CVE-2026-41947, CVSS 9.1) and a Plugin Daemon path traversal (CVE-2026-41948, CVSS 9.4) that let attackers exfiltrate private AI chat histories and cross-tenant files. Version 1.14.2 patches three flaws; operators should build the latest GitHub release to fix all four and apply WAF rules for CVE-2026-41948.

title: WhatsApp phishing attack uses fake business docs to hack PCs
blurb: A WhatsApp campaign tracked by Kaspersky is spreading obfuscated VBScript files from compromised accounts, disguised as financial and billing documents to trick contacts into executing them. The chain disables UAC via Registry edits and silently installs the legitimate ManageEngine Endpoint Central RMM tool pointed at attacker servers, granting full remote administration across victims in Brazil, India, Mexico, the UK, and at least seven other countries.

title: Security shops among the 'hundreds' of Klue hack victims
blurb: Attackers used a compromised legacy credential tied to a Klue integration service to mint OAuth tokens into customers' Salesforce instances, exfiltrating CRM data including business contacts, price quotes, and sales messaging from hundreds of the market-intelligence firm's customers. Security vendors including Huntress, Snyk, HackerOne, Tanium, and Recorded Future were among the victims, and the group Icarus claimed the June 11 breach, so affected orgs should revoke Klue's OAuth grants and audit Salesforce access logs.

title: Securing LLM-Agent Long-Term Memory Against Poisoning: Non-Malleable, Origin-Bound Authority with Machine-Checked Guarantees
blurb: This paper shows that LLM-agent long-term memory is vulnerable to poisoning, where an attacker plants untrusted content in one session to steer a payment, config change, or exfiltration in a later one. The authors prove that content- and lineage-based defenses are unsound because attackers can launder a malicious origin through agent summarization, trusted-tool echo, and manufactured corroboration, reaching up to 68% attack success. Their construction, TMA-NM, enforces write-time origin binding and hit 0% attack success across eight frontier models at full utility.

title: Flock-Powered Police Chiefs Stalking Women Shows Why Warrants Are Needed
blurb: The police chief of Holiday Hills, Illinois, was arrested June 18, 2026, and charged with two felony counts of official misconduct for using Prairie Grove's Flock license plate readers and the LEADS database to track six people, including three former romantic partners. The Institute for Justice has documented at least 18 similar cases of officers abusing Flock LPR, fueling a constitutional challenge that argues such tracking should require a warrant.

title: Red-Teaming after Mythos — Zico Kolter & Matt Fredrikson, Gray Swan
blurb: In a Latent Space interview, Gray Swan cofounders Zico Kolter and Matt Fredrikson argue that AI security is a distinct discipline from traditional cybersecurity, because agents like Codex and Claude Code expose a new exploit class via indirect prompt injection. They detail Gray Swan's tooling, including Shade, an automated red-teaming model they say now outperforms humans at breaking AI systems, and contend that frontier models do not become inherently safer as they scale.

title: Crypto Heist Fueled by Elaborate Fake Reputation-Boosting Campaign
blurb: Check Point uncovered a global cryptocurrency-theft campaign that builds fake credibility across GitHub, SourceForge, YouTube, and a WordPress phishing hub to push a Rust-based clipboard hijacker with Windows and macOS variants. The malware swaps copied wallet addresses for Bitcoin, Ethereum, Monero, Binance Chain, and Solana and maintains persistence, so defenders should flag clipboard-monitoring behavior and treat "crypto advantage" tools promoted via coordinated reviews as malicious.

title: THORChain Resumes Trading After Month-Long Halt From $10.7M Exploit
blurb: THORChain resumed full operations after a six-week halt triggered by a May 15 compromise of one of its six Asgard vaults, which drained $10.7M across Bitcoin, Ethereum, BNB Chain, and Base before the network stopped signing. Security firm V12 alleges it reported a near-identical bug weeks earlier that was silently patched without a bounty, and says it plans to release exploit code for additional unpatched vulnerabilities.

title: Cloudflare teams up with big browsers to help websites tell welcome from unwelcome visitors
blurb: Cloudflare partnered with major browser vendors on Web Bot Auth, a standard that uses cryptographically signed HTTP message signatures to let sites verify which automated agents are legitimate rather than relying on spoofable user-agent strings or IP allowlists. Operators can register signing keys so welcome crawlers and AI agents authenticate themselves, giving defenders a cleaner signal to block unverified bot traffic without breaking sanctioned automation.

title: Trump executive orders speed up post-quantum migration, boost industry
blurb: Trump signed two executive orders Monday accelerating federal adoption of NIST-vetted post-quantum encryption ahead of the current 2035 deadline and directing federal financing toward the domestic quantum computing industry. Civilian agencies that miss the new migration deadline must report to OMB explaining why, following a May Commerce announcement of more than $2B in CHIPS Act incentives for nine quantum companies.

title: Scope of Salesforce Attacks Expands as Icarus Leaks Data
blurb: The extortion group Icarus expanded its Salesforce data-theft campaign by abusing OAuth tokens stolen in a breach of market-intelligence vendor Klue, hitting LastPass, Huntress, HackerOne, Recorded Future, Snyk, Tanium, Jamf, and others. Affected organizations should suspend Klue integration, rotate all exposed API and OAuth tokens, and audit Salesforce instances for unauthorized access, as Icarus has signaled more victims are coming.

title: 오픈AI, 오픈소스 보안 강화 나선다…‘패치 더 플래닛’ 프로젝트 출범
blurb: OpenAI launched Patch the Planet, an AI-based program built with Trail of Bits that uses its models and Codex Security to find and fix vulnerabilities in widely used open-source projects including Python, Go, cURL, Sigstore, NATS Server, aiohttp, and pyca/cryptography. Each finding passes human review to filter false positives before reaching maintainers, and the program has surfaced hundreds of issues and merged dozens of patches so far, with HackerOne and Calif assisting on triage and disclosure.

title: Cloudflare teams up with Chrome, Firefox, and Edge on a privacy-first anti-bot protocol
blurb: Cloudflare, Mozilla, Google, Microsoft, and Shopify are co-developing PACT (Private Access Control Tokens), a protocol that issues anonymous tokens proving a visitor is human or an authorized agent, replacing CAPTCHAs and forced logins without tracking users or reconstructing browsing history. The group plans to submit it for formal standardization as Cloudflare Radar data shows bots now generate roughly 58% of global HTTP requests, overtaking human traffic.

title: Windows 11 KB5095093 update rolls out new Point-in-Time restore feature
blurb: Microsoft released the KB5095093 optional preview update for Windows 11 24H2 and 25H2, which introduces Point-in-Time Restore, a feature that rolls back the OS, apps, and files to a prior state in minutes using VSS-based restore points stored locally. Consumer devices capture snapshots every 24 hours and retain them for 72, while enterprise licenses can configure shorter snapshot intervals and longer retention for faster recovery from compromise or misconfiguration.

title: PowerFuzz: Power-Based Black-Box Firmware Fuzzing
blurb: PowerFuzz is a black-box firmware fuzzing framework that uses power side-channel measurements instead of binary instrumentation, reconstructing a high-level control-flow graph from power traces to guide the fuzzer toward unexplored branches. Across three embedded platforms and ten firmware benchmarks, it reached branch coverage within 13.5% of gray-box fuzzers and beat state-of-the-art black-box fuzzers by up to 22%, making it usable against closed firmware with no source access.

title: FirmCure:Towards Autonomous and Adaptive Rehosting of Linux-Based Firmware
blurb: FirmCure is an LLM-driven framework for full-system rehosting of Linux-based firmware, automating the initialization and runtime fixes that previously required expert intervention for custom IoT devices. Across 21 firmware images from 10 vendors and 5 architectures, it hit a 100% network port opening rate and 90.5% service interactivity, reproduced known vulnerabilities, and surfaced new flaws.

title: An update on FortiBleed — what's happening with victim orgs
blurb: Attackers exploiting FortiBleed have backdoored roughly 75,000 Fortinet FortiGate firewalls, exporting device configs and offline-cracking the password hashes to harvest plaintext credentials now being sold on forums as FortiVPN client logins. CloudSEK confirms internal compromise at around 1,000 organizations, so defenders should check published victim lists, rebuild affected appliances, rotate IPsec keys and certificates, and re-create admin accounts with MFA.

title: Trump Order Sets 2030 Deadline for Federal Post-Quantum Crypto Migration
blurb: Trump signed an executive order on June 22 (EO 14409) requiring federal agencies to migrate high-value assets to post-quantum cryptography by December 31, 2030, with digital signatures following by December 31, 2031, pulling the prior 2035 target forward to counter "harvest now, decrypt later" attacks. The deadlines align with NIST's FIPS 203, 204, and 205 standards and extend to covered contractors, who must meet the same PQC algorithms by the 2030 mark.

title: Healthtech firm Xolis suffers data breach impacting 1.4 million people
blurb: Xsolis, a healthtech firm whose Dragonfly platform serves over 600 hospitals and insurers, disclosed a breach exposing data on 1,396,519 people after a January 20 phishing attack gave attackers access to its network. Compromised files included Social Security numbers, dates of birth, health insurance details, and medical treatment information; Xsolis has reset passwords, expanded monitoring, and is notifying affected individuals by mail.

title: PHANTOM: A Large-Scale Dataset of Multimodal Adversarial Attacks for Vision-Language Models
blurb: PHANTOM is an open-source dataset of 47,524 pre-generated multimodal adversarial attacks against vision-language models, spanning 7,826 harmful intents across 10 categories and 55 subcategories. The authors built it from state-of-the-art attack strategies in recent literature to let teams stress-test VLM guardrails without bearing the compute cost of generating attacks themselves. Defenders deploying VLMs can use it to benchmark robustness and tune detection before adversaries probe production systems.

title: Apple patches Beats Studio Buds flaw that could turn earbuds into a wiretap
blurb: Apple patched CVE-2025-20701, an authentication flaw in Airoha SoCs used in Beats Studio Buds that lets an attacker within Bluetooth range connect to earbuds in pairing mode and listen through the microphone before the user completes pairing. Beats Firmware Update 1B211 rolls out automatically once the earbuds connect to an iPhone, iPad, or Mac; exploitation requires proximity and specialized tooling, limiting it to targeted rather than opportunistic attacks.

title: OpenAI Expands Daybreak With GPT-5.5-Cyber to Help Defenders Patch Security Flaws
blurb: OpenAI released GPT-5.5-Cyber to trusted defenders under its Daybreak initiative, pairing it with an updated Codex Security plugin that runs deep scans, validates scanner and bug-bounty findings, and generates codebase-specific patches for review at scale. It also launched Patch the Planet with Trail of Bits to harden open-source projects including cURL, Python, Sigstore, and the Go project.

title: AutoSpec: Safety Rule Evolution for LLM Agents via Inductive Logic Programming
blurb: AutoSpec is a framework that automatically evolves expert-written safety rules for LLM agents using counterexample-guided inductive synthesis paired with inductive logic programming, mining false-positive and false-negative counterexamples from annotated traces to learn which predicates discriminate safe from unsafe agent actions. Across 291 code-execution and embodied-agent traces it raised rule F1 to 0.98 and 0.93, cut false positives by up to 94%, and converged in 4-5 iterations while keeping rules human-readable and auditable.

title: Patch the Planet: a Daybreak initiative to support open source maintainers
blurb: OpenAI launched Patch the Planet, a Daybreak initiative built with Trail of Bits that pairs AI-assisted vulnerability research using OpenAI's models and Codex Security with human review to find and patch flaws in critical open-source projects. Initial participants include cURL, pyca/cryptography, aiohttp, Sigstore, NATS Server, and the Go and Python projects, with Trail of Bits, HackerOne, and Calif handling triage, patch development, and coordinated disclosure.

title: You have got to be KDDI-ng – Japanese telco exposes 14.2 million managed email credentials
blurb: Japanese telco KDDI exposed 14.2 million managed email credentials, including plaintext or recoverable passwords, in its hosted mail service. Organizations relying on KDDI-managed mailboxes should force password resets across affected accounts, revoke any reused credentials elsewhere, and monitor for credential-stuffing and business-email-compromise activity tied to the leaked addresses.

title: Ukraine puts its Russian war trophies online for allies to pick apart
blurb: Ukraine published a public database of captured Russian military hardware, inviting allied governments and defense researchers to analyze the recovered electronics, weapons systems, and embedded components. The trove offers Western intelligence and supply-chain analysts a direct look at the foreign chips and commercial parts Russia relies on, exposing sanctions-evasion routes and the provenance of components inside fielded systems.

title: AWS Continuum offers devs help with securing code
blurb: AWS released Continuum, a service that continuously discovers, validates, and remediates vulnerabilities across first-party and third-party code, proposing fixes through existing dev workflows and adding STRIDE threat modeling generated from source or design docs. An "enforce mode" lets it autonomously patch code lapses once it learns an environment's guardrails, and it inherits penetration-testing and code-scanning capabilities from AWS Security Agent.

title: Dragos Unveils AI for OT Security
blurb: Dragos released EmberAI, an OT-specific security assistant built on its decade-old Intelligence Fabric dataset that lets analysts run plain-language queries to correlate threat intel, asset, vulnerability, and network data within their own operational context. The tool runs inside the customer-controlled Dragos platform so data never leaves the environment, keeps every recommendation auditable, and ships generally available shortly after Accenture took a majority stake in Dragos.

title: Court rules SAVE database illegal, orders it dismantled
blurb: A federal court ruled the Trump administration's SAVE national voter database illegal and ordered it dismantled, finding it violated the Privacy Act, the Social Security Act, and the Administrative Procedure Act. Judge Sparkle Sooknanan vacated the system after records showed agencies knowingly combined unreliable citizenship data from multiple databases, resulting in eligible voters being wrongly flagged as non-citizens and removed from the rolls.

title: Tata cyberattack allegedly exposes confidential Apple documents
blurb: Tata Electronics confirmed a cyberattack in which the ransomware group World Leaks exfiltrated over 630GB across more than 200,000 files, including component design specs for customers Apple and Tesla, employee passport copies, and years of email and event logs. The data has been on the dark web since at least June 10, Tata has received a ransom demand, and Apple is running a full analysis; manufacturing partners should treat shared design documents as exposed.

title: Cloudflare forms pact with browser giants to protect your privacy with tokens
blurb: Cloudflare partnered with Mozilla, Google, and Microsoft on a proposed web standard called Private Access Tokens (PACT), which issues anonymous "personhood" tokens that browsers present to other sites as proof a visitor is human, without revealing identity or browsing history. The standard aims to replace cookie tracking and CAPTCHA as agentic AI traffic grows, distinguishing human users from autonomous agents acting on their behalf.

title: Accenture snaps up majority stake in Dragos, acquires runZero and NetRise in critical infrastructure security push
blurb: Accenture acquired a majority stake in OT security firm Dragos plus runZero and NetRise in a roughly $4.175B critical-infrastructure security push. Dragos stays independent under CEO Robert M. Lee, combining its threat-detection platform with runZero's exposure assessment and NetRise's device and supply-chain security; the three generate about $208M in annual recurring revenue, up 53% year-over-year.

title: What we call "age verification" is actually mass surveillance
blurb: Cory Doctorow argues that online "age verification" mandates are mass surveillance in disguise, requiring everyone to submit to fine-grained tracking of their activity to prove they are adults. He contends the push unites anti-Big Tech campaigners and the conspiratorial right as unwitting allies of the surveillance-advertising industry, which gains a legal mandate that makes opting out of tracking effectively illegal.

title: Okta expands Cross App Access ecosystem to secure AI agent connections
blurb: Okta said more than 25 software makers, including Anthropic's Claude, Slack, Atlassian, Cloudflare, and Cursor, have adopted its Cross App Access framework, which routes AI agent connections to enterprise apps through a company's identity controls. Built as an OAuth extension and now an official authorization layer for MCP, XAA aims to replace static API keys and unseen consent screens that leave agents with permanent standing privileges.

title: Kops: Safely Extending the eBPF Compilation Pipeline with Native Operations
blurb: Kops is an extension interface that lets userspace compilers and kernel modules add new eBPF operations without touching the kernel core, pairing each operation's verifier-checked proof sequence with a native JIT emit that is the only addition to the trusted computing base. Its EInsn operation set, with Lean 4 correctness proofs, speeds up eBPF microbenchmarks by up to 24% and production applications by up to 12% on x86-64 and ARM64.

title: Enhanced security monitoring with expanded Admin password reset alerts
blurb: Google Workspace expanded its admin alerting to cover all administrator password resets, replacing the narrower "Super Admin password reset" rule and turning the new alert on by default for every Workspace customer. Any custom recipient lists carry over automatically, and the gradual rollout began June 22 with no end-user impact and no action required from admins.

title: DoJ Seizes Huione Cloud Account Tied to Cyber Scam Money Laundering
blurb: The DoJ seized a cloud computing account hosting backend infrastructure for subsidiaries of Cambodia-based HuiOne Group, including HuiOne Guarantee, a Telegram marketplace that moved billions in proceeds from Southeast Asian scam centers between 2021 and 2025. Treasury concurrently sanctioned nine individuals and 26 entities tied to Prince Group, targeting infrastructure that sold money laundering, phishing kits, and deepfake impersonation tools used in cryptocurrency investment fraud.

title: Password manager maker LastPass says hackers stole customer support case data during Klue breach
blurb: LastPass disclosed that attackers stole customer names, phone numbers, email and physical addresses, and customer support case data through a breach at its market research partner Klue, the same supply-chain compromise that also hit HackerOne, Recorded Future, and Tanium. LastPass says its own infrastructure and encrypted password vaults were untouched, but support tickets have historically contained credentials and identity documents, so affected users should watch for targeted phishing.

title: LastPass notifies users of yet another data breach
blurb: LastPass is notifying users that a breach at market research partner Klue exposed CRM data, including customer names, phone numbers, email addresses, and support case records, though encrypted password vaults were not affected. LastPass revoked the partner's access, rotated exposed API tokens, and published attacker IPs and sender domains; defenders should hunt for those IOCs and brace for phishing using the stolen contact data.

title: A Comparison of Kubernetes Compliance Standards and Configuration Scanners
blurb: This paper benchmarks eight Kubernetes hardening guidelines into 79 configuration recommendations, then evaluates ten popular static config scanners against them. The authors find substantial gaps in which issues each guideline and scanner covers, plus inconsistent severity scoring and ranking across tools. Teams relying on a single scanner to gate cluster deployments should expect blind spots and reconcile findings across multiple tools rather than trusting any one scanner's prioritization.

title: ComputeFHE: A Privacy-Preserving General-Purpose Computation Library
blurb: ComputeFHE is an open-source C++ library, built on OpenFHE and the TFHE cryptosystem, that lets developers write privacy-preserving applications on encrypted data using familiar imperative code, with encrypted integer and fixed-point types plus arithmetic, comparison, conditional, and oblivious array-access operations. Its optimized ALU architecture using FHE-friendly logic primitives cuts bootstrapping operations to deliver up to 3.9x speedups, and a simulation mode estimates circuit complexity and bootstrapping cost without running actual cryptographic computations.

title: Cisco Acquires WideField To Secure AI Agents And Non-Human Identities
blurb: Cisco announced plans to acquire WideField Security, a 2023-founded startup focused on visibility and detection across human, non-human, and AI-agent identities, and will fold it into its Splunk business.
