The deal, undisclosed in value, follows Cisco's Astrix Security and Galileo acquisitions and targets identity telemetry correlation for agentic AI SOC operations.

title: Justice Department seizes infrastructure used by cyber scam and criminal marketplace
blurb: The Justice Department seized a cloud computing account hosting backend infrastructure for the Cambodia-based Huione Group's Huione Guarantee marketplace, which prosecutors say laundered billions in proceeds from Southeast Asian scam centers, stolen card data, and malware-enabled thefts. Treasury concurrently sanctioned nine people and 26 entities linked to the Prince Group and added H-Pay Service as a successor entity to its earlier Huione restrictions.

title: HelpBench: Assessing the Ability of LLMs to Provide Privacy, Safety, and Security Advice
blurb: HelpBench is a benchmark of 450 real-world questions, each with accuracy-and-tone rubrics, that scores how well LLMs answer digital privacy, safety, and security queries, with an auto-rater applied across 18 frontier models. Models averaged 82%, but one in ten responses scored below 65%, reflecting inaccurate or actively harmful advice that undermines their use as trustworthy security guidance.

title: DoHFuse: A Dual-Branch Architecture with DMAGLSTM for Website Fingerprinting over DNS over HTTPS/3
blurb: Researchers released DoHFuse, a dual-branch website-fingerprinting attack that pairs inter-arrival timing sequences with an improved DMAG-LSTM to classify DNS over HTTP/3 traffic, hitting 88.05% accuracy across 449 sites in a closed-world setting and 0.975 AUPRC in open-world detection. The work shows that DoH/3's padding mechanisms alone do not defeat traffic-analysis attacks, leaving encrypted DNS in IoT and edge networks open to user-activity inference despite obfuscation.

title: A Conditional Timing Protection Level: Holdover-Limited Undetected Time Error Under GNSS Spoofing
blurb: This paper shows GNSS timing receivers can be spoofed into a slow, common-mode clock pull their own accuracy flags miss: a recorded JammerTest 2024 attack dragged a u-blox ZED-F9P by 1.01 ms while it reported at most 51 ns, a ~20,000x gap. The authors prove no finite bound exists under a single self-referential clock monitor, then offer a Timing Protection Level using an independent cross-satellite consistency check, calibrated at 114 ns to 458 ns. The open-source bound is calibrated, not yet field-validated.

title: SocGholish Takedown Highlights Malicious TDS Threats
blurb: Operation Endgame seized 106 servers and remediated 14,971 mostly-WordPress sites tied to SocGholish, the multistage JavaScript framework that poses as fake browser updates and brokers initial access for ransomware crews including Evil Corp. The action spotlights traffic distribution systems, which silently redirect web users into the infection chain; Infoblox found SocGholish domain queries across nearly every vertical over the past five months, concentrated in government, education, banking, and healthcare.

title: Burnyard: Future of Malware Analysis
blurb: Burnyard is a lightweight binary emulation platform for malware analysis that captures runtime behavior as structured CSV event traces, avoiding the full virtual-machine sandboxing used in large-scale data centers. The authors argue it sidesteps two sandbox drawbacks: the resource and time cost of executing samples in isolated VMs, and the risk of leaking those samples to public platforms like VirusTotal and MalwareBazaar.

title: Exabeam launches Praxen, an open-source tool to verify AI agent behavior
blurb: Exabeam released Praxen, an open-source (Apache 2.0) tool that performs Agent Behavior Verification, checking an AI agent's actual tools, configs, memory, and integrations against a policy contract before deployment. Built as a coding-agent skill, it reports where an agent's real capabilities diverge from its assigned role, recommends fixes, and assigns a security-posture maturity score.

title: PixJail: Self-Evolving Paper-to-Pipeline Reproduction for Text-to-Image Jailbreak Evaluation
blurb: PixJail is a self-evolving agent framework that reproduces text-to-image jailbreak attacks from a source paper and optional reference code, automatically building a paper-specific attack module and runnable evaluation pipeline under a unified contract. Across eleven representative T2I jailbreak methods, including code-unavailable ones, it recovers the original results with 2.1% average error and 0% median, backed by a memory bank of attack patterns and reusable templates.

title: Algerian Man Extradited to US for Running Cybercrime Marketplaces
blurb: Algerian national Abdellah Belmili was extradited from Spain to the US, where he faces up to 30 years for running the Market0Day and Spoxy cybercrime marketplaces, which sold phishing kits targeting JPMorgan Chase, Bank of America, and other financial institutions. The DOJ ties roughly $900,000 and 5,600 victims to the scheme, underscoring that the US continues to prosecute marketplace operators years after the activity.

title: Algerian man charged with running two cybercrime marketplaces
blurb: Prosecutors charged Abdellah Belmili, a 26-year-old Algerian known online as "SPOX," with conspiracy to commit bank fraud after extraditing him from Spain for running the cybercrime marketplaces market0day.com and spoxy.us. The Bitcoin-only sites sold financial credentials, phishing kits, and compromised email server access, allegedly defrauding thousands of victims and moving roughly $900,000 over three years.

title: Intel agencies: Frontier AI models will reshape cybersecurity faster than expected
blurb: The Five Eyes intelligence alliance warned in a joint statement that frontier AI models capable of transforming offensive and defensive cyber operations are "months away" from broad public availability, citing models like Anthropic's Fable 5 and OpenAI's Daybreak. The agencies, with signatures from the NSA and acting CISA leadership, flagged legacy systems, slow patching cycles, and weak identity controls as the weaknesses AI will excel at exploiting.

title: Disguised Russian banking apps topped US App Store again
blurb: A second sanctioned Russian banking app, a disguised T-Bank (formerly Tinkoff) client posing as an event-MC workspace called Toastmas, climbed to the number two spot on the US iPhone App Store before Apple removed it. It follows an identical app masquerading as a Pomodoro timer that hit the top three on June 5, suggesting sanctioned entities are repeatedly using stealth listings to reach US users.

title: Klue says hackers stole credential from 2022 that led to customer data breaches
blurb: Klue confirmed that a 2022 OAuth credential from a limited pilot, never decommissioned, was used by the Icarus group earlier this month to access customer data stored across third-party clouds and databases. The breach hit LastPass and several other cybersecurity firms, with Icarus threatening to leak the stolen data unless ransoms are paid; Klue detected the intrusion on June 12 and is reviewing its credential and vendor-access controls.

title: MSG Made Dossier on Activists Who Opposed Facial Recognition
blurb: Madison Square Garden compiled a dossier on activists who publicly criticized its facial recognition system, logging their tweets and comments into a document accessible to other employees. The file surfaced in a 45GB cache that hackers stole from MSG and posted online this month, exposing both the venue's biometric surveillance program and its tracking of named critics, including an EFF privacy litigator.

title: Free VPN And Streaming Apps Are Polluting Business Networks With Residential Proxy Traffic
blurb: Infoblox Threat Intel found that 65% of its Threat Defense Cloud customers made DNS queries to residential-proxy domains in 2026, totaling over 500 billion queries per month. Free VPN, streaming, and productivity apps often enroll users' devices as exit nodes via buried EULA clauses, lending home routers, phones, and IoT gear to fraud, scraping, and other abuse. Defenders should monitor DNS for proxy-orchestration domains and treat affected hosts as exposed to legal and IP-reputation fallout.

title: LastPass suffers another data breach, but this time your password vault is safe
blurb: LastPass confirmed that customer names, phone numbers, email and physical addresses, and support-ticket contents were exposed through a breach at Klue, a third-party market-research vendor, while its own systems and encrypted password vaults remained untouched. Attackers reached Klue's network via a still-active credential from a 2022 pilot project, also hitting Gong, Jamf, HackerOne, Snyk, and Tanium; treat the exposed data as phishing and social-engineering fuel against affected users.

title: Inside Crypter-as-a-Service: An Ecosystem Analysis of the exploit.in Underground Forum Research Talks
blurb: A new paper characterizes the Crypter-as-a-Service (CraaS) ecosystem on exploit.in, analyzing 491 threads and 2,949 posts from January 2020 to August 2025 to show that crypters are sold as continuously maintained evasion services with daily stub renewal, sustained AV bypass, and trust-based delivery. Social-network analysis finds the market hierarchically organized around a small core of central trust brokers, backed by escrow, guarantors, and reputation systems, and more professionalized than the comparable CraaS market on HackForums.

title: Snyk launches Evo Agentic Development Security to police AI coding agents
blurb: Snyk launched Evo Agentic Development Security (Evo ADS), a layer of its AI security platform that governs autonomous coding agents by vetting the MCP servers, skills, and tools they use, enforcing policy on agent actions at runtime, and scanning AI-generated code as it is written. Snyk telemetry from nearly 9,700 developer environments found over half had MCP servers installed and one in 12 of those carried a high or critical finding.

title: OpenAI's new GPT-5.5-Cyber tops Claude Mythos 5 in vulnerability benchmark
blurb: OpenAI launched GPT-5.5-Cyber for verified defenders, scoring 85.6% on CyberGym versus Claude Mythos 5's 83.8%.

title: MEV Bot Exploited for $15M via Fake-Token Route Attack
blurb: An MEV bot lost $15M after an attacker baited its arbitrage logic into routing through a fake-token pool.

title: Apple and Tesla trade secrets reportedly exposed following a Tata Electronics cyberattack
blurb: The World Leaks ransomware group breached Apple and Tesla manufacturing partner Tata Electronics, exfiltrating over 200,000 files (630GB) including iPhone circuit board specifications, Tesla Model Y and Project Highland component drawings, employee passport copies, and years of event logs. Tata confirmed the incident and says operations are unaffected, but the actor has posted documents bearing Apple and Tesla proprietary footers to the dark web and issued a ransom demand.

title: Cisco's SSE Rollout Shows The Real Work Behind Zero Trust
blurb: Cisco IT engineers detailed their 12-month migration from a hardware-heavy, 12-location VPN model to a unified cloud-delivered Security Service Edge (SSE) fabric built on their earlier Zero Trust Access foundation. They report an 18% drop in help desk cases, near-zero security incident rates, and the retirement of 20+ legacy VPN options. The writeup focuses on the engineering grind of dismantling end-of-life infrastructure and consolidating disparate hardware to cut MTTR and close visibility gaps.

title: Where IT meets OT and railway cybersecurity gets harder
blurb: In a Help Net Security interview, DNV's Jorge Aldegunde describes how IP-based open networks and condition-based maintenance dissolved the air gap around railway OT, turning isolated signalling and SCADA assets into connected, exploitable data producers. His operating rule for monorail control is to triage each vulnerability by exploitability and risk rather than chase a clean patch window trains cannot afford, and to plan for resilience against months-long intrusions rather than perfect prevention.

title: Cryptographic certificates of validity for trustworthy AI
blurb: This paper proposes cryptographic certificates of validity for agentic AI, attaching an independently checkable proof that an agent's action satisfies an agreed formal policy. The approach compiles a correctness or policy predicate into a polynomial-constraint witness-checking problem, then uses a succinct proof system (optionally zero-knowledge) so verifiers trust the proof rather than re-executing the agent. The authors position it between formal source verification and cryptographic authentication, drawing on proof-carrying code and zkVMs, and flag open specification, auditing, and deployment questions.

title: AutoPRAC: Automating Attack Discovery for PRAC-Based Rowhammer Defenses using Model Checkers
blurb: AutoPRAC is the first automated framework to test PRAC-based Rowhammer defenses by modeling DDR5 implementations as bounded state machines and checking safety properties against a worst-case oracle attacker, emitting concrete attack traces when a property fails. Applying it, the authors found a counter-reset flaw in MOAT, a state-of-the-art PRAC defense, that lets up to 34 activations exceed the Rowhammer threshold undetected.

title: Open-source security is posing challenges governments can’t easily solve
blurb: Open-source software is facing a wave of supply-chain compromises that experts attribute to years of underinvestment, with US government efforts reportedly weakened after protections built up following 2021's Log4j flaw were rolled back. Frontier AI compounds the problem: Project Glasswing scanned 1,000+ projects and found 6,202 high- or critical-severity vulnerabilities, but disclosed only 502 to maintainers and saw just 75 patched as of May 22.

title: FFmpeg fixes PixelSmash flaw in widely used video decoder
blurb: FFmpeg patched PixelSmash (CVE-2026-8461), a high-severity heap out-of-bounds write in the MagicYUV decoder that JFrog used to achieve remote code execution on a Jellyfin media server through its normal library-scan pipeline. Any application built on libavcodec is exposed, including Kodi, OBS Studio, Nextcloud, PhotoPrism, and Linux desktop thumbnail generators, so IT teams should prioritize updating FFmpeg across self-hosted media and file-preview infrastructure.

title: FFmpeg PixelSmash Flaw Allows RCE on Video Players, Media Servers, NAS Appliances
blurb: JFrog disclosed PixelSmash (CVE-2026-8461, CVSS 8.8), a heap out-of-bounds write in FFmpeg's MagicYUV decoder that allows unauthenticated remote code execution via a crafted 50KB media file. Because libavcodec underpins desktop players, file-manager thumbnailers, self-hosted media servers, cloud transcoders, and NAS appliances, the flaw exposes a wide enterprise attack surface, including zero-click exploitation when automated library scanners process downloaded files.

title: Eight-Year-Old Samsung KNOX Flaw Exposed Millions of Galaxy Devices to Kernel Attacks
blurb: Samsung patched an eight-year-old high-severity flaw in its KNOX kernel (CVE-2026-20971, CVSS 7.8) that affected nearly every Galaxy device from the S9 to S25, where a race condition between the PROCA and FIVE integrity subsystems opened a use-after-free path to kernel memory corruption. LucidBit Labs showed an untrusted app could trigger it for deeper device control, and Samsung shipped the fix in its January 2026 update.

title: LastPass confirms data breach in Klue supply chain attack
blurb: LastPass confirmed attackers accessed customer data in its Salesforce environment after stealing OAuth tokens in the Klue supply chain breach, exposing names, phone numbers, email and physical addresses, and support case data. The company says products, infrastructure, and customer vaults were unaffected, but the CRM exposure gives the Icarus extortion group material for phishing against LastPass users.

title: Announcing Amazon EC2 G7 instances accelerated by NVIDIA RTX PRO 4500 Blackwell Server Edition GPUs
blurb: AWS released its EC2 G7 instances into general availability, becoming the first major cloud provider to offer NVIDIA RTX PRO 4500 Blackwell Server Edition GPUs paired with custom sixth-gen Intel Xeon processors. The instances deliver up to 4.6x AI inference and 2.1x graphics performance over G6, with 32GB of memory per GPU, 700 Gbps of EFA networking, and up to eight GPUs per node for inference, rendering, and VDI workloads.

title: ShapedPlugin WordPress Pro Plugins Backdoored in Supply Chain Attack
blurb: Attackers compromised ShapedPlugin's build and distribution pipeline, injecting backdoor code into Pro releases of Product Slider Pro for WooCommerce, Real Testimonials Pro, and Smart Post Show Pro delivered through the vendor's licensed update channels, with one flaw rated CVSS 10.0. The malware harvests wp-config.php credentials, admin accounts, SMTP secrets, and recent WooCommerce order data, making this a procurement-level reminder to audit third-party plugin supply chains, not just core platforms.

title: FortiBleed Attackers Turn Firewalls Into Credential Stealers as Heists Persist
blurb: Researchers at SOCRadar detailed FortigateSniffer, a Golang tool that abuses FortiOS's built-in packet-sniffer diagnostic to turn compromised FortiGate firewalls into passive credential collectors, harvesting 110 million credentials across 24 authentication protocols from an estimated 430,000 devices. CISA has urged organizations to patch and rotate credentials as the Russian-linked campaign, active since February, expands to high-value targets including a NATO-aligned defense contractor.

title: Fake AI Agent Skill Passed Security Scans and Reportedly Reached 26,000 Agents
blurb: Security firm AIR built a fake AI agent skill, brand-landingpage, that passed every scanner it tested (Cisco's, NVIDIA's, and skills.sh's) and reached roughly 26,000 agents, some on corporate accounts, by inheriting GitHub stars from a 36,000-star marketplace repo and running an Instagram ad. The scanners cleared a clean package that pointed to an external setup page AIR controlled, then AIR swapped that page to deliver a payload after install.

title: Microsoft fixes AutoGen Studio flaw that enabled code execution
blurb: Microsoft patched a vulnerability chain dubbed AutoJack in AutoGen Studio, the GUI for its open-source multi-agent framework, which could let a malicious webpage trick a developer's browsing agent into executing arbitrary PowerShell or Bash commands on the host. The flaw was fixed before any PyPI release, so only developers who built from the main GitHub branch during a narrow window were exposed.

title: China’s LineShine dethrones El Capitan as the world’s fastest supercomputer
blurb: China’s LineShine debuted atop the 67th TOP500 list at 2.198 exaflops, beating Lawrence Livermore’s El Capitan by more than 20% and ending U.S. leadership for the first time since 2017. The system reached No. 1 with a CPU-only domestic stack built on China’s LingKun platform, LingQi interconnect, and Kylin OS, sidestepping the AMD and Intel accelerators that power the top U.S. systems.

title: AWS debuts Lambda MicroVMs with up to 8 hours runtime
blurb: AWS launched Lambda MicroVMs, extending its serverless platform to support workloads that run up to eight hours, a major jump from the previous 15-minute execution ceiling. The longer runtime and microVM isolation target untrusted code, AI agents, and long-running batch jobs, letting enterprises consolidate workloads that previously required Fargate or EC2 onto Lambda's pay-per-use billing.

title: 29-Year-Old Squid Proxy Bug 'Squidbleed' Can Leak Cleartext HTTP Requests
blurb: Researchers at Calif.io disclosed Squidbleed (CVE-2026-47729), a 29-year-old heap over-read in the Squid web proxy that lets any trusted client leak another user's cleartext HTTP requests, including credentials and session tokens. The flaw is live in Squid's default config and hits shared deployments like schools, offices, and public Wi-Fi; proof-of-concept code is public, though no in-the-wild exploitation has been reported.

title: Cisco Unified CM flaw CVE-2026-20230 now exploited in attacks
blurb: Cisco confirmed that CVE-2026-20230, a high-severity SSRF flaw in Unified Communications Manager and its Session Management Edition, is now under active exploitation, weeks after patching it on June 3. The bug lets an unauthenticated remote attacker write arbitrary files via the WebDialer component and escalate to root, so IT teams running Unified CM should apply the fixed releases immediately rather than wait for CISA KEV listing.

title: New macOS ClickFix attack silently mounts DMGs to push infostealer
blurb: Unit 42 disclosed a new macOS ClickFix campaign that tricks users through a fake CAPTCHA into pasting a Terminal command, which silently downloads and mounts a DMG via hdiutil and auto-launches the Atomic macOS Stealer to harvest browser credentials, Keychain data, and crypto wallets. The technique routes around Gatekeeper prompts that catch manual DMG installs, raising the bar for endpoint controls and user-awareness training on Mac fleets.

title: FortiBleed campaign used custom FortiGate sniffer to steal credentials
blurb: SOCRadar reported that the FortiBleed campaign has compromised FortiGate firewalls since at least February 2026 using "FortigateSniffer," a Golang tool that abuses FortiOS's built-in diagnose sniffer command to harvest RADIUS, NTLM, Kerberos, and LDAP credentials across 24 protocols. The finding contradicts Fortinet's claim that the incident is only a collection of old leaked credentials, indicating an active operation against more than 430,000 internet-exposed devices.

title: White House drastically shortens deadline for dropping quantum-vulnerable crypto
blurb: A new White House executive order requires US government systems classified as "high-value assets" or "high-impact systems" to adopt post-quantum key establishment by December 31, 2030, and quantum-safe digital signatures by 2031, roughly four to five years earlier than the prior 2035 deadline. The compressed timeline follows research lowering the cost estimate for a cryptographically relevant quantum computer, and mirrors recent moves by Google and Cloudflare to pull their own migrations forward to 2029.

title: Cisco Unified CM Flaw Exploited After PoC Reveals File-Write Path to Root
blurb: Attackers are exploiting CVE-2026-20230 (CVSS 8.6), a critical SSRF flaw in Cisco Unified Communications Manager that lets unauthenticated remote attackers write files to the OS and escalate to root, with a public PoC already landing file:// payloads on honeypots. Exploitation requires the WebDialer service, which is off by default; Cisco patched it in versions 14SU6 and 15SU5 and advises disabling WebDialer until admins can apply the fix.

title: Fortinet: Oracle and other goliaths in firewall fallout as GPU 'democratization' blamed for mass hack
blurb: Attackers compromised roughly 74,000 internet-facing FortiGate devices, nearly half of all publicly exposed Fortinet firewalls, harvesting enterprise credentials from organizations including Oracle, Samsung, Siemens, PwC, Accenture, and a NATO defense contractor. Researchers tied the campaign to a rented 45-GPU cluster used to crack and generate password variants at scale, evidence that cheap on-demand GPU access has lowered the bar for credential attacks on enterprise edge infrastructure.

title: AryStinger Malware Infects 4,300 Legacy Routers to Build Reconnaissance Proxy Network
blurb: AryStinger malware has infected at least 4,300 legacy routers, mostly D-Link DIR-850L units running Realtek RTL819X chips, turning them into a distributed reconnaissance and proxy network that scans, fingerprints, and tunnels traffic before an attacker breaks in. A second strain hits QNAP NAS boxes through CVE-2025-11837, a flaw patched in November 2025, so unpatched appliances and end-of-life routers remain the exposed surface IT teams need to retire or segment.

title: BeyondTrust, LastPass Impacted by Klue-Salesforce Incident
blurb: LastPass, BeyondTrust, 8×8, and Pendo are the latest of more than a dozen organizations to confirm data theft from the Klue breach, in which a threat actor called Icarus used a compromised legacy credential to mint OAuth tokens and bulk-exfiltrate connected Salesforce instances. Salesforce and Gong have disabled the Klue integration, and affected firms say the stolen data was limited to CRM business contact and sales records, with no internal systems or product infrastructure touched.

title: FortiBleed Targeted FortiGate Firewalls in 110 Million-Credential Harvesting Operation
blurb: A Russian-speaking access broker has run FortiBleed, a credential-harvesting campaign active since February 2026 that deployed a Golang sniffer called FortigateSniffer on compromised FortiGate firewalls to capture cleartext and hashed authentication traffic, harvesting over 110 million credentials across more than 430,000 devices. The operation concentrates on IT-services providers and SMBs under 200 employees in the US and India, where one breached firewall can open access paths into downstream customer environments.

title: Linux and Secure Boot certificate expiration (2025)
blurb: Microsoft's 2011 key used to sign the shim UEFI bootloader for Secure Boot expires in September, after which it will no longer sign the first-stage loader that Linux distributions rely on to boot under Secure Boot. A replacement key has existed since 2023 but is absent from many systems and may require a vendor firmware update, leaving distributors and IT teams to handle new installs and patch coverage before the deadline.

title: Scattered Spider members plead guilty to hacking Transport for London
blurb: Two members of the Scattered Spider cybercrime group, Thalha Jubair and Owen Flowers, pleaded guilty to the September 2024 breach of Transport for London that cost the agency £29 million ($38.3M) and forced all 28,000 employees to reset passwords in person. The NCA credited TfL's early engagement with law enforcement for the conviction, a pointed message to enterprises weighing whether to disclose intrusions on critical infrastructure.

title: The post-quantum EO is an important milestone. Now it’s time to get to work
blurb: President Trump signed Executive Order 14409 on June 22, 2026, requiring federal agencies to migrate their most sensitive systems to post-quantum encryption by the end of 2030 and post-quantum authentication by the end of 2031, with contractors bound to post-quantum FIPS standards by 2030. The mandate uses federal procurement to force enterprise adoption, the same playbook that drove IPv6 and DNSSEC, and lands as Q-Day timelines accelerate toward 2029.

title: Following user outcry, AMD reinstates memory encryption in consumer CPUs
blurb: AMD reversed course and will restore Transparent Secure Memory Encryption (TSME) to consumer Ryzen chips via firmware, after a recent update silently stripped the cold-boot-attack protection from lower-end processors. The episode is a reminder that platform security features delivered through firmware can be revoked at the vendor's discretion, which complicates endpoint hardening assumptions for IT teams standardized on AMD's consumer-tier hardware.

title: Hackers Exploiting Cisco Unified CM Vulnerability
blurb: Attackers are exploiting CVE-2026-20230, a critical flaw in Cisco's Unified Communications Manager that lets an unauthenticated remote attacker write arbitrary files and escalate to root, with SSD Secure Disclosure now publishing PoC code for remote code execution. Exploitation requires the non-default WebDialer service, but enterprises running on-prem Unified CM should patch immediately, as the bug is not yet in CISA's KEV catalog and Cisco has not confirmed the attacks.

title: Trump sets post-quantum crypto deadlines, launches broader federal quantum initiative
blurb: Trump signed two executive orders setting federal deadlines for migrating to post-quantum cryptography, directing agencies to inventory cryptographic assets and signaling future quantum-resistant requirements for government contractors. The mandate extends beyond agencies to suppliers and critical infrastructure operators, who will face procurement pressure to prove readiness against the "harvest now, decrypt later" threat that already drives adversaries to stockpile encrypted data.

title: Tata Electronics confirms cyberattack as hackers leak data
blurb: Tata Electronics confirmed a cyberattack on parts of its IT infrastructure after the World Leaks extortion group leaked data allegedly including internal Apple component schematics, PCB designs, and SDK files from the iPhone manufacturer. The breach extends World Leaks' run of high-profile data-extortion victims, following confirmed hits on Dell and Nike, and raises supply-chain exposure questions for enterprises whose proprietary designs sit with contract manufacturers.

title: Production-Ready Autonomous Incident Resolution with AWS DevOps Agent (now GA) and Datadog MCP Server
blurb: AWS made its DevOps Agent generally available alongside the now-GA Datadog MCP Server, giving teams a production-ready path to autonomous incident triage and resolution across AWS, multicloud, and on-premises environments. The integration lets AI agents pull Datadog observability data through the Model Context Protocol instead of brittle API calls, positioning AWS and Datadog as the joint stack for automated incident response and cutting on-call resolution from hours to minutes.

title: Chinese supercomputer using local processors heads TOP500 list
blurb: A Chinese supercomputer built on domestically designed processors took the top spot on the latest TOP500 list, displacing US-based systems that rely on Nvidia and AMD silicon. The result signals that export controls have accelerated rather than stalled China's homegrown HPC stack, a shift enterprise architects with cross-border operations should weigh when planning long-term compute sourcing and vendor dependencies.

title: Germany went off the rails as wireless outage saw all trains cancelled
blurb: A nationwide wireless outage knocked out Deutsche Bahn's GSM-R radio network, forcing the operator to cancel rail services across Germany until communications were restored. The incident underscores the operational risk of running safety-critical signaling on a single dedicated wireless layer, a dependency enterprise and public-infrastructure operators carry whenever a core network has no live failover.

title: WhatsApp phishing attack uses fake business docs to hack PCs
blurb: A WhatsApp phishing campaign tracked by Kaspersky is spreading obfuscated VBScript files disguised as financial documents, hijacking compromised accounts to reach victims' contacts across Brazil, India, Mexico, the UK, and seven other countries. The infection chain disables UAC and silently installs ManageEngine Endpoint Central, a legitimate IT management tool, configured to phone attacker-controlled servers and hand over full remote administration of the Windows machine.

title: 21,000 Oracle jobs vanish amid Big Red's big bets on AI
blurb: Oracle's headcount fell from 162,000 to 141,000 over the past year, a cut of roughly 21,000 jobs, even as the company pours billions into datacenter expansion to chase AI workloads. The reshuffle signals Oracle is funding its capex-heavy AI infrastructure bet partly by trimming staff, a trade-off enterprise buyers should weigh when assessing the vendor's support depth and long-term roadmap stability.

title: Security shops among the 'hundreds' of Klue hack victims
blurb: A breach at competitive-intelligence vendor Klue exposed data across hundreds of customer organizations, with cybersecurity firms among the victims. The incident is a reminder that third-party SaaS tools sit inside the enterprise trust boundary, so IT leaders should treat their CI and sales-enablement vendors as in-scope for vendor-risk review and incident-response planning, not as low-priority point solutions.

title: Mythos discovers 'Squidbleed,' a memory leak that's gone undetected since Clinton era
blurb: Security firm Mythos disclosed "Squidbleed," a memory-disclosure flaw in the widely deployed Squid proxy that has sat undetected since the late 1990s. Enterprises running Squid as a forward or caching proxy now face a patch-and-audit cycle across decades-old deployments, since the bug can leak memory contents and the long exposure window complicates any assessment of whether data was already taken.

title: AWS enters the context layer race with a graph that learns from agents, not manual curation
blurb: AWS announced a context intelligence stack for AI agents, centered on AWS Context, a knowledge graph service that infers data relationships automatically and refines itself based on how agents use it, alongside GA of S3 Annotations and a preview of skill assets in Glue Data Catalog. Metadata publishes in open Apache Iceberg format with IAM and Lake Formation permissions inherited per query, positioning AWS against context-layer rivals on existing enterprise controls.

title: Scope of Salesforce Attacks Expands as Icarus Leaks Data
blurb: The extortion group Icarus widened its campaign of Salesforce data thefts, which trace back to a breach at market-intelligence vendor Klue and the abuse of its OAuth tokens to reach customers' Salesforce instances. LastPass, HackerOne, Recorded Future, Jamf, Snyk, Tanium, and Huntress are among the disclosed victims, underscoring the supply-chain risk that third-party OAuth integrations introduce into enterprise SaaS stacks.

title: Cloudflare teams up with big browsers to help websites tell welcome from unwelcome visitors
blurb: Cloudflare partnered with major browser vendors on a standard that lets websites cryptographically verify which automated agents and crawlers are legitimate, separating sanctioned AI bots from scrapers and abusive traffic. For enterprise IT teams running bot management and WAF policies, a browser-backed signing scheme could replace brittle user-agent and IP heuristics with verifiable identity, reshaping how access controls are written across the AI-agent traffic now hitting corporate sites.

title: Boost BigQuery with Python: Managed Python UDFs now generally available
blurb: Google made BigQuery Managed Python UDFs generally available, letting data teams run custom Python with libraries like NumPy, pandas, and scikit-learn directly inside SQL queries on serverless, auto-scaling infrastructure. The functions are now fully supported for production workloads and integrated into BigQuery's billing SKUs, removing the need to maintain separate containers or compute services to run Python helper logic against enterprise data.

title: Anthropic’s Claude Tag is learning your company, one Slack message at a time
blurb: Anthropic introduced Claude Tag in research preview, an always-on Slack teammate for Claude Enterprise and Team customers that accumulates persistent channel memory, works assigned tasks in threads, and can proactively flag items across the organization. Admins scope each Claude identity to specific channels and tools, positioning Anthropic against Microsoft's Copilot Graph, Glean, and the Snowflake and Databricks organizational-knowledge layers that enterprise agents increasingly compete to own.

title: Microsoft and Chevron plan one of the largest gas-powered data center projects in US
blurb: Microsoft and Chevron will develop Project Kilby, a 2.67-gigawatt natural gas plant in West Texas dedicated to a Microsoft-operated AI and cloud data center under a 20-year power purchase agreement. The dedicated buildout signals that Microsoft is prioritizing guaranteed AI compute capacity over its 2030 carbon-neutral pledge, since the plant could emit more than 13 million tons of CO2 annually.

title: Trump executive orders speed up post-quantum migration, boost industry
blurb: Trump signed two executive orders Monday accelerating federal adoption of post-quantum encryption ahead of the current 2035 deadline and directing federal financing toward the domestic quantum computing industry. Civilian agencies that miss the new timeline must justify the delay to OMB, which pulls forward the timeline for vendors selling NIST-vetted quantum-resistant crypto to the government and pressures enterprise stacks to plan their own migrations sooner.

title: AWS unveils sovereign-centric micro serverless virtual machines
blurb: AWS launched Lambda MicroVMs, a Firecracker-backed resource that gives each end user or session an isolated environment for running untrusted or AI-generated code, with up to 16 vCPUs, 32GB of memory, and eight hours of runtime per machine. The offering targets enterprises facing data-sovereignty and AI-security pressure, letting teams run code in regional, lifecycle-controlled sandboxes without managing virtualization infrastructure or standing up a separate vendor.

title: Google Apps Script is now a Google Workspace core service with enterprise-grade data protection
blurb: Google reclassified Apps Script as a Google Workspace core service, extending enterprise-grade data protection, compliance coverage, and technical support to the automation platform that powers custom functions and add-ons across Sheets, Docs, and Slides. Organizations that previously disabled Apps Script over security or compliance concerns can now re-enable it under Google's standard data governance terms, removing a procurement blocker for sanctioned in-house automation.

title: Blinding the Watchmen: Abusing Cloud Logging Services for Defense Evasion and Visibility
blurb: Unit 42 documented attack techniques that target cloud logging services like AWS CloudTrail and Google Cloud Logging, showing how attackers disrupt log flows to evade detection or quietly route copies of audit logs into their own accounts for continuous visibility. The breakdown gives security and cloud-ops teams concrete misconfigurations to harden and specific service-misuse patterns to add to their detection rules.

title: Why American data centers can't plug in
blurb: The bottleneck on America's AI buildout is not power generation but grid interconnection, as a backlogged first-come, first-served queue leaves data centers and the plants meant to power them waiting years to plug in. The median interconnection wait jumped from under 20 months in 2005 to 55 months by 2023, even as projects like OpenAI and SoftBank's $40B Stargate campus draw 1.2 gigawatts each.

title: 오픈AI, 오픈소스 보안 강화 나선다…‘패치 더 플래닛’ 프로젝트 출범
blurb: OpenAI partnered with security firm Trail of Bits to launch Patch the Planet, an AI-driven program that pairs automated vulnerability research with Codex Security and human review to find and fix flaws in widely used open-source projects including Python, Go, cURL, and pyca/cryptography. The program has surfaced hundreds of issues and merged dozens of patches so far, targeting the software-supply-chain exposure that Log4Shell and the XZ Utils backdoor exposed across enterprise stacks.

title: Linux Foundation extends DNS to AI agents with new Agent Name Service
blurb: The Linux Foundation announced the Agent Name Service, an open standard that gives AI agents verifiable identities through the existing DNS, letting organizations confirm an agent's owner, permissions, and whether its code or activity log has been tampered with. The project ties identity to DNS rather than a new registry, supports decentralized identifiers and Legal Entity Identifiers, and launches with backing from GoDaddy, Cloudflare, Cisco, and Salesforce.

title: Dell launches AI server based on Nvidia Vera Rubin GPUs
blurb: Dell launched the PowerEdge XE8812, a liquid-cooled AI server built on Nvidia's Vera Rubin NVL4 architecture that scales to 144 GPUs per rack and anchors the Dell AI Factory with Nvidia preconfigured stack of compute, PowerScale and PowerStore storage, networking, and Nvidia AI Enterprise software. The system adds 50% more memory per socket and GPU versus the prior GB200 generation, letting enterprises run larger models and simulations entirely in-memory without staging or swapping.

title: Arm servers capture over 45% of data center market revenue — GPU clusters and high-end AI infrastructure fuel a tectonic shift away from x86
blurb: Arm-based servers now command over 45% of data center revenue and accelerated GPU/ASIC/FPGA systems drove more than 70% of a record $122.6B in Q1 2026 server sales, per IDC, with Dell's revenue surging 244% to $20.3B on AI demand. Enterprise buyers standardizing on x86 should plan for an architecture split, as sovereign AI and branded-vendor deployments increasingly steer infrastructure procurement toward Arm and accelerator-heavy systems.

title: Introducing the Cloudflare One stack: agent-powered deployment
blurb: Cloudflare released the Cloudflare One stack, a set of agent skills that configure, deploy, and manage a customer's Zero Trust environment and map an existing security suite into Cloudflare One. The toolkit packages Cloudflare's SASE migration expertise into prescriptive guidance any agent can execute, reducing the manual work of decoding network topology and policy intent during a Zero Trust rollout.

title: Windows 11 KB5095093 update rolls out new Point-in-Time restore feature
blurb: Microsoft began rolling out Point-in-Time Restore in the optional KB5095093 preview update for Windows 11 24H2 and 25H2, letting users roll back the full system state to a VSS-captured restore point from the past 72 hours in minutes. Enterprise licenses can configure snapshot intervals at 4, 6, 12, 16, and 24 hours with matching retention, giving IT a built-in recovery path that cuts dependence on third-party backup and imaging tools.

title: The memory crisis is getting so bad that even retro RAM prices are going to the Moon
blurb: Memory prices are climbing across the board as AI infrastructure demand soaks up DRAM and NAND supply, with the squeeze now reaching even legacy and retro RAM formats that normally sit outside the data center supply chain. Enterprise buyers face higher costs and longer lead times on server memory and storage refreshes, pushing IT teams to lock in procurement early or stretch existing hardware further into 2026.

title: Trump Order Sets 2030 Deadline for Federal Post-Quantum Crypto Migration
blurb: Trump signed an executive order on June 22 requiring federal agencies to migrate high-value assets to NIST's post-quantum standards (ML-KEM, ML-DSA, SLH-DSA) by December 31, 2030, with digital signatures following in 2031, pulling the prior 2035 target forward by four to five years. The order extends to "covered contractors" via a proposed FAR rule and mandates cryptographic bills of materials, putting procurement and inventory pressure on vendors selling into federal systems.

title: Healthtech firm Xolis suffers data breach impacting 1.4 million people
blurb: Xsolis, a healthcare AI vendor whose Dragonfly platform serves more than 600 hospitals and insurers, disclosed that a January phishing attack exposed Social Security numbers, health insurance details, and medical treatment data for nearly 1.4 million people. The breach reached sensitive records inside a system that payers and providers rely on for utilization management and reimbursement decisions, raising vendor-risk scrutiny for any health organization running Dragonfly in its stack.

title: Apple's A12 and A13 Chips Facing New Unpatchable Exploit
blurb: Researchers published usbliter8, an unpatchable BootROM exploit affecting Apple's A12 and A13 chips, covering iPhone XS through iPhone 11.

title: Microsoft says Windows 11 26H2 is coming soon, details upgrade process
blurb: Microsoft confirmed Windows 11 26H2 as its next annual feature update and detailed a shared servicing model that lets devices on 24H2 and 25H2 upgrade through a 174 KB enablement package and a restart, versus a 6.5 GB full install. Systems on 23H2 and older sit on a different servicing branch and need the full upgrade, with no changes to hardware requirements and general availability expected in the fall.

title: You have got to be KDDI-ng – Japanese telco exposes 14.2 million managed email credentials
blurb: KDDI, Japan's second-largest telco, exposed 14.2 million email credentials from its managed hosting service after a misconfiguration left customer authentication data publicly accessible. The breach hits enterprises that outsourced email infrastructure to KDDI's managed offering, forcing credential rotation across thousands of business mailboxes and renewing scrutiny of single-vendor dependency for hosted communications.

title: Join Google Meet calls from Safari on iOS devices
blurb: Google Workspace added support for joining Google Meet calls directly from Safari on iOS, automatically redirecting users who lack the Gmail or Meet app to a browser-based session. The change removes an app-install requirement for ad-hoc participants and rolls out to all Workspace customers and personal accounts starting June 23, with no admin control to configure.

title: Texas lassoes massive Microsoft datacenter - and 20 years of gas turbine emissions
blurb: Texas regulators approved a large Microsoft datacenter backed by on-site gas turbines cleared to emit for the next 20 years, sidestepping grid constraints by generating power directly. The deal underscores how AI infrastructure demand is pushing hyperscalers toward dedicated fossil-fuel generation, complicating the carbon-neutral commitments enterprises increasingly weigh when selecting a cloud provider.

title: AWS Continuum offers devs help with securing code
blurb: AWS launched Continuum, a service that continuously discovers, investigates, and remediates code vulnerabilities across first-party and third-party software, with an enforce mode that autonomously fixes lapses once it learns an environment's guardrails. The service pulls penetration testing and code scanning from AWS's existing Security Agent and adds threat modeling that generates STRIDE-format models from source or design docs, positioning AWS to keep security triage pace with agentic development.

title: Verifiable, private AI: Google Cloud expands Confidential Computing frontiers
blurb: Google Cloud launched Confidential G4 VMs and Confidential GKE Nodes in preview, pairing NVIDIA RTX PRO 6000 Blackwell GPUs with 5th Gen AMD EPYC Turin CPUs to run AI inference and fine-tuning inside hardware-based Trusted Execution Environments. The setup encrypts data in use across the CPU-GPU path and is offered in every region where standard G4 runs, giving regulated buyers confidential GPU compute without sacrificing scale or consumption flexibility.

title: Log Analytics is now Observability Analytics: Query logs and traces with SQL
blurb: Google Cloud renamed Log Analytics to Observability Analytics and made trace data and a management Observability API generally available, letting teams query and JOIN logs and distributed traces with SQL in one place without moving the data. The unified telemetry layer, backed by BigQuery, lets SREs correlate observability data with business metrics and trace agentic workloads, putting Google in closer competition with Datadog and Splunk on enterprise observability.

title: Dragos Unveils AI for OT Security
blurb: Dragos released EmberAI, an OT-specific security assistant built on its decade-long Intelligence Fabric dataset that lets analysts query threats, assets, and vulnerabilities in plain language while keeping customer data inside their own deployment. The launch follows Accenture's $4.1B move to take a majority stake in Dragos and fully acquire runZero and NetRise, signaling a deeper push into operational-technology security for enterprise buyers.

title: Court rules SAVE database illegal, orders it dismantled
blurb: A federal court ruled that the Trump administration's SAVE national voter database violates the Privacy Act, Social Security Act, and Administrative Procedure Act, and ordered it dismantled after agencies "haphazardly" combined citizenship data from multiple government systems they knew was unreliable. The ruling, brought by the League of Women Voters and EPIC, underscores the legal exposure agencies face when repurposing personal data across databases without congressional authorization or required notice-and-comment procedures.

title: Tata cyberattack allegedly exposes confidential Apple documents
blurb: Tata Electronics confirmed a cyberattack in which the group World Leaks claims to have stolen over 630GB of data, including component design and quality-inspection documents tied to customers Apple and Tesla, plus employee passport copies. The breach lands on a key Apple manufacturing partner and underscores third-party supply-chain exposure for enterprises whose proprietary specs and IP sit on a contractor's systems beyond their direct security control.

title: Nvidia unveils Vera Rubin platform targeting AI, HPC infrastructure
blurb: Nvidia launched the Vera Rubin platform at ISC High Performance 2026, a rack-scale system pairing Vera CPUs and Rubin GPUs over NVLink-C2C with up to 144 GPUs, claiming over seven exaflops of AI performance and five petaflops of FP64 compute per rack. The 2.8x memory-bandwidth gain over Blackwell targets HPC buyers running climate, fluid-dynamics, and quantum-chemistry workloads who now consolidate simulation and AI training on one liquid-cooled infrastructure.

title: Cloudflare forms pact with browser giants to protect your privacy with tokens
blurb: Cloudflare is partnering with Google, Microsoft, and Mozilla on a new web standard called Private Access Control Tokens (PACT), which lets browsers present anonymous proof of "personhood" to distinguish human users from AI agents without cookies or CAPTCHAs. The standard aims to cut bot-mitigation friction as agentic traffic scales, and positions Cloudflare alongside the major browser vendors to shape how enterprises handle agent identity and abuse prevention.

title: Anthropic launches Claude Tag, replacing its Slack app with a persistent AI teammate that learns, monitors and works autonomously
blurb: Anthropic released Claude Tag, an AI agent that lives inside Slack as a shared multiplayer teammate, replacing its Claude in Slack app and entering beta for Enterprise and Team customers on Claude Opus 4.8. Admins control its tool access, data sources, spending limits, and channels, putting Anthropic in direct competition with Microsoft Copilot and Slack's own AI for the enterprise collaboration layer where work gets assigned.

title: AWS Weekly Roundup: NY Summit recap, Local Zone in Hanoi, Grok 4.3 in Bedrock, price reductions, and more (June 22, 2026)
blurb: At its NYC Summit, AWS introduced a stack of agent-focused launches, headlined by Continuum, an AI-native security service that scans and remediates code vulnerabilities, and a GA release of Bedrock AgentCore for moving agents into production. The weekly roundup also added Grok 4.3 to Bedrock, opened a Local Zone in Hanoi for Vietnamese data-residency needs, and posted unspecified price reductions across services.

title: Password manager maker LastPass says hackers stole customer support case data during Klue breach
blurb: LastPass said attackers stole customer names, contact details, and support case records through a breach at its market research partner Klue, the same third-party incident that also hit HackerOne, Recorded Future, and Tanium. The company's own infrastructure and encrypted password vaults were unaffected, but support tickets have historically contained credentials and identity documents, leaving the 33-million-user base exposed to follow-on phishing.

title: Okta expands Cross App Access ecosystem to secure AI agent connections
blurb: Okta said more than 25 software makers, including Atlassian, Slack, Zoom, Cursor, and Anthropic's Claude, have signed onto its Cross App Access framework, which routes AI agent connections to enterprise apps through a company's existing identity controls. Built as an OAuth extension and now the official authorization extension for the Model Context Protocol, XAA replaces static API keys and unseen consent screens with identity policy that follows an agent across applications.

title: Claude can now join your Slack channels and work alongside your team
blurb: Anthropic launched Claude Tag, which adds Claude as a persistent, shared participant inside Slack channels that teammates invoke with @Claude to analyze data, write reports, review code, or investigate incidents. The feature requires a Claude Enterprise or Team subscription, and admins control which tools, datasets, and systems each channel-level instance can access, with per-channel spending limits and monthly usage budgets.

title: Using Visual Studio Code’s ‘air-gapped’ AI model mode
blurb: Visual Studio Code 1.122 added a "Bring Your Own Key without GitHub sign-in" mode that runs chat, tools, and MCP servers against locally hosted models like Ollama, enabling fully offline workflows in air-gapped or restricted environments. The gap remains inline and next-edit suggestions, which still require GitHub Copilot, so IT teams standardizing on local LLMs can cut cloud dependency for chat but not yet for autocomplete.

title: Oracle laid off 21,000 employees over the past year, citing AI as one of the reasons
blurb: Oracle cut its workforce by 21,000 over the past year, dropping to roughly 141,000 employees, and named AI adoption among the causes alongside $1.8B in restructuring costs to free up cash for data center buildouts. The reductions fund commitments like the 4.5GW of US capacity Oracle is supplying OpenAI, and the filing warns that further AI-driven headcount cuts may follow.

title: Oracle’s 21,000 layoffs help drive its debt-fueled AI investments
blurb: Oracle cut 21,000 jobs over the past fiscal year, dropping to 141,000 employees, and tied the reductions partly to AI adoption across its operations in an SEC filing. The cuts coincide with a debt-fueled buildout of Oracle Cloud Infrastructure for customers like OpenAI, xAI, and Meta, funded by a planned $45B to $50B raise that pushes total debt past $120B and has already drawn a bondholder lawsuit.

title: Nvidia tops Ethernet data center switch charts by revenue as sales soar
blurb: Nvidia became the top data center Ethernet switch vendor by revenue in Q1 2026, with sales up 192.7% year over year to $2.1B and a 21.5% share, on demand for its Spectrum-X platform from hyperscalers building AI clusters. The win positions Nvidia's integrated GPU-plus-networking stack against incumbents Cisco, Broadcom, and Arista, who now face a buyer increasingly able to bundle compute and interconnect in a single procurement.

title: Enhanced security monitoring with expanded Admin password reset alerts
blurb: Google Workspace expanded its admin alerting to cover all admin password resets, replacing the narrower "Super Admin password reset" rule with a broader detection that is on by default for every Workspace customer. Existing recipient-list customizations carry over automatically and the rollout began June 22 with no end-user impact, so admins get wider account-takeover monitoring without any procurement or configuration work.

title: Oracle shed about 20k roles globally in the last year
blurb: Oracle cut about 21,000 roles over the past year, roughly 13% of its workforce, dropping headcount to 141,000 as it cites AI deployment across operations and reshapes its development group around cloud and AI. The restructuring drove $1.8B in severance and related costs, up from $374M the prior year, even as Oracle commits at least $50B to AI data center buildout for customers like OpenAI and Meta.

title: LastPass notifies users of yet another data breach
blurb: LastPass is notifying users of a breach at third-party market research vendor Klue, which exposed CRM data including customer names, phone numbers, email addresses, and support case records, though encrypted password vaults were not affected. The incident, which traces to Klue's integration with Salesforce and Gong, marks LastPass's third major exposure since 2015 and underscores the third-party-vendor risk that procurement teams now have to vet across their SaaS supply chain.

title: Nvidia announces liquid cooling system that runs ‘hotter than a hot tub’ — promises to reduce electricity consumption and cut water use by up to 100%, but sustainability challenges remain
blurb: Nvidia announced a closed-loop liquid cooling system for its Rubin chips that circulates 113°F coolant, letting data centers shed heat through outdoor dry coolers instead of chillers and cutting water consumption by up to 100% over the facility's life. The higher operating temperature lets most sites skip chillers that account for nearly 40% of data-center power draw, though sites in hot climates or with temperature swings above 113°F will still need them part of the year.

title: Starlink to power security from space with WEI
blurb: WEI partnered with SpaceX's Starlink on WEI Connect, an offering that validates satellite connectivity with SD-WAN and SASE platforms from Cisco, Palo Alto Networks, HPE Aruba, Fortinet, and Cato Networks for fixed and mobile sites. The bundle gives enterprises preconfigured terminals plus a Starlink API dashboard for real-time telemetry, positioning WEI as one of the few channel vendors offering satellite-delivered secure access alongside Vodafone Business.

title: Tesla plans to sell modular AI data center hardware called ‘Megapod'
blurb: Tesla filed an intent-to-use trademark for "Megapod," a modular, self-contained AI data center system bundling servers, networking, power distribution, and cooling, plus management software, less than a year after it killed its Dojo training computer. The product would compete directly with Nvidia's GB200 NVL72 and DGX SuperPOD, the rack-scale reference designs that Dell and Supermicro already build on, putting Tesla in a market Nvidia's chips effectively underpin.

title: Ubuntu Livepatch arrives on Arm64 to eliminate system reboots for kernel updates
blurb: Canonical extended its Livepatch service to Arm64, letting Ubuntu Core 26 and Ubuntu 26.04 LTS systems apply kernel updates without rebooting. The capability requires an Ubuntu Pro subscription, free for home users, and gives organizations a path toward Cyber Resilience Act compliance by keeping security patches current on machines that can't tolerate downtime.

title: Threat Brief: Mitigating Large-Scale Credential Attacks
blurb: Unit 42 details a large-scale password spraying campaign ("FortiBleed") harvesting credentials from internet-exposed Fortinet, Sophos, and MSSQL devices.

title: Cisco Acquires WideField To Secure AI Agents And Non-Human Identities
blurb: Cisco acquired WideField Security, a 2023-founded identity startup that raised an $11.3M Series A, and will fold it into Splunk to extend its agentic AI SOC across human, non-human, and AI-agent identities. The deal is Cisco's third recent security buy after Astrix and Galileo, tightening its bet that enterprises will standardize agent and machine-identity protection on the Splunk and Cisco Identity Intelligence stack.

title: O2 joins UK 2G switch-off with summer 2029 start date
blurb: O2 set a summer 2029 start date to retire its 2G network, joining the other UK carriers winding down the decades-old standard. Enterprises still running 2G-dependent estates, including IoT sensors, fleet telematics, payment terminals, and building alarms, now have a fixed deadline to budget migrations to 4G or NB-IoT before connectivity goes dark.

title: An update on FortiBleed — what's happening with victim orgs
blurb: Security researcher Kevin Beaumont reported that the FortiBleed campaign against Fortinet FortiGate firewalls has exposed roughly 75,000 devices, with about 1,000 organizations confirmed internally compromised after attackers exported full device configurations and cracked the password hashes offline using 36 rented enterprise GPUs. Telcos and MSPs managing customer connectivity are the hardest hit, and Beaumont found evidence of lateral movement into Active Directory, raising the risk of follow-on ransomware and extortion.

title: Algerian man charged with running two cybercrime marketplaces
blurb: Federal prosecutors charged Abdellah Belmili, a 26-year-old Algerian extradited from Spain, with conspiracy to commit bank fraud for running two cybercrime marketplaces, market0day.com and spoxy.us, that sold phishing kits, stolen financial credentials, and compromised email server access. The sites moved roughly $900,000 in Bitcoin over three years and supplied tools that spoofed JPMorgan Chase login pages to defraud thousands of victims.

title: ‘The claims in the suit are false’: Workday hits back amid lawsuit claiming AI recruitment discrimination
blurb: A California federal judge cleared a class action against Workday to proceed, ruling that its AI candidate-screening tools must face claims of age, race, and disability discrimination, including allegations that the software filters applicants using proxy indicators like employment gaps. Workday says the claims are false and that its tools only assess qualifications under human oversight, but the case is the first to test enterprise AI-hiring vendors' liability for customer screening decisions.

title: Anthropic reimagines Claude in Slack as nosy, always-on agentic AI coworker
blurb: Anthropic rebuilt Claude inside Slack as an always-on agentic coworker that reads channel activity, surfaces unprompted suggestions, and acts across connected enterprise tools. The move pushes Anthropic deeper into the collaboration layer Microsoft owns with Copilot in Teams, and forces IT leaders to weigh the governance and data-access tradeoffs of an agent monitoring workspace conversations by default.

title: Microsoft’s next big thing for the cloud: an agent that keeps its cool when everything falls apart
blurb: Microsoft made its Azure Copilot Observability Agent generally available, an incident-investigation agent that correlates logs, metrics, and traces across a customer's stack to point engineers toward likely root causes, and added a preview "autonomous operations" mode that triages alerts without a prompt but stops short of taking action. Priced on usage rather than per-seat, it enters a crowded field against Datadog's Bits AI SRE, AWS's DevOps Agent, and incumbents like Dynatrace and Splunk.

title: A US spectrum auction just raised $3.5B to pay for ripping out Huawei
blurb: The FCC raised about $3.5B in its AWS-3 mid-band spectrum auction, with up to $3.3B earmarked to repay a Treasury loan funding the "rip and replace" program that reimburses carriers for stripping Huawei and ZTE gear from US networks. The auction closes a roughly $3B shortfall left after Congress's 2021 law funded only $1.9B against nearly $5B in carrier reimbursement requests.

title: “직원 데이터는 모으고 보호는 못했다”…메타 AI 실험에 경고음
blurb: Meta paused its Model Compatibility Initiative, a program that captured employee keystrokes, mouse movements, screen contents, prompts, chat logs, and HR and performance data to train AI on human computer use, after unauthorized staff accessed the sensitive collection twice despite a claimed fix. Analysts faulted the access controls rather than the surveillance itself, noting the data was treated as low-risk because it fell outside strict PII definitions.

title: AI isn’t solving cybersecurity workforce woes
blurb: A new report found that more than half of cybersecurity professionals are considering leaving the industry, undercutting the assumption that AI tooling can offset the security talent shortage. For IT leaders, the data signals that automation alone won't close staffing gaps, leaving retention, workload, and burnout as procurement-adjacent problems that no security platform purchase can fully address.

title: Anthropic launches Claude Tag on Team and Enterprise plans
blurb: Anthropic launched Claude Tag, a Slack-based agent on Claude Enterprise and Team plans that teams summon with @claude to read thread context, run connected tools, and carry work across days, replacing the older Claude in Slack app on August 3, 2026. Claude Tag runs under an agent identity with its own service accounts, giving admins channel-scoped permissions, audit logs, and per-channel spend controls, with channel work billed to the organization rather than individual users.

title: The new database world according to Google: Inexact queries and AI in everything
blurb: Google laid out a database strategy built on inexact, semantic queries and AI embedded across its data platforms, with a product exec predicting that humans will no longer directly operate data platforms within three to five years. The pitch points enterprise buyers toward agent-driven querying over BigQuery and AlloyDB, reframing the database from a tool analysts run to infrastructure that AI agents interrogate on their behalf.

title: EDB converges analytics on Postgres to support AI agents
blurb: EDB added converged analytics to EDB Postgres AI, keeping Postgres as source of truth via Iceberg to feed AI agents fresh data.

title: Meta pauses mandatory AI training program that tracked employee keystrokes after internal data leak exposed sensitive staff information company-wide — employees express frustration over poor handling of data
blurb: Meta paused its mandatory Model Capability Initiative, an internal AI training program that recorded employee keystrokes, mouse movements, conversations, and performance data, after an internal leak made the collected information broadly accessible company-wide. Meta classified the incident as a SEV 2 and says it found no evidence of improper access, but the backlash highlights the governance risk of training AI models on surveilled employee workflows.

title: Dell and Nvidia deepen AI factory love-in with high-density PowerEdge servers
blurb: Dell launched the PowerEdge XE8812, a direct-liquid-cooled, rack-scale server co-engineered with Nvidia that scales to 144 GPUs per rack using the Vera Rubin NVL4 platform. The system extends Dell and Nvidia's "AI factory" co-engineering beyond PowerRack and Exascale Storage, targeting HPC and sovereign-AI buyers with iDRAC orchestration, OpenManage telemetry, and automated leak detection.

title: Explainer: Why your legacy storage is choking your expensive GPU
blurb: The piece argues that legacy storage architectures starve modern GPU clusters, leaving six- and seven-figure accelerator investments idle while they wait on data that slow file systems can't deliver fast enough. For IT leaders, the implication is that GPU ROI hinges on the storage tier, so AI buildouts increasingly require parallel file systems and NVMe-class throughput rather than the SAN and NAS already in the datacenter.

title: Google Cloud ups telecom game with Nokia AI agent infusion
blurb: Google Cloud and Nokia expanded their partnership with AI agents for autonomous network operations, built on Google's Agent Development Kit and Gemini Enterprise Agent Platform and deployed in Nokia's Assurance Center. Two of six agents are live now, with the rest arriving when the platform launches as a SaaS offering on Google Cloud Marketplace in September 2026, and Google claims the agents cut issue-resolution times by 50% to 80%.

title: Bold move, Cotton: Trump administration tells US techies it expects American quantum computer by 2028
blurb: The Trump administration set a 2028 target for US industry to deliver a working American-built quantum computer, framing the effort as a strategic race against China. The directive signals coming federal funding and procurement priorities around quantum hardware, putting IBM, Google, and IonQ in line for government contracts while raising questions about whether a fault-tolerant machine is achievable on that timeline.

title: Microsoft Access finally breaks free of its 22-inch form limit
blurb: Microsoft Access removed its decades-old 22-inch form-width limit, expanding the design canvas for legacy database apps.

title: IBM Getting Ready to Scale Quantum Computing
blurb: IBM is moving to commercialize quantum computing this year, building the manufacturing, software, and partner infrastructure to turn its quantum program from a research effort into a scalable business. The shift signals IBM's bet that enterprise customers will pay for quantum access through its cloud, positioning it against Google and emerging quantum startups as the technology approaches practical workloads.

title: Snyk launches Evo Agentic Development Security to police AI coding agents
blurb: Snyk launched Evo Agentic Development Security, a layer of its AI security platform that governs the tools, runtime actions, and generated code of autonomous coding agents from inside the agent workflow rather than scanning output afterward. Drawing on telemetry from nearly 9,700 developer environments where over half run MCP servers, Snyk pitches Evo ADS as inventory and policy enforcement for the agent toolchain that conventional code scanners never covered.

title: Microsoft outs Windows 11 KB5095093 with long list of new features
blurb: Microsoft released KB5095093, a June 2026 preview update for Windows 11 24H2 and 25H2, adding point-in-time restore, a calendar-based 35-day update pause, a quieter Widgets experience, and accessibility additions like screen tint and Magnifier zoom controls. The features roll out gradually, so IT admins should expect staggered availability across fleets and plan validation around the optional C-release rather than the mandatory monthly patch.

title: 파이브 아이즈 “AI 시대엔 사이버 위험도 경영 과제”…기업 리더 역할 확대 주문
blurb: The Five Eyes cybersecurity agencies, led by CISA and the UK's NCSC, urged CISOs and boards to treat AI-driven cyber risk as a core business risk, warning that AI will reshape both offensive and defensive capabilities in months rather than years. Critics including security advisor Joseph Steinberg called the guidance too generic, noting that four of its five recommended actions predate AI and never mention it.

title: Upscale AI readies Skyhammer scale-up networking tech, raises new funding
blurb: Upscale AI raised $190M in a Series A-1 extension led by Premji Invest, bringing total funding to $500M at a $2B valuation, with Nvidia, Salesforce Ventures, and Temasek joining the round. The startup is building Skyhammer, a purpose-built scale-up switch ASIC to link GPUs and XPUs at high speed, positioning itself as a pure-play backend-networking supplier to hyperscalers and neoclouds.

title: Connect to Google Meet hardware with room codes now in Early Preview
blurb: Google Meet added room codes in Early Preview, letting users connect to nearby conference hardware by entering a 5-character alphanumeric code on their personal device as a manual fallback when ultrasound proximity detection is unavailable or disabled. For IT admins, the feature cuts troubleshooting overhead in rooms with difficult acoustics or restricted proximity settings, with codes refreshing every few minutes and requiring a physical tap on the hardware to confirm presence.

title: Accenture snaps up majority stake in Dragos, acquires runZero and NetRise in critical infrastructure security push
blurb: Accenture agreed to acquire a majority stake in OT security firm Dragos plus runZero and NetRise in a roughly $4.175B deal, combining threat detection, exposure assessment, and supply-chain security into a unified critical-infrastructure offering. The move pushes Accenture beyond cybersecurity services into OT software products, targeting a market it estimates will grow from $27B in 2026 to nearly $59B by 2031.

title: How we found a bug in the hyper HTTP library
blurb: Cloudflare engineers spent six weeks tracing why their rearchitected Images binding intermittently returned truncated payloads, 200-status responses that cut a two-megabyte image down to a few hundred kilobytes, only for larger files. The culprit was a race condition in the hyper Rust HTTP library governing how processed image data streamed back to the client. The fix was four lines of code, a reminder that edge-network rollouts surface failure modes invisible under normal load.

title: Announcing Amazon EC2 G7 instances accelerated by NVIDIA RTX PRO 4500 Blackwell Server Edition GPUs
blurb: AWS made general availability for EC2 G7 instances, the first major cloud offering of NVIDIA RTX PRO 4500 Blackwell Server Edition GPUs, delivering up to 4.6x AI inference and 2.1x graphics performance over G6. Each instance scales to 8 GPUs and 256GB of GPU memory, with 700 Gbps of EFA networking (7x G6) and 7.6TB of local NVMe to keep large models and datasets close to compute.

title: AWS debuts Lambda MicroVMs with up to 8 hours runtime
blurb: AWS launched Lambda MicroVMs, extending the serverless runtime from a 15-minute cap to as long as 8 hours per invocation with hardware-isolated execution. The longer ceiling targets untrusted code, AI agents, and batch jobs that previously forced teams onto Fargate or EC2, though sustained 8-hour invocations shift the cost calculus closer to always-on compute than traditional event-driven Lambda.

title: Run isolated sandboxes with full lifecycle control: AWS Lambda introduces MicroVMs
blurb: AWS introduced Lambda MicroVMs, a Firecracker-backed serverless primitive that hands each user or session its own isolated, stateful execution environment with VM-level isolation, near-instant launch and resume, and pause-to-idle pricing. It targets multi-tenant apps that run untrusted user- or AI-generated code, such as coding assistants and analytics platforms, removing the choice between slow-booting VMs and containers that need custom kernel hardening.

title: Building Jaeger’s ClickHouse backend: 8.6× compression on 10 million spans
blurb: Jaeger v2.18.0 ships a native ClickHouse storage backend, giving teams a columnar alternative to Cassandra and Elasticsearch for distributed tracing. The maintainer measured 8.6× compression on 10 million spans, driven by the heavy repetition in trace data: service names, operation names, and status codes that a column-oriented OLAP store deduplicates cheaply. The post walks through the schema design and the trade-offs against the index-heavy operational overhead of the older backends.

title: Making secret scanning more trustworthy: Reducing false positives at scale
blurb: GitHub partnered with Microsoft Security & AI's Agents Offense team to add LLM-based contextual verification to its secret scanning, reasoning about how a detected value appears in code rather than whether it merely matches a secret-like pattern. The goal is to bring AI-detected secrets closer to the precision GitHub already achieves on provider-pattern detections across billions of pushes. Fewer low-value alerts means less triage toil and more developer trust in the system, without sacrificing coverage.

title: GitHub Updates actions/checkout to Block Common Pwn Request Attack Patterns
blurb: GitHub updated actions/checkout to refuse fork pull request code in pull_request_target and workflow_run workflows by default, blocking the most common pwn request pattern that lets attackers steal the privileged GITHUB_TOKEN and secrets. The change ships in v7 effective June 18, 2026, backports to all supported major versions on July 16, and requires authors to set allow-unsafe-pr-checkout to true to opt out.

title: Linux and Secure Boot certificate expiration (2025)
blurb: The Microsoft 2011 key used to sign the shim first-stage UEFI bootloader expires in September, after which Microsoft will stop signing the bootloader that Linux distributions rely on to boot under Secure Boot. A 2023 replacement key exists but is missing from many systems and often requires a vendor firmware update, so the breakage mainly hits new installs on Secure Boot hardware and adds work for distributors and users.

title: AWS unveils sovereign-centric micro serverless virtual machines
blurb: AWS released Lambda MicroVMs, a new Firecracker-backed resource that hands each user or session its own isolated environment with up to 16 vCPUs, 32 GB of memory, and 32 GB of disk for running untrusted or AI-generated code. Idle instances suspend via API or lifecycle policy while preserving memory and disk state, capping costs and targeting the data-sovereignty and isolation demands of multi-tenant AI workloads.

title: Blinding the Watchmen: Abusing Cloud Logging Services for Defense Evasion and Visibility
blurb: Unit 42 researchers detail how attackers target cloud logging services like AWS CloudTrail and Google Cloud Logging to blind defenders, breaking down two attack classes: defense evasion that modifies or disables logging resources to execute unnoticed, and continuous visibility that siphons logs into attacker-controlled accounts. The writeup maps the specific configurations and detections platform teams need to catch logging-service tampering before it erases the audit trail you rely on for incident response.

title: Introducing the Cloudflare One stack: agent-powered deployment
blurb: Cloudflare released the Cloudflare One stack, a set of agent skills that configure, deploy, and manage a Zero Trust environment and map an existing security suite into Cloudflare One. The toolkit packages migration expertise from thousands of customer deployments, giving agents the network-topology and vendor-configuration context they otherwise lack to operate on SASE infrastructure.

title: Google hits 50% IPv6
blurb: Google's measurements crossed 50% IPv6 adoption among its users for the first time, a milestone showing the protocol is mature and deployed at global scale across real-world networks. APNIC Labs pegs worldwide capability lower at 42% because it weights ad-based measurements with World Bank usage data, and its per-economy figures show adoption curves in India, Vietnam, and Saudi Arabia diverging sharply from the global trend.

title: Verifiable, private AI: Google Cloud expands Confidential Computing frontiers
blurb: Google Cloud expanded its Confidential Computing lineup with Confidential G4 VMs and Confidential GKE Nodes, pairing NVIDIA RTX PRO 6000 Blackwell GPUs with 5th-gen AMD EPYC Turin CPUs to keep AI inference and fine-tuning data encrypted inside hardware TEEs, including in transit between CPU and GPU. The preview ships in every region the standard G4 is available, across On Demand, Reservations, DWS Flex Start, and Spot consumption models.

title: Log Analytics is now Observability Analytics: Query logs and traces with SQL
blurb: Google Cloud renamed Log Analytics to Observability Analytics and made trace data and the configuration Observability API generally available, letting teams run SQL across logs and distributed trace spans in one place without moving or duplicating data. SREs can now join application logs to trace spans in a single query, surfacing slow checkout requests or P95 latency on external AI agent tool calls for faster root-cause analysis.

title: AWS Weekly Roundup: NY Summit recap, Local Zone in Hanoi, Grok 4.3 in Bedrock, price reductions, and more (June 22, 2026)
blurb: AWS recapped its NY Summit, where it introduced Continuum, an AI-native security service for the code vulnerability lifecycle, pushed Bedrock AgentCore to GA with a managed orchestration harness and the new Context service, and added release-gating to its DevOps Agent. The roundup also covers a new Local Zone in Hanoi with S3 and EBS local snapshots for data residency, Grok 4.3 landing in Bedrock, and several price reductions.
